
# Writing with Flair
## How to become an exceptional writer

by Join Shani Raja—a former editor for the Wall Street Journal—as he shares practical techniques for making your writing sparkle. Shani dives into the mindset of an elite writer, spelling out the key principles that help great wordsmiths keep their writing purposeful, concise, and pleasurable to read. Learn how to avoid common issues that can weaken your prose, such as careless repetition and out-of-focus ideas. Plus, learn strategies that can help you craft elegant paragraphs and transitions, add freshness to your writing, and more.

**Chapter 1 - THE WORLD OF ELITE WRITING**

**Welcome**

For those of you who don't know me, I've been a journalist for more than 20 years and was for some years, a senior editor at the Wall Street Journal. In my time, I've also written for many other top news organizations including The Economist, Bloomberg News, Dow Jones, and The Financial Times. So I've learned a lot about composing highly polished writing consumed regularly by millions of people. In this course, I'm basically teach you how to become an exceptional writer. The principles will help you to write better, whether you're a consultant of some kind, a marketer, a salesperson, a blogger, an author, a journalist, or even a college student. After learning these principles, you'll become who's writing stands out massively from anyone else in your field. Now how will you achieve this? Well, you're gonna learn how top editors on the most well-known newspapers put magic into their writing using techniques that hardly anyone outside of the world of top journalism knows about. Type of journalists that I've taught writing to as part of my role as a writing coach including, most recently, at the Wall Street Journal. Now, I've spent my years as a journalist trying to come up with a really powerful formula and perspective that would be capable of taking students up the steep learning curve of great writing really quickly. And in this course, you're gonna find that formula. It basically boils down to four really powerful concepts:

·         **Simplicity**

·         **Clarity**

·         **Elegance**

·         **Evocativeness**

Now believe it or not, an understanding of just those four ideas is all you need in order to turn yourself into an exceptional writer. First of, in the simplicity section, you'll discover all the powerful tactics that accomplished editors and writers use to bring conciseness to their writing by removing all unnecessary fluff and complication. After you learn those techniques, you're writing will no longer be slow and heavy, to have the main qualities that contribute to amateurish writing. And after that, we'll look at the second crucial ingredient, clarity, and there I'll give you countless techniques for bringing sharpness, brightness, and focus to your ideas. You'll learn how to approach your sentences with extremely precise thinking about what you want to say so you don't get down any fuzzy or out of focus ideas that may turn your readers off. Then we'll get to the third secret ingredient of the of exceptional writing which is elegance. And you'll discover how order and flow can do wonders for your writing. You'll see how every sort of elegance from the presentation of your prose to its structure and to its rhythm can create a totally different kind of reading experience when you apply this idea of elegance with skill.

And finally, I'll give you a tour of the fourth quality, evocativeness. You'll learn for instance how varying your sentence structure and your words can bring a completely new flavour to your prose. And you'll discover how to make your writing more forceful and potent by turning your sentences into vivid pictures, capable of firing up a reader's imagination and working on their emotions.

But the best part of this second edition is that you won't just learn these ingredients. You'll get to see how to combine them and balance them as an artist would to create true magic in your writing.

So why is having such profound writing skills important? Well, if you're looking to stand out in terms of your communication skills in whatever field you're in, sales, marketing, consultancy, anything else.

You'll gain a clear advantage by knowing how top editors on magazines like The Economist craft their prose. The techniques in this course will empower you to write with a kind of style and flare that's rarely seen in the business world and that will therefore easily be noticed by others or perhaps you have your own business. If so, having these elite writing skills will undoubtedly improve your marketing copy, your sales emails, your letters to clients, your website copy, and much more.

Maybe you want to position yourself as an expert or an authority on a certain subject and that means promoting yourself or your services through things like content marketing. You might want to write articles and post them on things like LinkedIn and Facebook. The image of your company and your own reputation will skyrocket when your ideas are wrapped in highly professional writing that reads as beautifully as an article that you might read in the Wall Street Journal for example.

The aim of this course is to get you to where you can do that effortlessly by using the techniques, I spent two decades of my life perfecting.

Finally, you may be someone who wants to become a professional writer, perhaps a full-time blogger or an author of best-selling books. If so, these advanced writing skills will put you to the top of the pile, helping you to stand out massively from all the noise that's out there on the internet. Because, believe me, strong and sharp writing is a rare commodity on the internet.

Whoever you are and with whatever ambition you've arrived here, I'd like to give you a very warm welcome and hope that I'll get to see you on the other side.

**Tools, not rules**

Now, I don't want you to think of each tactic I give you as a rule that you need to follow all of the time. Think of them as tools, tools that give you a maximum choice over how you communicate through your writing.

**All of these principles must be weighed in the context**, of course, of what you're writing and above all, **they're tools that allow you to write in a conscious way rather than defaulting to old habits that don't serve you well**. Now, most of the time, these tools will serve you brilliantly and you would do well to use them as often as possible, but there will be occasions when you wouldn't for very good reasons. Here's an example. In the simplicity section, I'm gonna give you a tactic like, you know, use plain language to express your ideas rather than official sounding words and phrases.

Now, that's a great tactic. It makes your writing more punchy and more easily comprehensible to others, but does it mean that you should never use official sounding words at all in your writing? Well, no, there may be times when official language is necessary or appropriate. Say you're sending out an invite to a funeral. You wouldn't say, you know, "Martin Braithwaite has died. "Please come over for a sit down and a brief send off." You need to be more diplomatic than that, right? You would probably say something more along the lines of "It is with great sadness we regret to inform you "of the passing away of Martin Braithwaite."

Still, in most contexts, your writing will be more engaging when written in plain language than when it's littered with heavy and formal words.

So, **just don't be dogmatic with these tactics** is all I'm trying to say. Most of the time, every principle will create a certain refinement in your writing, but please don't view them as eternal truths.

Now, another thing to look for is a clash of principles or tactics which can often happen. One tactic to boost elegance, for instance, may clash with another tactic that's used to enhance the clarity of you're writing. In that case, you have to choose between the two conflicting principles. You can weigh them up and decide which one is more important to you in that particular case. In the elegance section, for example, I tell you to avoid repeating the same word in a sentence because that can be inelegant, but you may find that however much you try to eliminate the duplicated word, you can't do so without making your point less clear somehow. So, the principles of elegance and clarity clash in that case, and in such cases, you are justified in sacrificing some elegance for some clarity if clarity is the most important thing to you in that instance, but you won't just be defaulting to unconscious choices that don't serve you best and that will be the difference after you learn these principles.

So, there will occasionally be exceptions and there will be some grey areas with these principles. You must understand, however, that being in this position of weighing principles is preferable to that of the amateur writer who doesn't even know what principles they should be considering.

**Proof the secret sauce works**

My last full-time job as a journalist was as a senior editor for The Wall Street Journal. Now, while working at the newspaper, I began to notice something very interesting. Whenever I'd edit a reporter's article, no matter how much time I spent on the actual edit, I'd end up making exactly four types of changes.

1.      I'd make the writing simpler.

2.      I'd make the writing clearer.

3.      I'd make the writing more elegant.

4.      I'd make it more evocative.

Now, as I thought about it, it suddenly became obvious to me that this was because those are really the essential four ingredients that give writing a feeling of higher quality. I was so impressed with those ingredients that I started referring to them as the secret sauce of exceptional writing.

**Again, simplicity, clarity, elegance, and evocativeness**.

Watch, now, how these four ingredients can transform even the most basic of sentences into something more slick and polished. Take this sentence.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Is that enjoyable to read? Is it simple? Is it clear? Is it elegant? And, is it evocative? I'm sure you'll agree that it's none of those four things, is it? Now, pause this, and try to apply just those four concepts. Simplicity, clarity, elegance, and evocativeness to that sentence to make it better in whatever way you understand. There's no need to post your attempt. All these exercises are just for you to practice with. Now, I had a quick go, myself, at applying those four ingredients, and got this.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Is that better than the original? If so, I hope you can agree that what a powerful formula this can be when applied, even to a simple sentence.

And to do it, I've used only a fraction of the techniques that I'm gonna be teaching in this course. Soon, you'll be able to empower your writing using this secret sauce, yourself. But here's a quick summary of what it will do for your writing.

·         Simplicity will help you create punchiness in your writing.

·         Clarity will ensure your writing is easy to comprehend.

·         Elegance will create orderliness and flow in your writing.

·         Evocativeness will make your writing stimulating.

As you'll see, there really isn't much else you need, other than those four qualities to make your writing exceptional.

**Chapter 2 - MINDSET OF AN ELITE WRITER**

**Mindset principle: Write with integrity**

One of the biggest problems with amateur writers is pretentiousness. It's usually not their fault. They've read so much pompous and pretentious writing in college and maybe school textbooks that they assume that they have to emulate that style in order to sound mature and intelligent. Unfortunately, many people carry that turgid style of writing straight into the workplace, which is why so much business writing ends up being so boring and unreadable. The main problem with such writing is that it lacks authenticity. As a journalist, I used to read press releases, government papers, academic studies, and so on, full of this kind of pretentious writing that was enough to make you feel quite queasy.

As an elite writer, you need to be real first of all. **Always write like you mean what you say. In other words, write with integrity**.

You wouldn't ask your friend, would you, if you could utilize his toilet? You wouldn't ask a member of your family if, per chance, they knew what time it was. So why would you use such pretentious words in an everyday piece of business writing? If you're going for fancy language, just be very clear on what your motivation is. Is it because it serves some kind of an artistic purpose, or it helps your communication in some way? Or is it simply because you think you might look smarter than you actually are? In other words, is it a noble motive, an honourable one? Or is it just plain vanity? Now remember this piece of advice, because it's really important in your journey towards becoming an elite writer.

As an elite writer, **it's never about showing off. Your aim is to communicate brilliantly, not to impress**.

I once got emails from a public relations executive that sounded a bit like this. My client would regard it as eminently gratifying and propitious if you would deem it worthy of your time and resources to acquiesce graciously, in a fitting manner, to a confabulation in an environment considered conducive to both parties. The sentiment is nice, and it's pretty courteous and polite. But it's also so blatantly an effort to impress with vocabulary, that it comes across as immature and childish more than sophisticated. **When you start trying to impress as your primary goal, you risk confusing your message and diluting it so that your overall piece of writing becomes a lot less effective**.

Now vocabulary like that may have a place. I'm certainly not saying dumb your writing down by avoiding big words that carry a real and powerful meaning.

And I'm definitely not saying you shouldn't work to expand your vocabulary, because doing so will undoubtedly help you to express yourself more precisely and more strongly.

But when fancy language is used flagrantly to try to appear sophisticated, it ends up making you seem, well, unsophisticated, really, and even, possibly, insecure. Cut out this kind of over-the-top words, the needlessly official words, the jargon words, and all of that.

It's far better to be straight and say that your client would really love to sit down to a coffee at a convenient time, where your communication comes across as honest and sincere.

By all means, **go to a thesaurus when you feel a word you're using doesn't quite hit the mark** when you're looking for a better one to better express what you're meaning.

But don't use it to just try to make yourself sound smart, especially if the words you've already got communicate very well what you mean.

**Mindset principle: Yearn to connect**

The next important aspect of the elite writer's mindset is to **care deeply for your readers. You should always write with a very strong will to connect with whoever it is that you're writing for**.

You should be very serious about making sure your particular audience is going to really get what you're saying in exactly the way that you want them to. Now, this might sound too obvious to be worth saying, but I know from experience how easy it is to lose touch with your audience and as a result, to use words or expressions that they may not be familiar with or to address them in a tone that is maybe inappropriate.

This used to happen to me personally, actually, at the Wall Street Journal, where I worked for awhile in the paper's Australian offices, and editors in the United States might come back to me with questions about what we meant by certain terms and expressions that we wrongly assumed were universal. For example, in Australia it's common to refer to an idea as being confronting, if it is particularly controversial or difficult to accept. We learned that the phrase confronting wasn't all too common in other parts of the world, leading some editors elsewhere to be confused by it.

Now, little things like that show you the importance of cultivating the utmost sensitivity to your readers. So as I say, when you're writing, you have to have a very strong desire to want to connect and to not alienate your readers in any way.

And **the best way of ensuring you don't alienate them is to read back your own writing from their point of view**. This means that **you have to have some conception, even roughly, of who your readers are, or at least what type of mix of people you're addressing**.

Write for a specific audience.

Sometimes you can't be sure that everyone in the target demographic will understand everything you say in the way that you want them to exactly but at least you should have a generalized intention to connect with a particular type of audience.

The closer you can get to cognizing who they are, the more effective your writing is gonna be. You'll know exactly the types of words and expressions to use that will work best, the type of examples and anecdotes to use, the kind of pace to adopt and so on.

Now, writing with this kind of sensitivity will have a ripple effect right across the piece of writing that you're creating. Let's say you're doing a piece of persuasive writing. Then you want to know the audience you're trying to persuade as intimately as possible, don't you? Their likely values, their interests, the kind of words they're likely to be familiar with, their core assumptions about the world and so on. And you have to target that piece of writing directly to the existing mindset, values, beliefs, perceptions of that particular audience category.

When you have that kind of intimate sense of who you're writing for, it's gonna make your writing more powerful and way better than that of most other people.

**Now, if it's a job application, for example, see the writing from the viewpoint of your potential interviewer, and you'll communicate much more effectively, knowing what to leave in, what to leave out, and how to say it all.**

I've read countless resumes where the person is explaining their current job, which is in a completely different industry to the one that they're applying for, and they use terminology that only someone in their industry would really understand. Now, as a result, the potential employer can't easily figure out even sometimes what they actually do in their job right now.

**So again, write for your reader. I want you to really get this. The amateur writer thinks of themselves and what they want to say. The elite writer yearns to connect with their audience.**

**Mindset principle: Know your intent**

Now, whenever you come across writing that seem unfocused, it's usually because the author isn't entirely clear about their intent, the reason why they're engaging in this particular piece of writing, they mix up intentions and, you know, if you do this it will show in the unfocused style of your writing.

**An elite writer, an exceptional writer remains conscious of the reasons why they're writing and what they're writing, even if the intention changes during the actual process**.

The moment you sit down to write, you have at least an unconscious intent, and it's very useful to make this unconscious intent as conscious as possible before even writing.

**Having this clarity of intent gives your writing focus and direction, helping to ensure that it doesn't get lost or ever become too rambling.**

So, let's take a look at a few different types of intentions that you might have. Notice that there are one of several general reasons why people write, and here are four common ones:

**1.**      In order **to inform people of something**. Let's say you write a blog that covers new consumer technology, for example. And you wanna inform your readers about a cool new smartphone that's just hit the market. Now, knowing your primary intent is informative, you'll likely focus on explaining to your readers what the smartphone can do, what features and functions it has, and what's different about it from rival or earlier models of that phone. You create a certain perspective around that piece of writing that holds it together with the force of that intent, the intent of informing reasons about this new device. Now, let's consider another reason why we write.

**2.**      **To persuade people,** as in the commentary section of a newspaper. And so, the whole perspective of your writing changes changes accordingly, including how you start it, the points you make, and the kind of structure it has, and what examples you use to illustrate things. Your smartphone blog post might, therefore, turn into an opinionated review of the new device, where, above all, you're trying to persuade people either to buy the thing or not buy the thing, unless you're offering a particular take on the smartphone rather than focusing on an overview of its features. That the focus changes, and the narrative will move in a completely different way once you're writing with that conscious intent. Now, in the process of persuading, you'll no doubt be informing your readers, to some extent as well, about the features and the capabilities of the new smartphone. So, **you'll be using some of that information as you did before, but the whole thing comes dressed up differently, the material will tilt itself in a different way, and it will likely take on a different structure, different content,** different tone from the article primarily seeking to inform people. An exceptional writer must seek to have this degree of **conscious control over their material**. It's one of the things that makes your writing raise above the ordinary with that focused, conscious type of approach. We control and direct our ideas with this powerful force of conscious intent, and it will make a huge difference to how your writing ends up looking in the end. Now, you may be doing both a bit of informing and a bit of persuading in either of those pieces. But still, knowing your fundamental or primary intent will help you to place the different points in your article  with care and precision with the appropriate weight in the right places.

**3.**      **To entertain**. Your overriding purpose may be to **create certain kinds of states and emotions in people**, to make them laugh, for instance, or thrilled or fascinated. And carrying that intention with you through the whole piece of writing will keep it focused and guide you to use, for instance, anecdotes and examples to fulfill that primary entertainment need best. In the smartphone example, the facts about the phone become mere fodder to drive the entertainment rather than the focus being on either informing or persuading.                 **Each intention creates its own peculiar narrative structure. Each can include elements of the other, but one will usually be the driving force**. So say your primary intent was to persuade people not to buy the phone. You may still choose to do this persuasion in an entertaining, for instance, in a humorous way. But being conscious of your primary intent will help you provide the right amount of relative weight to the humor and to the persuasion. One will form the narrative skeleton of your piece, the other will give it dimension. Now, persuasive writing is what you often see in editorial writing, in newspapers, as I said, where they're writing to convince you of a particular point of view or to convince you to take a particular stand or stance on a controversial issue. Now usually, this is done in a very serious tone because it's normally about very serious subjects, say, whether or not to go to war. The actual information, the facts of the situation, say, raising hostility between two nations becomes fodder for the persuasion, which remains the primary focus of the article. The facts are usually woven into the persuasion and are chosen because they are relevant to it, which is what you get in good commentary writing. With a fact-based news article, for example, it's the opposite. People's opinions about an issue are usually chosen for their relevance to the informing that the writer wants to do, though, these days, I must admit, there's increasingly a blurring line between news and opinion.

**4.**      Now, a fourth purpose of writing is **to inspire**. So here, you've got things like inspirational speeches, which are written mainly to motivate you to do something, and if that's your primary objective, again, the whole piece will hang together in a different way. Let's say you wanted to inspire strong revolutionary zeal among your readers. You might start in a certain kind of way, perhaps somberly with an attitude of graveness about the situation. You'll create a certain kind of mood, a certain atmosphere through the writing when you write consciously with that intent. You'll build up momentum in a certain kind of way in reaching emotional peaks at times that are capable of inspiring your readers to action, if that's what you want them to do. Once again, the facts of the case will be woven into this ultimate objective of inspiring people in a careful and conscious way.

And so, **decide before you engage in a piece of writing on what your overriding purpose is**.

**Are you writing fundamentally to inform, persuade, entertain, motivate, or something else?**

Each intention skews the way the writing will be done, and each intention carries a power that gets infused into the writing at a very deep level.

**Mindset principle: Aspire for beauty**

The elite writer fundamentally views himself or herself as an artist. They view the page they write on as a blank canvas on which they begin with a sketch, and on which they proceed to paint a beautiful picture.

Now, whether it's a report, an essay, or an article that you're writing, you've got to regard yourself as a sculptor of words, a painter of sentences, a composer of narratives. Now, the mind of an artist is usually directed at creating something of beauty, and that's exactly how I encourage you to approach your writing, with a commitment each time to creating something of beauty, even if you're composing something as basic as a cover letter for a job application.

At the beginning it has no depth, no structure, no style, but as an artist, you have to progressively give it all of those other dimensions.

**Your job is to turn it into a beautiful picture by trimming things down where you've been too, you know, perhaps long-winded, by moving points and ideas around to get the ideas to flow more gracefully, by giving the writing rhythm, by adding shades and textures until it transforms stage by stage into a beautiful picture.**

**The tactics and principles in this course are the tools for creating that beauty.**

If you really want to wow people with your writing, this yearning for beauty must be your fundamental aspiration.

Every comma has to be weighed and deemed to be contributing to the overall beauty of the writing. Every full stop must exist because you've decided that it's more beautiful to end a thought right there instead of connecting it with another in the same sentence. Every capital letter on your page has been evaluated for its contribution to the beauty of the finished piece. Every word you're using has been assessed with the same goal in mind. You have decided that it truly belongs where it is. Every phrase has been scrutinized, every metaphor weighed for its contribution to the lucidity of your message. The structure of every sentence has been considered for its beauty as well, and you have decided that this, not that, is the best way of expressing your idea.

The size and length of each paragraph has been evaluated so it's beautiful to you. You've even given though to the distance between the headings and the writing that falls under it, once again, for its effect and impact on the beauty of your writing.

**Your attention to detail really must go that deep if you want to create exceptional writing.** Is this the best order of words, or is there a way I could say what I want to say more simply, more clearly, more elegantly, or more evocatively?

It's only by looking at your writing in this way that my writing framework is gonna make sense.

**I believe simplicity, clarity, elegance, and evocativeness are the key ingredients that will give you the most leverage to create this beauty** that I'm talking about in your writing. That's because each ingredient has its own aspect of beauty, which I'll explain at the start of each section.

But first of all, your attitude must be right, and to receive this information deeply, you've got to see yourself fundamentally, as I say, as an artist.

As you'll see, what matters is that a certain quality of artistic thought has gone into all of your choices about every aspect of your writing.

Now of course our ideas of beauty might differ slightly. Even in literature, some people love Oscar Wilde's ornate and witty style. A man is very apt to complain of the ingratitude of those who have risen far above him, for example. Others may find this over the top, and even tedious. Some relish Hemingway's simple and direct style. I love to go to the zoo, but not on Sunday. I don't like to see the people making fun of the animals when it should be the other way around. Now, others may find that style childishly simplistic, for example.

**Each writer has a different concept of beauty, but each is trying in their own way to create it.**

Your own concept of beauty may tilt you towards one style or another, say in your business, blog, or book writing, and it's not my place to tell you which of these individual styles to actually prefer.

Instead of prescribing how you should write, I want in this course, to empower you **to choose your own style**, by showing you, really, all the different sorts of things that you can do to make it exceptional in these four ways.

**Mindset principle: Balance the ingredients like an artist**

Now all the different tricks and techniques in this course go towards the beauty showing you how simplicity, clarity, elegance, and evocativeness can contribute to the beauty of your writing. But you must use the ingredients like an artist, not like a technician.

**Using these four ingredients like an artist can sometimes mean considering whether simplicity, for instance, may be compromised for evocativeness, or whether elegance may occasionally have to be sacrificed for the sake of clarity.**

Jane Austen, her writing isn't necessarily the simplest form of writing. We could probably think of many words to cut out to make it simpler. But it is, for many people, evocative, making us laugh out loud at times with its delicious irony for instance.

An elite writer doesn't just throw everything into the pot and hope that it tastes nice. Like a master chef, an artistic writer balances his or her ingredients in a way that achieves the best taste for whatever meal that they're preparing.

Once you've absorbed all the techniques around simplicity, clarity, elegance, and evocativeness, respectively, you will have a huge amount of creative power in your hands, but that doesn't mean you must drop all of the tactics, all of the time into your writing without thinking.

As you come to know all the tactics intimately, and once they become second nature, you'll simply feel tremendously powerful. You'll know what you can do. That doesn't mean you have to do everything all the time.

As an artist, you'll be balancing the ingredients each time, which at times, as we saw, can mean trading off one ingredient for another ingredient. Nevertheless, being aware of all the different ways that you can achieve simplicity, clarity, elegance, and evocativeness, you'll start to feel a huge freedom to create, exactly the tone, texture, and character that is exactly right for your purpose each time.

Remember, these are tools that let you exercise the maximum control over how you say what you want to say. But if after learning all the tactics for boosting simplicity, for instance, you decide consciously to ignore all of them, well, okay, that's your right. I wouldn't advise it, but it's your right.

You may decide, for instance, that you prefer very ornate language over the economical style that I usually advocate for business and blog writing. But you will have decided that after seeing the impact that simplicity can have on your writing and what it can do to enhance and empower your communication.

In fact, even just knowing the difference between ornate and simple writing, will give you a level of conscious mastery of your writing that you maybe never had before.

I can show you how to make your writing punchier through these countless simplicity techniques and I can even impress upon you that simplicity has a distinct aspect of beauty as I'll try to do here, but there's still nothing I can do if you decide as an artist that punchiness means nothing to you.

However, please factor in something else that's very important. The examples that I give you from literature or whatever, from fiction writing, there is a degree of artistic license granted in novels and so on.

For most types of business writing, where the ultimate goal is to communicate efficiently and effectively, that same license isn't usually given.

There are certain conventions that you will have to take into account, if you're smart.

Your boss may conceivably find it entertaining if your reports arrived in the sarcastic style of Oscar Wilde, but it also might be a bit baffling or even irritating.

I encourage you to instead see your objective as creating beauty within the conventions or the context in which you're writing.

**Mostly in a business environment, you'll want to express your ideas with minimum fuss and ceremony, in other words, with the utmost simplicity.** Therefore, as business writer, you'll want to know how to maximize the simplicity of your writing in order to make it super tight and super racy.

In fact, most of the techniques in this course are gonna be highly useful in that kind of business context. I'd advise many of you to use them whenever possible.

Even for things like blogs and books, I'm confident that these techniques will serve you extremely well overall. And unless you already have a finely tuned artist capability, where you know how to break all the rules brilliantly and you're very confident with that, I'd advise you to stick closely with the methods that I'm gonna be teaching you.

In other words, for most purposes, apply as many of these principles here as possible and as often as possible, but do so with a sense of artistry.

**Mindset principle: Don't be obsessed with perfection**

The final thing I want to tell you before we proceed to learn the principles and tactics is this: Relax, okay? Over the length of this course, you're going to be bombarded with skills and tactics that may, to some of you, feel overwhelming at times.

But you'll have to just trust me that the important stuff is going to in and you will naturally find yourself becoming a better and better writer, with each new composition. It's important during this process of receiving that you're not tensed up.

Also, once you're done with the course, don't become obsessed with making everything absolutely perfect all the time, because it can't usually happen.

I've learned, as an editor, that you're not capable of making everything you write absolutely perfect. Even if that were possible, time doesn't allow it, usually. In the real world, we're usually writing to a deadline.

**We have to focus, instead, in making it as perfect as possible in the time available**.

This more relaxed attitude should help you deal with this thing that people sometimes call **writer's block, which usually means either that you don't know why you're writing something or that you're too tensed up about making it perfect that you're afraid to really start**.

I want you to imagine a graph where one axis is time, and the other axis is perfection. Now usually, the more time you spend editing your writing, the more perfect let's say it will become, in your eyes.

But you don't have, usually, all the time in the world, so that's one thing. But nor is the ultimate perfection visible on the graph anywhere, it's just an endless axis.

So, your own idea of what's perfect, as well, may change, you know, different times and on different days. So, **it's foolish to become obsessed with absolute perfection**. It's an obsession that will only block you.

**So, instead, focus on making your writing as polished as it can be in the time available and as perfect as possible, according to your judgment** in that moment.

These tactics are going to help you to do that effectively. They'll give you conscious control, more conscious control than you ever had over your own creative expression, I think. Some of it will sink in immediately.

Other parts you'll slowly begin integrating into your writing. Don't worry. Just keep writing, allowing the creative part of your mind to come up with ideas.

And then use the analytical part of your mind, with these skills and techniques by your side, to refine and polish what you've written in order to make it sparkle.

You see **most ideas come out raw**. Let them come out that way, and **then craft them into as perfect an expression of your thought as you can in the time that you have**, using the techniques that I'll be giving you.

But throughout this intensive process, I want you to see writing for the magical gift that it is. Recall continuously what a blessing it is to have even some time to sculpt and mould your thoughts into beautiful and elegant expressions, rather than as things that you just blurt out.

**Chapter 3 - SIMPLICITY**

**The beauty of simplicity**

**Writing is easier to comprehend and absorb when it has the minimum amount of complexity in it**.

It's usually better, therefore, to **aim for maximum simplicity**.

In this section, I'll show you the numerous tricks and tactics you can use to simplify your writing to the highest degree.

The techniques that many journalists use to create what we call tight writing.

·         **Tight writing is writing basically that has no redundancy**.

·         It's economical.

·         Every word is there because it is necessary.

·         Every sentence is expressed as concisely as possible with minimum extraneous detail so that there's no long-windedness or bloatedness.

·         **There's no needless complexity that makes comprehension difficult**.

·         The heaviness of official language is also stripped out as much as possible, and distractions that arise from unnecessary punctuation, for example like quote marks or hyphens when you don't really need them, are eliminated.

**Now when you go for maximum simplicity like that, your writing generally becomes easier to comprehend and to digest**.

It also gives it the **greatest amount of punchiness and pace**, eliminating that feeling of the writing being slow going and instead creating a feeling of it kind of racing along.

So simplicity has a distinctive beauty to it, which you're now going to discover, including everything that contribute to or get in the way of it.

**Fancy, flowery, and official language**

- One big thing that stands in the way of simplicity in your writing is fancy or flowery language when simple things are stated in a pointlessly fancy way.

Now, by fancy language, I don't mean language that's colorful and evocative, and that helps to get your meaning across in an interesting or stimulating way.

I'm talking about pointlessly fluffy and long-winded ways of saying things that make your writing basically slow and heavy.

To avoid this kind of pointlessly flowery language, say what you wanna say in as simple a way as possible, and then spice it up as necessary if you need to afterwards. Look at this sentence which illustrates fancy writing on the website of the plain English writing campaign.

I think it's called the Plain English Campaign.

**High-quality learning environments are a necessary precondition for the facilitation and enhancement of the ongoing learning process.**

You've probably come across such writing in government reports or in internal communications. **There's the assumption that professional writing needs to sound somehow different, more official than ordinary writing**. And that idea does tremendous damage to people's growth as competent writers.

I'd advise you to **shun such pretentious writing in almost every context**, because it doesn't really help your communication at all, unless of course, you deliberately want to hide something.

With writing like this, it's as if there's something more, a more concrete idea hidden in the weeds of the sentence that's struggling to get out. Now the reason it feels like that is because it's just simply not plain enough.

**Where the meaning doesn't hit you instantly, your communication is really falling short.** If I was confronted with a sentence like this as an editor, I'd straightaway begin looking for what the specific idea is that's yearning to get out of this horrible abstraction. And I'd **look for the plainest way possible of saying it.**

Now, my advice to avoid this kind of pointlessly long-winded language could really have gone into any of the sections of this course, because it's so bad stylistically. Apart from not being simple, it isn't terribly clear either because it's just so abstract. It definitely isn't elegant either. Just look at the sentence. It's ugly as anything, right? And it certainly isn't stimulating to read, because it doesn't create any pictures for the reader. It's just a lot of dry words. So it's not evocative either.

**In other words, this sort of fancy language fails to deliver on all aspects of beauty.**

Now let's say you discover this kind of sentence in your own writing, right? High-quality learning environments are a necessary precondition for facilitation and enhancement of the ongoing learning process.

**Getting to the habit of deconstructing things like that, and then stating them much more plainly**, it will bring a breath of fresh air of simplicity to your writing, because believe it or not, abstract, bureaucratic, and unnecessarily official writing remains a very common practice.

The good news is that **you really can distinguish yourself massively just by focusing on fixing this particular bad tendency.**

So let's ask what might the writer be really trying to say here? High-quality learning environments, let's break it down, right? Now, what exactly does learning environments actually mean? How could we express that in a more plain way? Could the writer be talking about schools? This does seem to be the case here. So let's assume it's schools that the writer is really talking about in this sentence. What would high-quality mean in that context? It seems to me just a fancier way of saying good schools, right?

So the next one, are a necessary precondition, we're just breaking it down further. Is there a clearer way, a more concrete way, of saying that? How about just are needed? If we use that, then so far, we'll have good schools are needed.

Now what's next? For facilitation and enhancement. Can you think of a more simple way of saying that? Well, how about to improve, or to enhance, or to support? The word facilitation actually seems a little bit pointless in that sentence anyway. So I personally think that the word support captures the meaning best of the sentence.

And that gives us this, good schools are needed to support. So you see how we've gone from the abstraction down to something much more straightforward and plain.

What's next? Of the ongoing learning process. Now to me, all that just means, is learning. So then we get good schools are needed to support learning.

Now that we've unmasked the idea to some extent, we can actually begin to evaluate it properly, right? And to be honest, it's a pretty weak idea beneath all that sophistry. It wasn't all that easy to see how weak it was. Good schools are needed to support learning.

Now it might be helpful to mention children, I think, or students somewhere in the sentence, because those are the people actually being talked about as benefiting from education in that sentence. So we could state the whole sentence more plainly like this,

good schools are needed to support children's learning.

Now on the Plain English Campaign's website, the solution offered turns the sentence around a bit to say children need good schools if they're to learn properly.

**Now either way, you can now understand why the original sentence took us so long to comprehend**. Just imagine having to read sentence after sentence laden with this type of bureaucratic language. It really wouldn't be that pleasant of an experience, would it?

**So whenever you come across sentences that aren't plain, immediately start deconstructing them to see, first of all, if there's a substantial point actually being made** in all that bureaucratic language.

And if there is, then go on **to try and simplify it into the plainest language** possible **without making it sound perhaps too colloquial or slangy**.

**Fancy, flowery, and official language: Exercises**

- Deconstruct this sentence in the same way that I showed you before. Pause the video and have a go.

**In the light of the extent, scale, range, and immeasurable complexity of the uncertainties that persist on the economic front, we conclude, having evaluated the country's mid-to-long-term prospects, that multifarious scenarios could yet unfold.**

Now, I get this. This next sentence also needs translation because it's definitely not plain speaking. It's one made up by George Orwell to make a point about the bureaucratic jargon that he was so against.

**Objective considerations of contemporary phenomena compel the conclusion that success or failure in competitive activities exhibits no tendency to be commensurate with innate capacity but that a considerable element of the unpredictable must invariably be taken into account.**

It's very inaccessible, isn't it, and comprehension is very slow, if at all possible. Translate it into as few and familiar words as possible. If you really edit it down as far as possible, you may end up with something looking like this.

**People aren't necessarily born successful.**

You may have had something slightly longer or a bit different; it doesn't matter as long as you get the importance of translating this type of language into something more plain.

Some types of fancy language though can be a bit less obvious, like this.

**Delta Air Lines has implemented a number of new initiatives to boost ticket sales on domestic routes****.**

That could be a lot plainer. Try and do it yourself. As soon as I see words like implemented, initiatives, and a number of, it rings alarm bells for me. It just sounds unnecessarily formal or bureaucratic to my ears, so I might say something like this.

**Delta Airlines is trying to boost ticket sales on domestic routes.**

See how that just clicks more easily? So, begin to ask ruthlessly of every sentence, "**Can I express this even more simply, even more plainly**?" and keep asking that until you feel that you've reached a point where it really can't be expressed any more simply or plainly than it has been.

**Economical ("tight") writing**

In journalism, we use the word tight to describe writing in which points are expressed with maximum efficiency.

Even when you think a sentence is as tight as it can be on reflection, you can often find that even more words can be stripped out to make it even more economical.

When I was teaching myself to become a journalist I'd often admire the way that good writing, say in a well written newspaper like The Economist, would have this kind of **crispness** about it.

**There were no noticeably excess words. Each was placed with the utmost regard for relevance**, and that's how you want to think.

**When there's no more room to tighten, you've probably found the optimally simple expression of an idea**.

There's an example often that I use but it's worth repeating because it gets across well the idea of economy and tightness. I found it first in a book on journalism I think by Harold Evans. Imagine a sign above a fishmonger's that reads

**Fresh Fish Sold Here.**

Consider which of those words could be deleted. Here is redundant because after all, you're right there reading the sign, right? Sold isn't strictly necessary because nobody's likely to assume the fish are being given away, right? Fresh probably isn't required either, because the opposite would be stale fish and no one would advertise that. And finally, if you're standing close enough to the sign, you could probably smell the fish anyway.

Now, that's a humorous exercise not to be taken too literally, of course. For marketing purposes, you probably need a sign, but it just goes to show how much can be cut when you think deeply about every single word.

**Economical ("tight") writing: Exercises**

Let's look at a more realistic example now, of a bloated sentence.

**I will provide you with an update by the end of the day.**

Now, that sounds reasonable enough, doesn't it? But, have a go at writing that more economically. How about this,

**I will update you by the end of the day.**

Let's look a little bit closer at that. The by the end of the day reference is also a bit long-winded, because it implies simply today, doesn't it? If you provide the update by the end of the day, it also means you will provide that sometime today. So maybe, end of the day might sound a bit more revocative to you, in that case you could keep it, or maybe you like the elegant rhythm of that longer sentence, it's okay, then you can keep it.

All I'm showing you is that when you zoom in to certain kinds of expressions, **you can find opportunities for cutting back, that you wouldn't notice otherwise.** And this for me, seems the tightest way of saying that sentence. I'll update you today, now that's what we call a tight sentence.

To be clear, there's nothing sinful about saying I will provide you with an update by the end of the day, it's not a sin, but notice how much punchier the sentence is now.

Now in isolation, you may not think what we've done here is you know, like a really big deal, but imagine if this kind of flabbyness was present across all of your sentences. It's going to make your writing slower and heavier, isn't it?

When you substitute flabby writing for highly charged, tightly edited sentences, the whole reading experience gets lighter and less cumbersome. How about this sentence,

**The specific point I am seeking to make is that the colors red and gray go well together.**

Now, think carefully about each word in that sentence and look for opportunities to cut.

Okay let's see, specific point? Points, are by nature specific, aren't they? So, we could get rid of that word. The point I am seeking to make is that the colors red and gray go well together.

Why do you need to say, the point I am seeking to make? You could shorten that to say, my point is that the colors red and gray go well together.

Do you even need to say my point is? We know it's a point, right? So, you could say, the colors red and gray go well together. It's obvious red and gray are colors though, right?

So, **red and grey go well together** would be just as good.

And going well together simply means that they match, right?

**Red and grey match.**

Now, just see how much real estate we've saved by boiling the idea down to it's essence and taking away all the unnecessary words. **That is how you get power and charge in your writing.** Red and gray match, is a strong vivid statement. The original sentence was dull and tiresome.

So, what about this one?

**I am seeking a new job at this moment in time.**

Well, where can a moment be, other than in time? So, you could say I'm seeking a new job at this time.

And why bother saying at this time, when this time is now anyway, right? **I'm seeking a new job.**

Now, **our sentences usually land on the page with extra fact in them because that's just how our thoughts are constructed, but you can usually only see how inefficient they are once you start really actively looking**. After some practice, you'll be able to tell immediately when a sentence is bloated, just by looking at it.

Here's another example from the plain English campaign's website.

**If there are any points on which you require explanation or further particulars we shall be glad to furnish such additional details as may be required by telephone.**

Now, look at it from the angle of tightness, and create a shorter, tighter, firmer sentence instead. Now, here is the website's solution.

**If you have any questions, please phone.**

And that's all it really means. And how about this,

**I will take your proposal into consideration?**

Well, you might think that sounds pretty tight already, but maybe not.

**I'll consider your proposal.**

Often, you can replace these other things like at the present time, you could say, what? **Now or currently.**

**Exhibits a tendency to**, you could say **tends to. In view of the fact that, because** often works instead.

**A large number of**, it's usually **many**.

So, please get into the habit of spotting these tremendous opportunities that you have for cutting down words to create this punchiness I'm talking about.

But, don't forget to factor in other important considerations while you do that.

·         Like, **have you shrunk the meaning?** That is, have you compromised the clarity of the idea too much, by cutting so much?

·         Have you **made the sentence sound too clipped**, perhaps? That is, have you sacrificed too much elegance in your quest for simplicity?

·         Or, have you **removed too much of the colorfulness** of the writing? That is, have you sacrificed too much evocativeness for simplicity?

Those are the sorts of considerations you should be weighing up when performing simplicity operations like this. Because remember, we're balancing the ingredients. **When none of those other ingredients are aversely affected, cut, cut, cut, for maximum efficiency**.

**Redundant words**

A special class of words that contribute to flabbiness in sentences are **redundant words**. They come in the form of twin words, often, appearing side by side, that mean exactly the same thing, phrases like the sum total. Well, the sum is the total. Take this sentence:

**The four pebbles in his left hand and the two in his right added up to a sum total of six stones.**

Bloated, as well, because added up to takes away the need to say either sum or total. You could just say:

**The four pebbles in his left hand and the two in his right added up to six stones.**

**So always question the way that words are used together, as many redundant words creep through** this particular net.

We're so used to seeing certain words twinned together that we don't even notice the redundancy unless we think about it.

**Redundant words: Exercises**

Okay, where is the redundancy here?

**As Bob opened his present, he got an unexpected surprise: inside the box was the red shirt he'd always wanted.**

Well, the surprise by its nature is always unexpected, isn't it? Now how about this one?

**The project, a joint collaboration between two rival firms, was a huge success.**

Well, a collaboration is always undertaken jointly, isn't it? Now this one's trickier.

**Michael's boss summoned Paul to his office to discuss the department's future plans.**

Well, you always plan for something that's gonna be in the future, so future plans becomes a bit of one of those redundantly twinned words.

How about this?

**Passive sentences harm not only clarity but also simplicity and evocativeness as well.**

Well, also and as well mean the same thing.

And this one:

**The athlete, born in China, set a new record at the last Olympics.**

A record, when it's set, is always new. **It's only necessary to use the phrase new record if you're distinguishing it from one that was set earlier**.

Now remember, **you can always add words back in after cutting them to enhance either the clarity, the elegance or the evocativeness of your writing.** For example:

**The American athlete, originally from China, set a record at the last Olympics.**

A relevant detail, the fact that he's American, has been added for clarity. But if it's not adding to either evocativeness, clarity or elegance, then it's simply hot air.

**More implied words**

You'd be surprised how many words we use casually in sentences that just imply what's being said anyway.

**I tend usually not to worry about the small stuff.**

Well, usually and tend to assert the same thing. I tend not to worry about the small stuff, or alternatively, I don't usually worry about the small stuff would do. You save valuable space by looking for opportunities to remove wasteful words like this, leaving room for ones that actually do important work for your writing.

**The sharp drop in earnings has triggered speculation the company may be more vulnerable than before to a downgrade.**

Well, more vulnerable means more vulnerable than before. The sharp drop in earnings has triggered speculation the company may be more vulnerable to a downgrade. It means pretty much the same thing.

**More implied words: Exercises**

Now try making this sentence a bit tighter.

**He vowed to carry out an investigation of the criminal gang's activities.**

An investigation is always carried out, isn't it?

**He vowed to investigate the criminal gang's activities****. It's implied.**

Did you spot the implied word that's still left in there, though? **Activities**. If you're investigating a criminal gang, what else would you be investigating other than its activities, right? You could say: **He vowed to investigate the criminal gang.**

Now, it's not that the first version is wrong or anything. It's just not efficient.

**We often use implied words unconsciously as well when we're kind of trying to describe something.**

**The room was quite dark and dimly lit.**

Well, quite dark, a quite dark room doesn't have to be dimly lit, but a dimly lit room would have to be quite dark, wouldn't it? So you want to think about stuff like that.

And this one:

**Nokia continues to maintain a close watch on regulatory developments.**

The **word maintain implies continuity already**, so to maintain means to keep on with something, right, which basically means to continue.

So you could say: **Nokia continues to watch regulatory developments**.

**Nokia maintains a close watch on regulatory developments**

**Don't let implied words slip through the net of your awareness**. Some invariably will slip through, but just do your best to catch them.

**Long words**

George Orwell was big on simplicity at a time when flowery language I guess was more fashionable than it is now. Famously he once said, "Never use a long word where a short one will do." Amateur writers habitually default to longer words when the shorter equivalence will basically do the job fine. And I'm not opposed to long words, but like Orwell, I get irritated when people assume that using longer words makes you more intelligent or more sophisticated.

At the same time, don't be dogmatic about preferring shorter words either. Sometimes a longer word is better than a similar shorter one if it helps you to get your meaning across better, like a clarity reason.

But there's still huge value in shorter words, even beyond their lack of pretentiousness.

·         They're **faster to read so they add pace** to your writing.

·         They **aren't too heavy,** so they don't weigh your writing down.

·         They're **easier for readers to process** and all other things being equal, a shorter word is generally preferable to a longer one of a similar nature.

Take this example. **"In case of an emergency, please utilize the exit near the stairs."** Well, in what possible way could utilize be better than use in that sentence? Use is less trouble for the reader and it's less distracting for the reader because utilize isn't commonly used in speech so there is some kind of resistance to it.

Use just flies into the consciousness unnoticed. Now imagine if you kept up with these pointlessly long words over many sentences and over many paragraphs. **The effect of that on the reading experience**.

So, find a shorter word for reinstatement in this sentence.

**"Around 10 percent of the population supports a reinstatement of the carbon tax."**

Well how about **"Around 10 percent of the population supports a return of the carbon tax."**

What longer words could you scrub out or delete here?

**"Jill previously made numerous attempts to obtain a restraining order."**

How about changing previously, numerous, attempts, and obtain to get some shorter words?

**"Jill tried many times to get a restraining order."**

Now to compare the ease and speed of comprehension of that with the original.

But make no mistake, when you apply all of these techniques for economical writing, the impact across an entire piece of writing is likely to be considerable.

You may, of course, find some of the shorter words more boring. You might like to say employ instead of use sometimes and that's fine if it makes your meaning more clear for example, or if it makes the sentence more elegant or more evocative. Perhaps use doesn't create the exact imagery you want. Perhaps you're saying use too often and it's sounding a bit boring. **Do what you have to while being aware of the power of a shorter word**, of shorter words, to make your writing faster and lighter.

**Long words: Exercises**

- Which words could you replace with shorter ones here,

**"Hema's manner of talking demonstrated a disregard for social graces."**

How about:

**Hema's way of talking showed a disregard for social graces.**

Longer words like manner and demonstrate and things like that add to the weight of your sentence and make them sound, it can make them sound stuffier.

See here **"Section 2.5 of the document demonstrates the manner in which surfaces are expected to be cleaned after use."**

You've probably heard stuff like that. But compare it with this,

**"Section 2.5 of the document shows the way surfaces should be cleaned after use."**

It's just that much more quicker and faster to grasp. Now, there is a place for words like manner. **They can sometimes invoke a tone that you might like to have in a sentence.**

As here, **"The ballboy had a peculiar manner of waddling onto the court."**

It's for you to decide, **the key is only just to weigh everything up**.

Which word here could you shorten?

**"I plan to go shopping today and acquire a new pair of shoes."**

You could just say, you know, **buy the shoes****.**

Not that you shouldn't ever use acquire, please, I want to get this absolutely clear**. Just ask yourself why you'd prefer to do so and make sure you're not just being pretentious**.

And here are some honourable motives for preferring acquire to buy.

First, clarity, remember, we're looking at it in terms of the other ingredients. **You may want to emphasize possessing the shoes over the act of actually purchasing them,** so acquire would be better for that purpose, for clarity. **After all, there are real distinctions between words that you need to pay attention to, like that you can buy something and not acquire it.**

You know, gift, you can buy a gift and not keep it for yourself, so you don't acquire it.

Conversely, you can acquire something that you didn't buy, again, a gift, someone gives you a gift, you acquire it, but you didn't buy it. Okay, you can acquire it without buying it.

In many contexts though, **long and short words you know, are interchangeable, and where they are, there is a strong case for brevity**. But in the English language, there are also words like show and demonstrate that can't always be interchanged. You can show, for instance, that water boils at 100 degrees, and you can also demonstrate that water boils at 100 degrees, but the words can also be used distinctly because they have a specific meaning all of their own, for instance, you can show someone an engagement ring without demonstrating the ring to them.

**You have to be sensitive to these kind of differences between words,** and that can really help you by knowing these distinctions.

Likewise, you can use **adopt or deploy** an argument against someone, so they're interchangeable. But you can adopt children without deploying children, so there is, again, a distinctive meaning alongside a common one, so there will be times when a longer word will actually express your meaning more clearly.

Still, **in many contexts, long and short words are interchangeable, and where they are the same, there is a strong case for brevity because of what it can do for your writing**.

What elegant reason though could you have for choosing acquire over the simpler version, buy? Well, acquire may sound rounder and nicer to you, or you just may like the rhythm that it creates within the sentence, because sentences do have a certain rhythm and if one of yours sounds too clipped with buy, and you feel acquire would give the sentence some extra gracefulness, then go ahead and use it.

Maybe you've repeated the word buy so many times in a piece of writing that it's beginning to sound inelegant to you. There could be that kinda reason, so **evocativeness is another reason** why you might prefer acquire to buy.

Perhaps the visual imagery associated with acquiring over buying is something that you prefer.

Remember, writing is an art, not a science. We're like chefs, each time selecting and blending the best ingredients for the dish that we're gonna serve up.

Now, could we make this sentence punchier with a shorter word?

**Every day, I commence my journey to work on the bus before boarding a train.**

Of course, you could say,

**Every day, I start my journey to work on the bus before boarding a train.**

It's less pretentious, it's less distracting, I think. I still haven't been able to find an honourable motive for using the word commence in my writing, but you might.

**Maybe if you want to evoke a certain mood of lethargy and boredom, you could use it**. I can imagine it in a novel, for instance,

**Every day, I commence my journey to work on the bus before boarding a train.**

**The word's heaviness itself actually is helpful in conveying the tedium of traveling to work on public transport**.

**Careless repetition**

**One of the biggest mistakes that amateur writers make is that they repeat themselves.** It happens mostly unconsciously.

They'll repeat points or details, maybe in a slightly different way than the first time they said it, but really it's just saying the same thing twice.

And here's a crude example.

**Ambiguity can occur at any time in your writing, so you need to be able to spot the many different ways it can crop up in your writing.**

So you see in your writing and in your writing is repeated. Now what can you do about something like that? In that case, it's very easy. You just delete one of the mentions, so you say ...

**Ambiguity can occur at any time in your writing, so you need to be able to spot the many different ways it can crop up.**

Or alternatively ... Ambiguity can occur at any time, so you need to be able to spot the many different ways it can crop up in your writing.

Just decide what version works better for you in the context that you're writing in.

**You might decide to keep repetition occasionally for clarity, elegance or evocativeness purposes.**

For **clarity**, **you may want to emphasize certain points by restating them** as I do here, to help me teach you. For elegance, think of a speech by Martin Luther King. His "I Have a Dream" speech, where the words "I Have a Dream" create poetry when repeated at the start of each passage.

Now with **evocativeness, repetition in the same speech moves the audience to heightened states of emotion**. So are you beginning to realize how powerful this simplicity, clarity, elegance, evocativeness framework is. It empowers you to artistically balance the four ingredients to make your writing beautiful in these four different ways.

Now here's careless repetition that contributes neither to clarity, elegance nor evocativeness.

**In an opinion poll, about one in three voters, or 33%, said they would welcome the return of the death penalty.** Well one in three voters and 33%, same thing.

What about when an entire point is repeated in the same piece of writing, but far apart, so it's a bit harder to spot? That's why you really want to scour through your copy, looking for any of this kind of careless repetition and then getting rid of it. **Duplication creates unnecessary work for the reader who has to stop and wonder, hey, haven't I already heard this**?

Don't force the reader to have to process needless repetition. **It yanks them out of the reading experience.** We want readers glued to their writing as if spellbound.

**Now repetition also makes you seem like a clumsy writer.**

It sometimes happens because we haven't thought through the structure of our writing carefully enough. Now we'll learn more about structure in the elegance section. For now, **observe that if a point gets repeated later in the text, you probably haven't paid enough attention to where it actually belongs within your particular narrative**. It could be a bright red flag that you haven't organized your writing all that well.

And a giveaway is when you find yourself saying things like **as I mentioned earlier or as indicated above or to repeat the point.**

You know, if you say things like that, you have to ask yourself why saying something again that I've already said. Do I have a good reason for doing it? Keep the duplication, of course, if you're sure it's justified. Otherwise, just ruthlessly delete it. **Aspire to make a unique point in every sentence, unless there's a very good cause not to**.

**Careless repetition: Exercises**

Now edit this short article so that all the pointless repetition is removed from it. Make sure every sentence makes a unique point. This isn't an assignment that you need to share with me. It's just for your own practice, but here's a hint. **Break up the paragraph to create individual standalone sentences first so that you can get a clearer overview of each point**. And remember, **only remove repetition if it is pointless, that is, if it serves no useful purpose**, and you'll learn a lot from this process, so it's one that's really worth doing.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Okay, how did that go? Now the way I wanna do it is to look at each sentence in isolation and identify any pointless repetition that's in it. Now there are five sentences and here's the first one.

**Five new contestants will be selected before the end of June to take part in next season's Top Chef after several participants, including Tom Pickle, Sophie Ding, and Ram Chet abruptly quit the show in March.**

That's all fine there as far as the repetition goes, so let's just go onto the next one.

**The program's London-based producer, Mark Sanders, told reporters on Monday that executives were working hard to identify suitable contestants to replace the five, who he said had sadly departed on bad terms.**

Don't feel that merely because the word five is repeated that it means you have to remove it. Here **it's simply a reference that helps the reader relate back to what was said earlier, so it may be seen as a useful repetition, helps the reader to stay focused**, so it's potentially an aid to comprehension rather than a distraction. Other than that, I can't really see any pointless repetition in that particular sentence. The point being made is unique and it's sufficiently different from the first one. The first sentence tells readers the show is looking for new contestants after several quit. This one relays that the show's producer, Mark Sanders, said executives were currently working hard to find suitable replacements for the five that have left and that those who had left had done so on bad terms. It takes the story further in terms of informing readers of more details.

Now the next sentence is a quote from the show's producer.

**We're determined to get the highest-quality contestants to make up for the talent lost, Mark Sanders said on Monday.**

Here we have further detail of the type of contestants the producer is looking for, highest-quality. So the sentence usefully expands on Sanders' thinking, so there's no pointless repetition there. But if we look more closely, **there is repetition of the producer's full name and of the day on which he said this**.

Now both of those things are unnecessary and therefore make your writing look amateurish, so let's drop the repeated reference to Monday, but we do need to know who's saying this, meaning Sanders. **We could make the person, however, recognizable without necessarily repeating his full name**. We could call him Mr. Sanders or just Sanders. I'll go for Sanders in keeping with the style of the surnames that I mention further down in the story, Pickle, Ding, and Chet.

We could say: **we're determined to get the highest-quality contestants to make up for the talent lost, Sanders said****.**

And that's much tidier, isn't it? Now the next sentence is,

**He added, we expect to declare our new contestants by the end of June, of which there will be five in total.**

Now all that is just mindless repetition. We've already been told that the new contestants will be announced before the end of June, and we already know that there'll be five of them in total. And the last sentence is this.

**Pickle, Ding, and Chet left the show back in March amid disagreements with its producer, Sanders, over the amount of prize money to be awarded to next season's winner of Top Chef, a popular primetime London program, sorry, primetime program produced in London****.**

And there's a fair bit of pointless repetition there. We've already said that the three people mentioned left in March. However, this is a debatable one. The writer could be deliberately reminding the reader about the March incident again, drawing their attention back to the earlier point or reinforcing it, and it's not a horrendous repetition by any means. It's one of those like earlier that we can be strict about if we wished, and if so, we could remove it and say,

**Pickle, Ding, and Chet left the show amid disagreements with its producer****.**

To me, that's slightly tighter because it's unlikely that the reader needs reminding of that so soon. It also has further details about the disagreement, so it's definitely worth keeping. But we have again got Sanders mentioned as the show's producer and that's a no-no. And did you notice London popping up again? We've already said before that it's a London-based show earlier on, right, but we didn't hear before that it's a popular primetime show, so that bit can still stay. And with all that, here's what we get.

**Pickle, Ding, and Chet left the show amid disagreements with its producer over the amount of prize money to be awarded to next season's winner of Top Chef, a popular primetime program****.**

We also don't really need to say next seasons' winner, because we already know that these people were due to appear in next season's show, so we could just say,

**Pickle, Ding, and Chet left the show amid disagreements with its producer over the amount of prize money to be awarded to the winner of Top Chef, a popular primetime program****.**

**Now what about the repeating of the show's name, Top Chef? Well, I wouldn't worry too much about that, because it's really just a friendly reminder of what we're talking about**. So if you didn't repeat the name of the show here and there, the writing could start to become very abstract, so **you need to apply this skill very delicately, as you can see**.

**Zoom in on mindless or pointless repetition of details and points and be a bit more considerate towards other types of repetition**. Don't worry about every time you mention the same entity. Having said that, we could again remove Top Chef here because there's no danger of the reader becoming confused if we did so, and here's what we get.

**Pickle, Ding, and Chet left the show amid disagreements with its producer over the amount of prize money to be awarded to the winner of the popular primetime program****.**

That works well. We've made the sentence even tighter. We should, therefore, be left with something that reads a lot better now than the original with all its repetition. Here are the four sentences out of the five that we had that we are now left with.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

I just wanted to point out one other interesting thing, which is that if you look at that last sentence about Pickle, Ding, and Chet leaving the show amid disagreements, you'll notice that there's a slight repetition there that's not particularly obvious, which is **the show** and then **the popular primetime program.**

Now you could, if you really wanted to be smart, is **exchange the words the show at the beginning with the popular primetime program**. So it would read:

**Pickle, Ding, and Chet left the popular primetime program amid disagreements with its producer over the amount of prize money to be awarded to the winner.**

And that's another way that you could play around with stuff to kind of deal with some kind of repetitions that are not necessarily all that obvious. I just want to point that out to you. **Comprehension there is perfect. There's no confusion and it's a lot tighter and leaner than the original and easier on the mind.** It's punchier and it's lighter, and that's really what we're aiming for with this and the other simplicity tactics.

**Rambling (saying too much)**

You know another big mistake that armature writers make is giving the reader too much information, more than they need to really understand a point. 

As an elite writer, **be acutely sensitive to the audience interest lever to make sure that you're not providing more information than is strictly necessary.** 

As a slick narrator, you should be in the habit of judging where the best cutoff point is for information. 

Ask yourself, am I saying too much about this topic, or this part of the topic in this sentence, or in this paragraph? Am I labouring the point? Stay closely focused on what the reader needs to hear. 

Now, that kind of discipline creates dramatic tension within your writing. Everything is important. Everything is essential. 

When you labour your points, your writing starts to drag. Your message starts to get diluted. 

**Stick to what the reader needs to know, not what you need to tell.** 

Now, there are two way you might give too much information. 

- One is illustrating too much, a point that you've made. 
- Second is explaining too much, a point that you've made. 

Say you're editing an obituary of Steve Jobs, the former Apple boss. Now, there are so many things that you could say about the man. I want you to just have a quick read about the one that I found in the London telegraph. Here's what it says in a section of the article about Jobs' early past. Pause and have a look at that. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Okay, did you read that? That done pretty well in my opinion. **The detail is tightly written focusing on fascinating and entertaining facts about the man after he passed away**.
