Now, there are many more details of Jobs' early life that could have been added into that section of the narrative. You could illustrate his offbeat character and the chaotic course of his life in so many more ways than that article does. But doing so endlessly would just labour the point right. 

It's quite a subjective thing, but even just one more example of his early life might have been overkill. You have to weigh that up. 

Asking**, have I said too much to make my point about this**, will set you apart from most other writers who just ramble on. At some point you're going sort of illustrate his early life too much. The segment could get over blown in relation to the rest of the piece. Another segment might be about Jobs' illness, for example, there our question will be again, have I said too much about the illness, about how it was diagnosed, and treated, and so on. And when is my point adequately made? 

Lets say you're writing another example, a college essay in English literature, and working on a segment about how Jane Austen uses irony to engage readers. One of several of the literary devices that she uses. You have a huge list of examples of irony to hand, but you simply can not and should not include them all. 

**You decide whether you have enough illustrations to make your point sufficiently.** It can mean the difference between boring your readers by over illustrating, and keeping them glued to your tightly focused writing. 

Now, the second way armature writers give too much information, as I said, is by **over explaining things**. Imagine this from a blog post opposing the controversial activity known as fracking. Fracking is used within the energy industry to describe the process of extracting natural gas from buried rocks. A practice some believe can have harmful environmental effects. Now, here's one paragraph from that blog. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

As you can see, that's a pretty detailed description of this process. 

Do your readers benefit from such a long-winded explanation of the process, or could you cut off the information tab much sooner than that? Use that same information now to create a shorter explanation that focuses tightly on just the environmental hazards of fracking. Imagine your blog post is for quite a general audience that isn't interested in the intricacies of the fracking process. 

Now, here's what I get. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Now, I left out the parts about injecting fluid at high pressure, and gases delivered to the surface through pipes and all that. You may have different details or phrased it differently, which is fine, as long as you've thought it through.

**Double negatives**

Okay, another thing that we've got to avoid doing is over-complicating things. 

In a way, you're making simple points sound a lot more complex than they actually are. Now, it's another way that we get simplicity in our writing. 

As an elite writer, look to simplify these things as much as possible, whatever your idea is. **Unnecessary complexity gives readers needless headaches.** 

And one way we **over-complicate is by using double negatives**, like this: 

**He was not certain that he would not make a mess of it.** 

Not certain and would not. It gives you a bit of a headache, doesn't it? 

So try and turn it 'round into a positive expression. How about this? 

**He thought he might make a mess of it.** 

Or perhaps,

**he worried he might make a mess of it.** 

Of course, there are **rare times when a double negative will capture a precise nuance that you want to have in your writing** and, if so, that's acceptable. But otherwise, they only interfere with comprehension. 

Now here's another one:

**It wasn't that John didn't care about Jenny; he just didn't love her.** 

Now it's not too difficult to understand that one. But, just for practice, try to iron out the negatives and put it positively. Okay,

**John cared about Jenny, but he didn't love her.** 

It means pretty much the same thing still. We should recall our four beauty-creating ingredients, simplicity, clarity, elegance, and evocativeness. 

We've seen that **double negatives can violate the beauty of simplicity**, right? 

But what clarity reason might you have for keeping a double negative in your writing? As I said, to capture maybe a particular nuance. Is not caring the same as caring? Does John actually care about Jenny? The writer may not wish for us to assume that John cares for her, so the double negative in that case could stand, if the writer's making some kind of distinction there. 

But is there an elegance reason for using a double negative? **Maybe you like the music of the sentence with the double negative there.** It does flow quite nicely, doesn't it? It wasn't that John didn't care about Jenny; he just didn't love her. 

So there are considerations that come into your choices, apart from just simplicity. As always, we want to balance these ingredients for the best impact. 

And what about an evocativeness reason for using a double negative? Is the original version of our sentence about John and Jenny more moving than the other one? 

Let's see. John cared about Jenny, but he didn't love her, versus, it wasn't that John didn't care about Jenny; he just didn't love her. 

Is there a different quality of emotion attached to each version? Is the double negative version a little bit sadder, perhaps? Personally, I think so. You may not agree. 

But the negative construction feels just a little bit sadder to me than the positive version of it. It pulls on my heartstrings just a little bit more. 

So ask, **does one way of putting an idea stimulate you differently?** And **that's what evocativeness is all about**. 

Still, **in a work report or an academic essay, you want to have a high bar for using double negatives**. Don't give your readers extra work unnecessarily, and think mainly about the simplicity, the beauty of simplicity that you get from getting rid of double negatives.

**Multiply entities**

And the other way we unnecessarily complicate our writing is by something I call multiplying entities beyond necessity. We also tack unnecessary descriptive words onto a sentence, like this,

**Air New Zealand's international long-haul flights division is doing a lot better.** 

Now weed out the unnecessary descriptors so the reader doesn't have to wade through too much detail in order to extract the main idea. Here's what I get. 

**Air New Zealand's long-haul division is doing a lot better.** 

For a New Zealand airline, it's international division is very likely its long-haul division, right, it's a small country, so you probably don't need to say flights, as well, if most people will know the company is an airline. 

Now here's another made-up example entities multiplied beyond necessity. 

**This is the US company's first overseas partnership since it acquired Italian gourmet food concept store chain Gourmet Foods.** 

Now it's a bit of a mouthful, isn't it? We've got gourmet food concept store chain as five descriptors. And whittling it down to all that's really needed to make sense of the idea, what would you get? Well, this would probably do. 

**This is the US company's first overseas partnership since it acquired Italy's Gourmet Foods chain.** 

Now that's easier on the eye and on the mind, isn't it? The fact that it's a store is obvious. The fact that it's a food store is also obvious, from the company's name. The fact that it's a so-called concept store, well, maybe conceivably relevant, I'm not so sure I even know what that means, exactly, but even so, it's a detail perhaps that could be left till later so that it doesn't crowd out the main idea. 

Now here's another example. 

**Malcolm Turnbull became leader of the Commonwealth of Australia in 2015.** 

How would you deal with the entities multiplied beyond necessity there? Well, you usually don't need to spell out the official, formal name of a country, do you? So I'd just say,

**Malcolm Turnbull became Australia's leader in 2015.**

So it's just a case of trying to spot where you've inflated things a little bit. 

Now consider a headline that reads, 

**Chinese Visitors Boost Heathrow Airport Traffic Volumes.** 

Where have we multiplied entities beyond necessity there? Isn't **traffic volumes** merely a long-winded way of saying just traffic? 

**Chinese Visitors Boost Heathrow Airport Traffic.** 

**Notice how much better it reads when the thickness and heaviness of stuff is just taken out.**

**Overstretching thoughts**

- The writer Mark Twain supposedly once said this to a student. 

**"I notice that you use plain, simple language, short words and brief sentences. That is the way to write English, it is the modern way and the best way. Stick to it, don't let fluff and flowers and verbosity creep in."** 

Now, I agree with that, largely. **Brief sentences are snappy and punchy, and generally, they make comprehension of your ideas very easy**. 

Have you ever read sentences that are just ridiculously long? And if you have, then you'll get this point. Sentences can often get too long for two basic reasons. 

- First, **a writer may put in too many subclauses into the sentence,** making his or her train of thought hard to follow. Here is an example of too many subclauses. 

**Honda faces growing competition in the U.S., where economic conditions remain weak, from Mazda, a rival from Japan, which recently teamed up with Ford, a North American automobile manufacturer, whose earnings have been severely dented by Europe's debt crisis.** 

Notice how difficult that sentence is to follow. It sends your head, really, into a spin, doesn't it? You easily lose the thread, making it really unpleasant to read. The endless thoughts within thoughts send you in circles, almost making you dizzy. Now, **some people write like this, unfortunately, because they assume that long sentences make them appear smarter**, perhaps. They go on and on with these long, convoluted sentences imagining that the audience is, maybe, impressed with it, but they're more likely to be irritated by it. 

- The second way that thoughts end up becoming too long in sentences is when a writer simply strings too many point together, points that could be broken up into shorter sentences. To create an example for this, I rewrote the top of an article from the Guardian newspaper linking up sentences that were written separately in the original piece. Have a read of that. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

It's not too difficult to follow this particular sentence, but just look at how many individual points are stacked together. 

**Sometimes I like to think of a sentence as a unit of breath and the reader here has to hold their breath for a pretty long time before they get to the end** of that sentence. 

There comes a point when you can't contain it, where it's uncomfortable to hold that breath. The whole thing, it's not as difficult to get a sentence that have these numerous thoughts within thoughts in them, but still, these sort of endless points makes it still slightly too long for comfort. 

**Shorter sentences than that allow readers supports to catch their breath.** 

So, you wanna decide consciously whether or not you have allowed a thought in a sentence to stretch beyond the reader's comfort zone. 

It's more a feeling than any kind of technical consideration like how many exact word you've used. But what can you do about it when a thought does feel too long? Well, you can start by breaking down the points in that sentence, and here's my breakdown

![](data:image/png;base64,)

You can stich those four points together in so many different ways. You could stack them all together into a monster sentence as we saw. You could combine the first two points and the last two points to make two self-contained sentences. You could combine three points into one sentence and make the fourth into a stand-alone sentence. You could even keep all the points as individual, separate sentences. 

So, pause this and play around with each of those scenarios and decide which one works best. 

Now watch me, here are two sets of two points, so you've got a two-two structure here. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

That's almost the same as how the Guardian actually did it, the two-two structure. It solves the problem of the over-long thought. We allow the reader to catch their breath each time, making the whole thing easier to follow. But you could also do it like this, in a one-two-one structuring of the points. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

 Now, we could also go the stand-alone sentence route, one-one-one-one, and say, 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Now, there's nothing wrong, nothing unsophisticated about doing it like that. It's very clear and it flows okay. I probably like the version three, but anyway, let's try and fix our first example now of thoughts within thoughts. So, remember we had too many subclauses that make our heads spin and we'll try and deal with that one now because that's a bit more difficult. Remember the stuff about Honda? Here it is. 

**Honda faces growing competition in the U.S., where economic conditions remain weak, from Mazda, a rival from Japan, which recently teamed up with Ford, a North American automobile manufacturer, whose earnings have been severely dented by Europe's debt crisis.** 

Now, it's fine to have your points containing passing references to other things, these side points or points within points, but never lose sight of what the main point is, as in this example where it is getting lost. 

**Try to showcase one point at a time, which may have its qualifiers and other things within them, but in a way that doesn't sabotage your main idea** as these over-long thoughts do. **The problem is that the more subclauses or parentheses you're putting into your sentences, the more the reader's mind may have to spin** this way and that, so I want you to pause this and have a go at rewriting the passage by breaking the whole thing down into two sentences. You can keep subclauses in there, but in a way that's not disruptive to the reading experience. **More simple and easy to comprehend when it's not so long and intricate, right?** 

As one sentence, two easily-identifiable points, albeit with their one little subclauses, but there are more tricks for simplifying these sentences even further. 

You could play around a bit with the subclauses, or the little asides, as I call them, such as a rival from Japan. You could incorporate that same idea, a rival from Japan, into the meat of the sentence instead of having it as a subclause or an aside. 

That gets rid of the commas and parentheses to make comprehension even easier. Now, here's a list of the current subclauses, or asides or thoughts within thoughts. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

At least two of those could be folded into the main points so that they're no longer asides. Can you see how you might be able to do that? Let's see. 

**Honda faces growing competition from Japanese rival Mazda in the U.S., where economic conditions remain weak. Mazda recently teamed up with North American car maker Ford, whose earnings have been severely dented by Europe's debt crisis.** 

Now, what was previously as an aside, a rival from Japan, has been folded into the main part of the sentence to say Japanese rival Mazda. Doing that has taken away the thought within a thought construction that we had earlier and integrated that subclause into the main idea. It means we've also made the thought, the whole thought, even shorter. Notice, too, that I've taken the subclause, a North American automobile manufacturer, and folded that in. I describe Ford a bit more simply as a North American car maker and I integrated this description in such a way that it no longer was a thought within a thought. Now, you can hopefully see that doing this has made both sentences a little punchier without all those asides and subclauses in them. 

So, **you wanna start noticing opportunities like that, where you can keep on simplifying your sentences further and further.** 

The end result is often sharper and crisper writing, which also makes your point progressively easier and faster to get. Now, here's an article from The Economist, but one where I've created one long sentence out of many sentences, just to illustrate the idea. Have a read of that. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Use that article to practice your new skills with. See if you can turn it into smaller sentences, bite-sized ones, that are easy to get and more pleasant to read. Now I'll reveal how The Economist wrote it so you can compare.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Unpopular president Jacob Zuma has to vet his crowds carefully at the end of his term.

he risks a clamour of boos and jeers Almost wherever he speaks, even from members of his own party, the African National Congress (ANC).

He never got the chance to speak At a rally organized by the country-s main trade union federation because Union members shouted him down

This should have been a perfect opportunity for him to drone on about the party-s achievements Since ending white-minority rule in 1994 as the Union is formally allied with the AN.

**Unnecessary ceremony**

- **Don't create unnecessary ceremony in your writing, like capitalizing words when you don't really need to**. 

I've noticed people do a lot of this in many different types of writing, like reports for an investment bank or press releases or news articles or blog posts. If they use the word government, for instance, in the middle of a sentence, for some inexplicable reason, they've put a capital G on it. Or if they write the word central bank, they'll put a capital C and a capital B at the front of each of those words. 

I often wonder why they're doing that. And I don't really understand why, but I used to do it myself, too, before I became conscious of it. And then I realized that it creates clutter around the writing that distracts from the message. 

**It draws too much attention to itself, and that's what I mean by being a distraction.** You'll find that when you remove all visual distractions from a piece of writing, it makes the whole thing that bit more readable and easily digestible.

**The fewer distractions there are, the more frictionlessly your words can enter a reader's consciousness**. 

A government is just a government, right? So there's no need to capitalize that word mid-sentence, unnecessary ceremony. Similarly, a central bank is just a central bank. It's just a description of an entity. Though it's an organization, there's no real reason why an organization that's merely described needs to be crowned with this capital letter. 

Okay, so compare just how it looks, you know. 

**The Central Bank meets today for the last time this year,** 

versus without the unnecessary ceremony of the capital letters.

 **The central bank meets today for the last time this year.** 

No distraction, it's somehow more sober and graceful. 

Of course, if you're writing out the official name of an organization, like the **United Nations**, you'd obviously want to capitalize the first letter of both of those words because that's a convention. 

But after, if after spelling out the official name once, you were just calling it, referring to it casually thereafter as like the **world body,** as a description, I mean, then we wouldn't want to write the world body like that with capital letters or anything over the top like that. 

Likewise, if you're referring to the **People's Republic of China**, you'd obviously want to capitalize the first letter of each of the substantive words because that's the official name for China. But if you were just writing the government of China or China's government, **there's really no need to capitalize what could be considered a description rather than an official name**.

**Unnecessary ceremony: Exercises**

- Let's start with some very easy examples of unnecessary ceremony. 

**North Korea's repeated Nuclear tests have been trying the Chinese Government's patience.** 

Easy, right? 

**North Korea's repeated nuclear tests have been trying the Chinese government's patience.** 

The capital G and N are a needless distraction from the meaning of the words. 

Now here,

**India's Central Bank kept interest Rates on hold this month.** 

You could just write it like this. 

**India's central bank kept interest rates on hold this month.** 

Again, the reader doesn't stumble across any clutter that's unnecessary and can just focus on extracting the meaning from the sentence. 

**Now, all of these little details might seem unimportant to you right now, but they make a difference when you add them together with all the other simplicity tactics.** 

Now as always, there are some nuances here that you also want to consider. Take a sentence like this. 

**The victory, on this occasion, went to Manchester United Football Club.** 

You could capitalize the whole of Manchester United Football Club because it's the official name of the club. But I'd be okay with this 

**The victory, on this occasion, went to Manchester United football club.** 

because we have enough of the actual official name there to recognize it. 

And therefore, we can lowercase the football club bit to function more as a description because it's just one less distraction. I don't want to give the reader any more work than necessary to identify a club that they know best as simply Manchester United. 

On the flip side, if you wrote it like this, you'd be oversimplifying it. 

**The victory , went to manchester united football club on this occasion.** 

Then you'd also have a problem because your mind doesn't easily register Manchester United written in that way. So I'm just sort of showing you the contrast there. An elite writer, remember, is always trying to create the smoothest reading experience possible with the minimum amount of distraction.

So what are we to do with the capital letters in this made-up sentence? 

**The Treasury, the Central Bank and the Prime Minister, Malcolm Turnbull, together expressed concern over the high Australian Dollar and the stress it is causing to the Nation's manufacturers.** 

Well, here's what I might do. 

**The treasury, the central bank and the prime minister, Malcolm Turnbull, together expressed concern over the high Australian dollar and the stress it is causing to the nation's manufacturers.** 

Do you find, like me, that this is easier on the eye? Notice that the only capitalized portions of the sentence are now those that would be too weird to lowercase, like the prime minister's name and the word Australian. 

Also **notice how, without all of that unnecessary ceremony in the sentence, it's much less visually distracting** to read, **leaving the reader free to focus solely on the meaning of the sentence**. So, how would you go for maximum simplicity with this sentence? 

**Michael Dent, Vice President of Michelin Magnets, resigned today.**

I'd say it like this 

**Michael Dent, vice president of Michelin Magnets, resigned today.**

because vice president can function as a description. And writing it like this isn't going to confuse anybody, and it causes the least amount of distraction to my readers. 

But really, **it's ultimately a style choice**. You may prefer capitalizing such words. And I'm not saying you shouldn't do it. I'm just giving you as many tools as I can for simplifying your writing in so many ways for maximum effect.

**Excessive punctuation**

- Another type of unnecessary ceremony is overusing punctuation. It sometimes happens because people aren't confident enough maybe in their use of punctuation and so end up over compensating. 

**Very good writers are usually quite minimalistic in their approach to punctuation using it only where it's necessary for either clarity or for elegance.** 

If you take the same approach to punctuation as that you'll probably use it better than most people. The biggest problem tends to be with the use of commas and you can often find writing littered with unnecessary commas. Now, the rules around comma placement can get very complex and promise you I'm no expert on them, so I'm not gonna bore you with that but I try to stick to using commas, myself, in a common sensical way that either keeps my meaning crisp or helps me to add a touch of elegance to my writing. 

Some people however, use a comma for every possible pause they imagine is present in the writing and that approach makes, for me, a lot of needless mess. 

Unnecessary commas create just another visual distraction for the reader. 

Usually **if commas don't help the reader to navigate your pros, you have to wonder why you need them**. 

If like me, you're not a grammar expert you can go a long way with common sense. Just take a look at this sentence. 

**Should the worst-case scenario, in fact, unfold, it would be impossible, unfortunately, in this instance, to guarantee, in a reasonable amount of time, the safe passage of the refugees, despite our best intentions.** 

Now, all of those commas could, you know, when spoken allowed correspond to natural pauses within the sentence. 

They may even be grammatically correct but does that mean that they should all remain there? That they have to remain there. I personally don't think so. 

Faced with a monstrous sentence like that I'd begin looking at ways to cut back on those commas without harming comprehension or elegance. Try doing that yourself using simple common sense. Leave the commas the contribute to clarity and maybe elegance to and do away with the rest. Don't, for the purpose of this exercise, get rid of any of the words. 

Test: Should the worst-case scenario in fact unfold, it would be impossible unfortunately, in this instance, to guarantee in a reasonable amount of time the safe passage of the refugees despite our best intentions. 

There you have all the commas you need to make perfect sense of the sentence while maintaining much of its gracefulness and elegance. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Now, there are other ways for you to do it, too, that you maybe have preferred but I hope you, too, are left with minimal distraction. 

**Another area where I see it over punctuation is in the use of quotation marks.** 

Now often these symbols are used without thinking and so can create unnecessary disturbance, as here where no one is being directly quoted. 

**The "controversy" surrounding this year's Pride march is difficult to comprehend.** 

Now there are times when putting words within quote marks is justifiable. 

They can be used to denote somebody's actual words, **they can be used to highlight slang words, phrases that you've coined or words you want to distance yourself from or two denote skepticism toward an idea**. 

But you want to really **establish whether they're necessary in a sentence.** 

**The controversy surrounding this year's Pride march is difficult to comprehend.** 

Is the writer saying they don't believe there actually is a controversy, are they quoting somebody, if so, who? You could quite easily just say it like this. 

Now, here's another one. 

**There's a real danger that the banking system will collapse if the "political interference" we're seeing continues.** 

Again, why the unnecessary ceremony of quote marks? Is someone saying political interference, if so who? Are you in doubt as to whether it actually is political interference? If not, you're creating a needless distraction and a mystery. You're better off writing more simply just

**There is a real danger that the banking system will collapse if political, political interference we are seeing continues.**

**Chapter 4 – CLARITY**

### The beauty of clarity

**In the simplicity section, you learned a bunch of tactics to help you write in a concise, no nonsense and non-cluttery way.** 

That was the first step toward becoming an exceptional writer, learning all about simplicity. 

You saw **how writing in a simple way helps readers to get your message with the least amount of friction, obstruction or resistance. That if you like is the beauty of simplicity** that I was talking about. 

In this section, I'm going to introduce you to a second ingredient in the secret sauce. And that ingredient is **clarity.** I'm going to reveal to you the beauty of writing in a clear and a focused way. 

I want you to understand that **clarity is arguably the most fundamental of the four ingredients** that I'm teaching you. Because unless your ideas are clear, there is really little point in trying to enhance them in other ways. 

Imagine you've written down a sentence that's not entirely clear. Now, if you start trying to dress the idea up by making it more punchy, more graceful, and more stimulating, **what's the point if the meaning remains unclear?** 

In other words, you can get away with not having any other ingredient in your writing, but if your writing isn't clear to begin with, the other ingredients are virtually going to be useless. So we really need to understand the concept of clarity very very well. 

- **Clarity is about ensuring that your ideas and points convey your intended meaning as effectively and as crisply as possible**. And there are a ton of things that stand in the way of that. To put it into context, **simplicity was all about removing the heaviness and the slowness in your writing**. Which, to be sure, also stands in the way of easy comprehension. 
- **Clarity is about making sure your meaning gets across beautifully to your readers. It's about making sure they understand completely what you’re saying** without having to question anything in your writing. It's about making sure no one gets baffled by your argument, for example. Making sure that their minds, the readers’ minds, never go blank while reading your work. Making sure that they aren't confused at any time by the terminology that you're using. 
- Clarity is about **pinning down your message** so firmly and so precisely that **there can be no doubt whatsoever about your message.** 
- On top of that, it's about **making your writing so crisp and sharp that not only do people understand what you're saying,** they'll understand it pretty much instantly. 

Take the firm decision now, never to be lazy when it comes to writing. Never leave the reader to do the work of figuring out what you're trying to say. Rather, every point you make on your own has to be unambiguously clear and well defined. 

As an elite writer, you must be conscientious enough, willing enough, dedicated enough to ensure that what you're saying is **always distinctive and always meaningful**. 

You stand by the principal **that if a reader doesn't understand what you're saying, it's your fault, it's not their fault. You haven't composed or expressed your idea well enough**, that's all. Sensing that the meaning of a sentence may not be coming through clearly enough, you'll rearrange words and ideas in whatever way you need to in order to get your meaning across brilliantly. This scale of effort in the realm of clarity is absolutely vital to creating exceptional writing. I want you to take this concept of clarity very much to heart.

### Fuzzy thinking

One of the main things that stands in the way of clarity in your writing is quite simply, fuzzy thinking. By **fuzzy thinking, I mean you aren't really clear or sure about what it is that you're trying to say.** And when you're not really clear about the idea you're trying to get across, and then you leave a fuzzy idea in your writing, it's likely going to muddy up and cloud everything else that follows. 

The trouble is that **sometimes an idea doesn't fall onto your page with perfect clarity**. In that case, **you have to polish the idea up and make sure it's as clear as it can be** before you publish or submit whatever it is that you've written. 

We've already seen the many things that you can do to simplify your ideas. And later, we're going to learn about how to add the other two ingredients to your writing, elegance and evocativeness, to make it even stronger, and even prettier. I want you to understand that before you can do any of those things, effectively, you've got to make sure that your ideas themselves make perfect sense, and that they're meaningful and clear to begin with. 

In other words, **clarity must come first.** Otherwise, as I've said, you'll merely be trying to you know, put lipstick on a pig, hoping that somehow, it's gonna look pretty. So, **once you've got your ideas down on paper, you've normally got to play around with the structure of them, or you know, with the wording of them, until you hit that moment of clarity where you can say that this is now a very clear, and a very meaningful point**. 

That's especially true of the very first thing that you say in a piece of writing, because if the very first thing you say isn't clear, you've pretty much lost the reader. 

Let's say you started reading a blog post, and the opening sentence was this. 

**In the continuing quest for greatness, remember that a lot can be learned from people whose start-ups have crashed.** 

I don't know about you, but that sentence doesn't really click for me. And you find yourself struggling to extract meaning from it. And if so, you're likely dealing with a fuzzy idea. Now, pause and think about why that fuzziness is happening.

There's a lack of clarity about various things, like who's quest for greatness? And what kind of greatness? Things like that. If you ever write a sentence like this, the first thing you want to do is figure out, **what am I really trying to say here?** 

Don't leave it there, hoping that your readers will somehow manage to figure it out, because they probably won't bother, and why should they? Before we can even get into making the sentence more simple, elegant, evocative, we have to know what it's basically trying to say, alright? So, I want to give you a bit more of the blog post, to help you figure out what the author's intent is with that sentence.

**In the continuing quest for greatness, remember that a lot can be learned from people whose start-ups have crashed. They might, in fact, be the best people to consult with if your business is stuck in a hole, as their tragic tales may help you to avert a foreseeable catastrophe. I understand that for budding entrepreneurs, like yourself, it's natural to yearn for the countless success stories in the world of business. A new study, however, suggests it may be more instructive to go and grab a beer with some entrepreneurial losers.** 

Now, those subsequent sentences are clear enough, I think, and they suggest that this might otherwise be, you know, a pretty engaging piece. But, we've still got that first sentence that hurts a bit to read, because it's fuzzy. It's lacking in sharpness, the sharpness of the rest of it. And maybe you notice that the problem is really that the two ideas in that first sentence, well, they don't really link up too well, do they? 

**In the continuing quest for greatness, remember that a lot can be learned from people whose start-ups have crashed.** 

And the problem really seems to be in the first half of that sentence. In the continuing quest for greatness. Now if you look at that, it's not very precise, is it? It's vague, it's fuzzy, it's abstract. As I said, who's quest for greatness? You know, the author hasn't really pinned down the idea well enough to make it click with the reader. So, let's have a think. 

How could we make that idea a little bit less fuzzy? So pause this, and use the other information that's there to create a first half of the sentence that links up well with the second half. 

Okay, how did it go? Here's something I came up with based on the rest of the information that's provided in the post. 

**As you battle to turn your company into a raging success, remember that a lot can be learned from people whose start-ups have crashed. They might, in fact, be the best people to consult with if your business is stuck in a hole, as their tragic tales may help you to avert a foreseeable catastrophe. I understand that for budding entrepreneurs, like yourself, it's natural to yearn for the countless success stories in the world of business. A new study however, suggests it may be more instructive to go and grab a beer with some entrepreneurial losers.** 

See how in a pretty simple way, you can turn that fuzzy sentence into something that just clicks? It just sounds, you know, jazzier now, doesn't it? Don't you feel we've achieved a better link-up of the two ideas there than in the original version? So, I hope you got something similar. Or hopefully, even better than that.

### Missing links

We've just seen how not being very clear about what you wanna say creates fuzziness in your writing. I want you to see now the clarity also suffers when causal links in a sentence don't line up properly. Now, this is a really subtle point but I want to give it to you anyway because it helps to make your writing really exceptional. Let me show you what I mean by causal elements in a sentence. Here's a sentence about Australia, a country that exports a lot of raw materials like coal, copper and iron ore. I want you to really try hard to understand what it means completely, this sentence. And don't worry if you don't have any knowledge of the subject because that doesn't matter here. We're just looking at the sentence as a piece of writing and it's really helpful to do that with stuff that you aren't that familiar with. Now, here's the sentence. 

**Plunging coal exports forced a sharp drop in government revenue this year, economists say.** 

Did you get it? Or do you experience, as I do, a bit of a mental blank when you're reading, as if something's kind of, you know, missing from it. Do you find you had to read it again in order to really get it? Do you find that you have to supply some kind of information of your own to truly get it? Well, that's because something actually is missing. Take a close look. 

Basically, it's meant to be a causal statement, right? It's saying that plunging exports caused a steep drop in government revenue this year. The sentence is trying to draw a link, basically, between two happenings. Between falling exports of coal, et cetera, on one hand and lower government revenue on the other. Now, there is definitely a causal link between those things, as most economists will know. Falling exports do often cause government revenue to fall, but why? Why do they do that? Why are those two things linked? In this sentence that we have, that's not really spelled out, the link is not spelled out. 

The problem is that the two things are quite far apart, they are linked, but they are far apart. Exports don't just fall and then lead to lower government income. **The causal link between those two things in this sentence isn't spelled out clearly**. 

In other words, there's what I call a missing link in the sentence. The two ideas correlated are just a bit too far apart to create immediate understanding of the meaning of the sentence. That means the reader has to supply the connection themselves, if they know it. If they don't know it, then they're really in trouble because they'll just draw a blank. **We can make this sentence easier even for a seasoned economist to understand if we choose to make that causal link between those two things that we've highlighted explicit**. And I want you to watch how doing this actually overcomes the abstract nature of the original sentence. But first we've got to know what the missing link is, right? And in this case, it's the link between falling exports and lower government revenue. The link is this. Lower tax receipts for the government from exporters due to their shrinking exports. That's the kind of way it links together. If you can provide this missing information within the sentence itself, you're gonna actually get a lot more clarity out of it. So let's look at this causal link again. 

Falling exports **leading** **to** less tax paid by exporters to the government **leading to** lower government revenue. 

That's the exact causal chain of events, right? Now, I want you to pause this now and have a go at writing this causal link into the sentence in a way that enables readers to get its meaning instantly, okay? 

There are various ways to make this link explicit. Here's one way. 

**Plunging coal exports forced a sharp drop in government revenue this year, economists say, as producers of the fuel paid less tax.** 

What we've done is put the ideas together in a way that's more logically connected or fully connected. We could go a step further in establishing a causal link and I wanna show you how to do this, too. You could consider that the sentence might become even more clear if you connected another two dots. For instance, if you ask why do falling exports cause lower tax payments? And the answer is because coal companies earn less when, you know, exports drop which leaves them with less money to pay in tax. So now the causal picture would look like this. 

Falling exports **leading to** lower earnings for those companies**. Leading to** less tax **leading to** lower government revenue. 

Now you do have to decide, you know, the right levels of causal detail to go into because you can overdo this and end up with massive, unwieldy sentences that state a lot of what might be obvious, so it depends on the reader. And **you do have to draw a line somewhere with causal links in the chain**, but I just want you to **be aware of how you can turn abstract sentences with missing links into clearer ideas step by step**. 

So how about you write that new causal link, you know, lower earnings of those companies, into the sentence? Have a go and then come back. 

Okay, here's what I might do. 

**Plunging coal exports meant producers earned less and thus contributed less tax, triggering a sharp drop in government revenue this year, economists say**. 

I want you to just compare that with the original version where there is a large gap between the two parts of the sentence where the causal link is very far apart. 

**Plunging coal exports forced a sharp drop in government revenue this year, economists say.** 

Notice how much easier we've made it for readers to understand this sentence quickly by supplying the more links in the causal chain. 

**The real lesson here is that you've got to write in a way that doesn't leave the readers' mind going blank, or in a way that forces them to make inferences.** What you're saying should as much as possible spell out the logical connection between ideas without, of course, making the sentence too long and unwieldy, or stating what might be too obvious to be worth stating. 

**There's an easy way of telling whatever you've left out key logical connections** as in the original sentence, because simply when you read what you've written back**, if you find that your mind goes blank while reading it, it usually means the logical connection is too far apart and that there is at least one missing link in your sentence**. 

If you're not feeling it as if it's kind of instantly, an instantly guessable point, it's telling you that something is probably wrong which means you have to go back and fill out the picture for readers a bit more. 

A lot of writers will just let this go and hope that readers will somehow make the necessary inferences, but remember, this is your responsibility to make sure all your ideas are clear and that everything lines up nicely.

### Out-of-focus ideas

Sometimes have you noticed that sentences just feel out of focus? It can be quite obvious, like here. 

**I really enjoyed the concert, it took me back to another era.** 

The technical term for that is **comma splicing where you're basically using a comma where there really should be a harder stop between the two ideas** in order to make the two independent points connect properly. Can you just feel that the two points should be expressed more independently than that? Well, this sentence is similar. 

**Paul is a hard-working student, some days he doesn't go to sleep until the early hours.** 

Somehow the comma doesn't do enough to route the two ideas firmly in place. Can you just sort of feel that? Can you try using different punctuation and words to express the two points in both those examples in a better and smoother way? Try to come up with as many ways as you can. 

How did it go? Here are some of my attempts. 

**I really enjoyed the concert. It took me back to another era.** 

**I really enjoyed the concert; it took me back to another era.** 

So, first we had the full stop, now we've got a semicolon. 

**I really enjoyed the concert: it took me back to another era.** 

So, there we have a colon. 

**I really enjoyed the concert - it took me back to another era.** 

**I really enjoyed the concert, which took me back to another era.** 

And then with this one. 

**Paul is a hard-working student. Some days he doesn't go to sleep until the early hours.**

**Paul is a hard-working student; some days he doesn't go to sleep until the early hours.**

**Paul is a hard-working student: some days he doesn't go to sleep until the early hours.**

**Paul is a hard-working student - some days he doesn't go to sleep until the early hours.**

**Paul is a hard-working student who, on some days, doesn't go to sleep until the early hours.**

So, we've put the sentence into focus, all those sentences into focus. So, here's another type of out of focus sentence. 

**As an unapologetic meat eater, friends often ask me whether I ever eat salad.** 

I don't wanna get into the formal grammatical aspects of why it might be problematic, but can you feel that it's slightly off? I agree that you can basically make sense out of it, but notice how placing the word friends where it is just throws the reader off a little bit because the first part, as an unapologetic meat eater, sets up the reader's attention to expect this unapologetic meat eater to re-enter the picture, not some other entity, in this case, friends. For that reason, the idea is out of focus. Now, as elite writers, remember that we want to prevent even a moment's disruption for our reader, so I wanna show you that it's possible to identify problems like this in your writing simply by using common sense ideas like out of focus. 

When you're rooted in trying to maximize clarity for your readers, you're going to just feel when something isn't quite right. So, how would you rectify the sentence by kind of moving stuff around? Have a go yourself first. 

**As an unapologetic meat eater, friends often ask me whether I ever eat salad.** 

Okay, here's what I might do. 

**Friends often ask me whether, as an unapologetic meat eater, I ever eat salad.** 

Or I might write it like this. 

**Friends often ask me whether I, as an unapologetic meat eater, ever eat salad.** 

Each of those solutions has its own kind of rhythm and one of them you might prefer better than another, and a certain effect on comprehension, too, perhaps, **but notice how neither of them cause even a moment's disruption to your understanding of the idea**, your basic understanding of it. The reading experience is smooth in both cases, isn't it? **And it's merely to do with the placing of the words. The small shift we've made here creates firmness and certainly about the meaning**. In other words, it's clearer. 

Here are some more examples of out of focus ideas. 

**An Apple media statement said it will launch the new iPhone early next year.** 

It will be obvious to most people that the it here is supposed to refer to Apple, but do notice that it's out of focus. There's a little jarring effect and I hope you can feel it. Why is it there? Because it reads as if the statement is the thing that's gonna launch the iPhone. So, how would you resolve that problem? Okay, here's what I might do to make the meaning clearer. 

**In a media statement, Apple said it would launch the new iPhone early next year.**

Okay, once again, feel your way through this one. It's also a similarly out of focus idea. 

**Looking into the distance, a tall building came into view.** 

What's wrong with it? I want you to just try to feel where the problem is rather than thinking about, necessarily, grammatical rules. **It's out of focus because the phrase, looking into the distance, doesn't have any connection to a person who is doing the looking**. Without that key element, it may even sound, for a moment, at least, as though the building was looking into the distance. 

In any case, it's definitely out of focus, and I hope you're beginning to see that and kind of feel your way through that. **Sentences like that are commonly referred to in grammar as containing dangling modifiers**. In this instance, that just means that it isn't clear which bit of the sentence, the first part, relates to. But I'm showing you here that you can usually just feel that there's a problem anyway. You just don't get the click that lets you feel that everything's okay and you're looking for those failures to click in order to spot these out of focus sentences, and when you spot them, you need to fix them. So, how would you fix this one? I'll tell you that, in this case, the person doing the looking is called Marcus, let's say, so have a go. Okay, here's what I might say. 

**As Marcus looked into the distance, a tall building came into view.** 

See how that's now perfectly in focus? How there's no room at all for misunderstanding or doubt or there's no going blank? When you make sure all of your sentences are in focus like this, you ensure that your readers never stumble and that's another thing that goes to what's creating exceptional writing, so it's something I want you to really start paying attention to.

### Misplaced modifiers

Now there's something in grammar referred to as a misplaced modifier. It's another thing that can create out of focus sentences, but I want to try to explain it to you in layman's terms. 

First, let me just remind you that **a modifier is a word or a set of words that alter another term that's in a sentence.** 

So, in this example, **Lucy carefully studied the instructions.** 

**We've got the word carefully, modifying the word studied.** Because it's telling you the way in which Lucy studied the instructions, that is carefully. Carefully studied. So with the phase carefully studied, the word carefully modifies the word studies, simple right? 

Even in this sentence, **Lucy studied the instructions carefully.** 

Where we've moved the word carefully to a different place. The principle is still the same because the word carefully still modifies the word studied even though it now appears at the end of the sentence instead of right next to the word studied, okay. 

So if that's the modifier, **a misplaced modifier is one that stops the reader from knowing clearly which of the other terms in the sentence the write is actually trying to modify.** 

The problem often happens when two or more terms are present that could, you know, potentially be the ones that are being modified by the other, by the modifying word. **You wanna handle the business of modifying terms in a sentence with extreme care or else the reader can misunderstand your actual point.** And that's bad for clarity. 

Take this sentence, **our firm specializes in safely analysing and storing sensitive data.** 

Well, what's the problem here? Can you tell? That's right, it's the out, it's out of focus because the modifier safely is only meant to apply to the term storing, safely storing. It's not meant to apply to the term analysing as well. After all, it's not even clear what safely analysing something could mean, right? It seems obvious that the writer wants to apply this modifier safely only to the words storing sensitive data. Or let's assume that. So please have a go at rewriting the sentence to place the modifier correctly so that the sentence is no longer out of focus. 

**our firm specializes in analysing and safely storing sensitive data.** 

Okay, how did it go? Here's what the sentence should say. 

**Our firm specializes in analysing and safely storing sensitive data.** That was a simple one, right? But **it's little tweaks like these that are going to improve the clarity of your sentences vastly when you put them together** with all the other tactics in this section, just like all the simplistic tactics. 

**Misplaying modifier, misplacing modifiers makes you sound careless**. It just makes you seem clumsy as well as **causing misunderstanding**. You should think deeply, always about where you're placing words within a sentence. 

Here's another example. **Lex called to talk about the meeting yesterday.** 

Which is the modifier here? Correct, the word **yesterday**. Because it modifies or describes, in this case, another term in the sentence. The only problem is that we don't know for sure which term it modifies, right? Which two terms in the sentence could it modify? Well, it could modify the word called, as in called yesterday, or it could modify the word meeting, you know, as having happened yesterday. In other words, we don't know for sure, do we, whether Lex called yesterday or whether the meeting was held yesterday. Let's assume the word yesterday is supposed to modify the term called. That it refers to when Lex called and not when the meeting took place. In that case, where would place the word yesterday inside that sentence? 

That's right, the sentence would read like this, **Lex called yesterday to talk about the meeting.** 

And what if the modifier yesterday is supposed to apply to the meeting and not to Lex's call? Well, that's right, you'd say **Lex called to talk about yesterday's meeting** or something like that. 

**The lesson here is don't be careless. Don't just hope readers will get what you're modifying. Zoom in and focus it for them.** 

You might feel, very occasionally, that for some other reason, say for the sake of elegance, you may want to compromise, ever so slightly, on this clarity principle. Perhaps you'll feel that the preceding context has made it already abundantly clear what the modified thing is. For example, the reader already knows from a previous sentence that the meeting, you know, has happened yesterday or didn't happen yesterday. So the term yesterday has to be referring to the call in that case. So you may conclude that it's okay if the sentence isn't perfectly in focus because you know, you happen to like the rhythm of it or something else, one of the other ingredients comes in. But I'll just say, please set a very high bar when it comes to clarity, even a momentary doubt about your meeting, your meaning, will spoil the reading experience. And as an elite writer, we really wanna avoid that.

### Ambiguity

Very close to the concept of out of focus ideas is the concept of ambiguity in your writing. 

**Ambiguity just means leaving readers unsure of what your meaning's actually, because what you're saying could have more than one meaning.** It goes way beyond the problem of misplaced modifiers, because just one major ambiguity in your prose, of any kind, can throw the whole thing off. 

The reader may lose confidence and trust in your words, and there goes your authority if that happens. So you need to get really good at **recognizing when ambiguity in all its forms is present in your writing.** 

Take this sentence, which seems harmless at first: **My older students know I'm extremely careful with my language.** 

What's the ambiguity there? That's right, older can mean, you know, can have two types of meaning. You could be referring to people who've been your students for a long time and are older in that sense of the word, or you could be referring to students who are advanced of age and therefore older in that sense of the word. **Be careful of leaving things open like this, unless the context already makes your meaning absolutely clear.** You don't have to bother if it's already very clear what you're talking about. 

There's also this type of an ambiguous sentence: **Shutting down the gym was justified on the grounds that children were routinely getting injured.** And see if you can spot the ambiguity there. 

That's right, this can mean either that the writer believes the gym's shutdown was justified or that the people who shut down the gym justified their decision on the grounds that children were getting injured. Now, try to make it unambiguous in both cases.

To clarify, you'd have to say either this: **The decision by authorities to close down the gym was justified given children were routinely getting injured.** That shows it's the writer saying it. Or alternatively: **Authorities justified shutting down the gym on the grounds that children were routinely getting injured.** See how each version pins the reader to a specific meaning rather than, as before, leaving it open to interpretation.

### Ambiguity: Exercises

Ambiguity can also occur at so many levels of your writing so you really need to be able to spot the different ways that it can crop up. So, let's look at some more examples of ambiguity and practice this skill of eliminating every shade of doubt about what you, as the writer, could mean.

**The friendship between Leonard and Mark has never been the same since he left town. What's the ambiguity?** 

Which of the two friends has actually left town. Let's suppose it is Leonard. So then, how would you make that clear within the sentence? 

Okay, to resolve an ambiguity like this, you've got three basic choices: 

1. you can make things clearer for your readers by actually naming the thing about which there is doubt, like this: 

**The friendship between Leonard and Mark has never been the same since Leonard left town.** 

But as you'll see in the elegance lectures, **this isn't a particularly elegant way of doing it. I mean, repeating the same word in the same sentence**. 

2. clear up the ambiguity is to alter the very structure of the sentence to make things clearer, like this: 

**Since Leonard left town, his friendship with Mark has never been the same.** 

And that's okay, isn't it? Moving around the sentence furniture has solved our ambiguity problem perfectly well. 

3. give more definition to the part of the sentence that is ambiguous. If Leonard is the one who left town, we can, without necessarily repeating his name in the sentence, make it clear in some other way that he's the actual guy we're talking about, the one who left town. Of course, in the current sentence, we don't really know, we don't have any other information about either Leonard or Mark so it's not really possible to do this. But let's say that we've established something earlier about Leonard and Mark in the piece of writing. Say that Leonard is a feisty character and a lawyer by trade, whereas Mark is a professional athlete who has more of a laid-back character. Now, just as an exercise, try using that information about those two characters to resolve the ambiguity that's in the sentence. So Leonard, we know is a feisty lawyer and Mark is a laid back athlete. Did you get something like this? 

**The friendship between Leonard and Mark has never been the same since the feisty lawyer left town.** 

Again, you have various options to resolve ambiguity and the three of them we saw**, repeat the name, change the structure or add a description**. 

Now here's one more, what's the ambiguity in this passage? 

**There is a big difference between a man who is truly generous, and one who is acting that way only for show. He will be the one offering to buy a round of drinks when most people have already left.** 

We wouldn't have any trouble figuring out which type of man the writer is referring to in the second sentence of the two that are mentioned. Obviously it is the man who offers to buy a round of drinks when everyone has left who's the one who's acting generous for show. So, the problem isn't that the words in the sentence will be misinterpreted necessarily. But the ambiguity **still creates a moment of disruption in the reading experience**. You don't get this absolute clarity as you're reading along. So how would you fix that? 

Well here's what I did: 

**There is a big difference between a man who is truly generous, and one who is acting that way only for show. The pretender will be the one offering to buy a round of drinks when most people have already left.** 

Now, there what have I done? **Characterize one type of man by giving this type of person a description**, pretender. So I hope you can see that little tweaks like this can make a very tangible difference to the reading experience. Fills everything in, it gives perfect clarity. 

The final thing I want you to think about is **ambiguity related to the use of punctuation**. The simple presence or absence of a single common can make the world of difference to the meaning of a sentence. So, you know, look at this: 

**Did you eat, comma, my friend?** A perfectly reasonable question. **But what happens if you remove the comma?** 

Did you eat my friend? Now that's much more sinister. 

**Or the panda eats shoots and leaves,** and **the panda eats, shoots and leaves**. 

A whole book is based around that concept. So please ensure you're using punctuation with all the care it deserves as well to iron out this terrible thing in writing called ambiguity.

### Careless comparisons

We already saw how ambiguity can stand in the way of clarity. Now I want to show you that to achieve clarity, you can go further than just avoiding ambiguity**. You can try to bring stronger definition to your ideas, it's taking the concept of clarity further**. You can give your writing an extra crispness in a number of different ways, and one way is to **make sure that any comparisons you make are really sharp and precise**. Don't make careless comparisons. 

**When you make direct comparisons between things, make sure that the things being compared are clearly differentiated**. The sharper your comparison is, the more powerful the writing will be. 

Imagine you must write a report for your company in which you have to compare the performance of your department between two defined periods. The periods you're comparing are the first three months of the current year, that is, January, February, and March, assume that's past, and the first three months of the previous year, that is January, February and March that is, January, February, March again, again but of the previous year last year.

We're going to call this period the first quarter for convenience because there are four quarters in the year in the year and those three months are the first quarter. Is that clear so far? Now, you're supposed to compare your department's performance in the first quarter of this year with the performance of the first quarter last year as companies often do, they compare quarter by quarter. 

What you're comparing specifically is let's say, staff turnover and how many staff from your department How many staff from your department left the company left the company in those two respective periods, in those two respective periods? Got it, okay? Here's the raw information that has to go into the report. Okay, I'm going to give you the information now, which you can also find in your resources. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Now when you're handling very detailed information like this in your writing, you need to make sure all the distinctions between the parts are really crisp, otherwise the readers could get really lost. 

So let's see how a very bad portrayal of that information would look like. Here in this one the distinctions are not crisp at all. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

It's not very clear, is it? The periods aren't crisply defined throughout, okay. What we want to do is instead write with extreme care and precision so that the reader can navigate our writing without any problem at all. 

First off, try to identify as an editor might every question you would raise seeking clarification for that first version if someone else had submitted that muddled report to you, and I'll see you back here after you've done that. 

|   |
|---|
||
||![](file:///C:/Users/montagnl/AppData/Local/Temp/msohtmlclip1/01/clip_image041.jpg)|

  
Now, here's what I think a good editor would ask in response to such a submission. So you can, if you want, pause the screen and have a long, hard look at this. 

Now, the next thing I want you to do is to rewrite those sentences with those questions in mind to provide the crisp distinctions that a reader really needs to make proper sense of the whole thing and come back here when you're done.

**6 people left the department during the first quarter of this year compared with 20 in last year’s same period.**

**One person left the department for unspecified personal reasons during the first quarter of this year while 4 left last year in the same period and for analogous reasons.**

**As of this April, 3 people left for a new job compared with 7 in the first quarter last year.**

**2 people were laid off in the first three months of this year as opposed to 9 in the previous year during the same period.**

Here's my version to compare with yours. Is that clearer? Spend some time studying closely what I've done sentence by sentence.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Notice how the distinctions are carefully managed throughout the whole piece. throughout the whole piece. Notice too how as you go down the piece once the main distinction distinction between the periods is clearly established, you can get a little bit more relaxed about specifying so much if you feel the reader is not going to lose the connection and the crispness of the comparison. So **you don't have to spell the whole thing out every single time**. **It's most important usually at the beginning where you set the parameters of what you're discussing and make clear what you're going to be doing.** And as long as you're striving to be understood perfectly, then you're gonna be on the right track.

### Clumsy contrasts

As elite writers we always want to make our writing sharp and bright and that means making sure that any contrast at all that we make, not just in direct comparisons as we saw, but any kind of contrast, you know, is always sharp, **making clear contrast within sentences is especially useful when we're dealing with information that's very busy in the sentence where there's a lot going on and you can lose sight** of the contrast being made, like in this sentence,

**The fund's investments include Southern Star Central Gas Pipeline, a transmission and storage facility provider for metropolitan areas in Missouri, Kansas, and Oklahoma, and Affinity Water, a UK-based water company serving more than 3.5 million people.**

now by the time you get to, and Affinity Water, you may have lost the plot because the sentence has so many intricate details and loose parts in it, right. Now what's the main contrast being made here, is the question you should be asking, and the sentence is basically aiming to highlight the two investments that this fund has, right. 

**The trouble is those two contrasting investments are getting kind of lost in the detail,** so it's a clumsy contrast, so we need to find some way of making the distinction between those two investments clearer, to make the sentence as a whole clearer. 

You have a couple of options here, **first you could use punctuation a little bit to make the contrast, you know, that little bit sharper,** have a go at changing the punctuation just to make the distinction between the two investments sharper, even just in a visual sense, just pause this and do it in whatever way for you makes the sentence easier to navigate. But keep it as one sentence if you can. 

**the fund's investments include Souther Star Central Gas Pipeline (a transmission and storage facility provider for metropolitan areas in Missouri, Kansas, and Oklahoma) and Affinity Water a UK-based water company serving more than 3.5 million people.**

Now here's one way to clear things up, using punctuation, 

**the fund's investments include Southern Star Central Gas Pipeline -- a transmission and storage facility provider for metropolitan areas in Missouri, Kansas, and Oklahoma -- and Affinity water, a UK-based water company serving more than 3.5 million people.**

Simply separating out the details of the first investment with dashes can make the whole sentence more approachable, can't it? 

**Another option is to break up a sentence into two smaller sentences** and make the contrast between the fund's two investments sharper in that way, so have a go at that because that might be the best way to go.

**the fund's investments include Southern Star Central Gas Pipeline (a transmission and storage facility provider for metropolitan areas in Missouri, Kansas, and Oklahoma). Another asset is Affinity Water a UK-based water company serving more than 3.5 million people.**

Okay now here's one easy way of doing it, 

**the fund's investments include Southern Star Central Gas Pipeline, a transmission and storage facility provider for metropolitan areas in Missouri, Kansas, and Oklahoma. Another of the fund's assets is Affinity Water, a UK-based water company serving more than 3.5 million people.** 

Again, the contrast between the two investments comes out more sharply than in the original.

### Dubious distinctions

**Jennifer's painting was the most breath-taking, impressive, and striking piece among the seven competition entries.** 

That sounds fine, on the face of it, right? But **think about whether any of those descriptive words are, in fact, lazy, whether they're making a meaningful contrast with another word.** 

Are striking and impressive and breath-taking, all sufficiently distinct words to be worth having together in the same sentence? Well, possibly. But, as an elite writer, you do need to at least ask that kind of question. 

Breath-taking and impressive and striking all sound quite similar, don't they? Some of them contain the other word, and so on, so you wanna look at those distinctions. Doesn't the word breath-taking, for instance, already imply that the painting is impressive? 

What about if we just remove the word from the sentence, the word impressive, and said,

**Jennifer's painting was the most breath-taking and striking piece among the seven competition entries?** 

Also wouldn't Jennifer's painting have to be striking in order to be breath-taking? It's just something you can consider. How about if we got rid of striking, too? Just try it out. 

**Jennifer's painting was the most breath-taking piece among the seven competition entries.** 

Now, has removing those words made the sentence any less potent? Maybe it has, or maybe two of them together sound particularly good and evocative together. But you wanna look at how making dubious distinctions can take away some of the force of your writing. You may think differently on that particular example, but as long as you're putting that kind of quality of thought into it, you're on the right path.

### Curly writing

An elite writer strives, as I've indicated, to be perfectly understood. And that's why clarity is so important. It's also why simplicity matters so much, as well. Alright. Because both of those ingredients help us to, to get our meaning across crisply and succinctly. **You never want to write sentences that people have to go back and reread.** Now, it's okay if they go back to appreciate a point better, to deepen their understanding of it, or to contemplate it more deeply, which is something we sometimes do while reading. But they shouldn't have to go back **because they didn't get it or because they were confused by it**. 

Now, **curly writing is a particular style writing that uses a roundabout construction to get across the points, points that could be said, in a more straightforward way**. This is an example of curly writing. 

**While a four out of five chance of avoiding recession doesn't sound too alarming, it is notable that this is the highest probability in the post-war period that didn't result in a subsequent recession outside of the global financial crisis.** 

There was an 80% chance of avoiding recession and it was the highest probability since WW2 that didn't result in a subsequent recession, excluding the global financial crisis. 

Now, have a go at rewriting that sentence so that it's more straight forward. Because at the moment, it's very curly. And don't worry if you can't do it. I've been trying for years and I'm still struggling with this one. Which is why I use it as an example. The construction of the sentence is so curly that a reader has to do mental gymnastics to try to stand it up straight in order to comprehend it. **And I just want you to recognize how much of a disservice it does to your readers when, when you write in this kind of way**. 

A reader basically has to uncurl such sentences in their minds first in order to get them. And it draws them out of the reading experience. If they still can't get it after trying so hard, they either drop out in frustration or they go forward to the next sentence with, a kind of a, hazy uncertainty. If you come across a sentence like this, never blame yourself for not getting it. It's actually not your fault, in most cases. The writer simply hasn't gone to the trouble of expressing the ideas in the clearest way possible. 

To be fair, the sentence is taken out of context. We mustn't judge it too harshly, just as a stand-alone sentence. But if you were confronted with this kind of sentence, even in a piece of writing that was otherwise good, would you be able to instantly get it and you know, read on? Now you can see some easy ways to help to make the sentence more easily comprehensible. Look at those negatives for a start. Like, avoiding a recession, not too alarming, didn't result in a recession. 

Notice how the writer is saying also a four out of five-chance avoiding recession. Which is giving us a fraction instead of an easier percentage number, which is normally easier for people to comprehend. Then, saying this is the highest probability that didn't result in a recession. It's a real headache to decipher, isn't it? We could also make the reader's life easier, perhaps, by **removing some of the fluff words** in there like, it is notable that. So we can do all these different things. Now that will take away the redundancy making the core ideas easy to get. And we saw some of that in simplicity. It may also help to **get rid of some of those overly formal words** like subsequent and using more familiar language when possible. Which we also learned about it simplicity. Now, with some of those changes, that I make, I end up with something like this: 

**Since World War II, a recession has always occurred when the probability of having one was more than 20% - except during the global financial crisis.** 

I still doubt whether this is what the writer actually intends to say. I'm really not sure. But notice, how the more straightforward construction makes the idea more palatable than before. Once we get it out like that you know, then we can start making sense of it. 

Here's another curly sentence that, that could put your life at risk. 

**In the event of the fire alarm not being triggered, it's not advisable, while the emergency light is flashing, to refrain from vacating the building immediately.** 

If the emergency lights are flashing, leave the building immediately, even if the fire alarm wasn’t triggered.

Now, contained in the sentence is an idea that could be stated much more straightforwardly and therefore clearly. Have a go at unscrambling it for your readers. Okay, once again, we could **turn some of the negatives into positives**, couldn't we**? Using familiar language** could also help. 

**Leave the building immediately if you see the emergency light flashing, even if the fire alarm hasn't gone off.** 

Which version, the curly or the straight one, would you prefer to see during an emergency?

### Jargon and buzzwords

A**ll professions use their own type of insider language**. Okay, it helps them to communicate in shorthand with each other. **But the jargon words and expressions they use often don't carry that well outside of those professions**. You need to stay mindful of this, **especially if you're writing for a mainstream audience or readership.** 

A doctor who does a bit of blogging for a general audience shouldn't keep saying BP for blood pressure without making clear what they mean at least first off. 

Imagine you read, you know, the letters FTP repeatedly in a policeman's blog, would you know that it meant failure to pay? **You've got to look at the audience.** 

There's also a type of general business jargon which came up a lot when I was an editor on business newspapers. Expressions like short selling, due diligence, sweat equity, which don't necessarily carry well outside of certain specializations. 

In politics you have jargon like POTUS, for President of the United States, or SCOTUS, for Supreme Court of the United States. They can be very jarring to read, especially if you're not an American. 

They even talk now about internet jargon like LOL and so on. 

If you're an insider within any particular niche, you may get comfort from seeing that kind of specialized terminology, but just be aware that they probably, you know, don't travel well outside of your profession or in different countries or among members of other social groups. It's something to just keep in mind. 

**This lecture is about jargon that harms the clarity of your writing, particularly.** 

Here are some examples to **help tune your ears to such rubbish which can cloud your meaning substantially, because they don't usually carry much of a specific meaning**. 

I've seen many press releases, for example, describing a company as providing:

customer facing solutions, 

customer focused solutions, 

client centred solutions, 

value added solutions. 

Or they describe the company as having an outcomes-based orientation, or systems aligned procedures, or best in class analytical tools. Now I mean, what actually is an outcomes-based focus? 

**You know, usually they're just big words, right, but what do they actually mean**? Usually nothing much at all. And saying with even very common expressions, more common than that, you know, that you might see, like value added solutions. What does that actually mean? I mean, please don't litter your copy with such things unless it has a very specific meaning. 

Challenge yourself instead to be clear and concrete about what you mean. Which is clearer, this jargon laden sentence that you might find in a press release? 

**We are the market leading service provider in the entertainment space offering state of the art, value added solutions to stakeholders around the globe.** 

Do you get what they do? Would you get it if they put it like this. 

**We are the world's biggest cinema chain.** 

So, avoid vague jargon words like value added solutions, stakeholders, and other things like that. **Go for language that nails what you mean** you know, really perfectly. 

Be especially careful with your cover letters and resumes, many of which I've seen laid on thick with this kind of impenetrable jargon. 

**I am a dynamic individual and best in class team player dedicated to evolving an innovative approach to managing workflow environments in the marketing arena of the retail sphere.**
