Okay, you mean, **you've spent the past three years running marketing campaigns for a major supermarket.** 

Well, that in fact, would be a lot more interesting way of putting it to a potential employer, who will at least know what you've been doing. 

Now one last point. In investment analyst reports, you often see sentences like this. 

**We see the company's outlook as solid over the medium term.** 

The jargon there makes it very **vague**. What does solid mean in your view, and what does medium term mean. Very commonly used. One year down the line, three years, 10 years. 

Learn to spot when you're using jargon, and unless you're deliberately trying to obscure your meaning, which is sometimes the case, spell out exactly what you mean. Usually, it's going to sound a whole lot better.

### Mixed tenses

Mixing up your tenses is one thing that contributes very much to unclear writing. Tenses are basically, you know, past, present, and future. Now, while talking, mixing up your tenses is forgivable. Right, it's sort of how we talk but as an elite writer**, you need to be highly conscious of your tenses and make sure you keep them straight in your writing**. 

**Make sure that you're not switching between tenses in a haphazard or lazy way**. 

I've read great articles that take a reader back and forth between events in the past and currently, but that's done consciously and deliberately for style, not by accident or by default. Carelessness with tenses can negatively impact even a simple news article, take this one. Now, can you spot the dodgy tenses there? The word rescue at the start. The word says further down. Both of those are in present tense while everything else is written in the past tense. The piece is jarring to read as a result. You can't get full clarity on it. It creates a certain fuzziness in your writing that takes away from this thing, absolute clarity, which we're trying aspire to. I'm not gonna go into the grammatical aspects of this. I just want to encourage you to use some common sense. To notice when you're mixing tenses. Like here. Jake says he will not go to the party, even though he knew it will probably be the last chance he had to see Cathy before she is leaving for Paris. You can just feel that that's wrong, can't you? It doesn't create a clear picture for the reader because the tenses are sending you all over the place. Okay? They're sending your mind all over the place. Look at the mix up. Jake says, present, he will not, future, go to the party even though he knew, past, it will be, future, the last chance he had, past, to see Cathy before she is leaving for Paris. Try to clear that up so that it establishes clearly that Jake is talking in the past about the future. So, you start with Jake said and then continue from there. And remember that throughout that Jake is speaking in the past, he's talking about an anticipated future event, so you're looking for some balance between past and future, there. Okay, here's my tidier version. Now, see how much more easily comprehensible that is? None of that jumping around effect. Now, try the same thing by putting it all into Jake speaking in the present tense with Jake says. Jake says he won't go to the party, even though he knows it may be the last chance he gets to see Cathy before she leaves for Paris. Now, you can normally feel when it's off, so you can go a long way without knowing, even without knowing the particular grammatical rules around tenses. So, I want you to be very conscious of tenses in your writing and to make sure that they all line up really beautifully.

### It versus they

Now which of these two sentences sounds better? 

**Microsoft has launched a new operating system that they say will be their best one yet.** 

Or,

**Microsoft has launched a new operating system that it says will be its best one yet.** 

The difference is between referring to Microsoft a company as an it or as a they. Now, I don't know which of those you prefer, it might be either one of them, but is a company an it or a they? 

My general rule which I picked up from my time writing about companies for newspapers is that **when you refer to an entity a company or an organization, you usually refer to it in the singular.** 

Apple is an it. The United Nations is an it. The Justice Department is an it. The Government is an it. Having this rule keeps things nice and clear. 

**There are however some notable exceptions.** Even in newspapers, for some reason, **sport teams are often referred in the plural as they,** so you would say,

**Manchester United play their first match of the season on Saturday in a game they are widely expected to win.** 

Notice the words their and they. You wouldn't normally see them, Manchester United referred to in the singular because it sounds funny. Manchester United play its first match of the season on Saturday. It's just maybe because we're so used to hearing it, the other way sounds a bit strange to the ears now. Now, it might be because we think of sports teams more in terms of their players, a collection of individuals. Maybe, I don't know. The same principle usually goes for pop or rock bands like the tendencies to refer to them as they. Like,

**Pop legends U2 kicked off their latest tour with a spectacular version of their best-know song**,

for example. It sounds better than, **pop legend U2 kicked off its latest tour with a spectacular version of its best-known song.** 

Again, a bit weird. References to police also tend to be in plural, as in, police say they have arrested two suspects in connection with an incident. So, pay attention to the its and the theys that you plug in to your writing when referring to entities and mostly, when you're talking about entities, it will be it and not they. And keep those references consistent throughout, don't keep switching it up as I've seen a lot of people do. As a journalist, I came across lots of reports randomly alternating between it and they when talking about companies. It looks clumsy and it takes away from the slickness that we wanna project as exceptional writers.

### This, that, and the

Two words used a lot in writing are this and that. When used carelessly, both of those words can harm the clarity of your writing. 

Let's start with the word this. **This is a word often used to refer back to something mentioned in a previous sentence**. 

**Teachers of the white crane style of kung fu encourage students to cultivate a tenderness of mind and lightness of body. This gives fighters the ability to kick more forcefully.** 

The problem with saying this to refer back to something in that way is that **it can often be imprecise.** 

You have as many as four different elements there that the this could potentially be referring back to and it could be referring back to one or more of those elements. You've got four things that might enable fighters, basically, to kick more forcefully. 

- First, teachers who encourage tenderness of mind and lightness of body, that's one way of reading it. Those teachers, by encouraging this tenderness of mind and lightness of body, might enable fighters to kick more forcefully, right? 
- Secondly, you've got the white crane style of kung fu. Now, that may enable fighters to kick more forcefully. You could be referring to it that way. 
- Third, you've got a tenderness of mind and that might enable fighters to kick more forcefully, and you've got a lightness of body and that may also enable fighters to kick more forcefully, or any combination of those things. You might say that, in this example, the writer's meaning is kind of obvious. 

I think I know what's meant, but how can I know for sure? That's the question. This used in that way creates an ambiguity of its own kind. Now, it doesn't do a great job of specifying which exact previous element or idea it's referring back to. 

Let's assume the writer is talking about lightness of body leading to stronger kicks. How would you make that clear in that sentence? Have a go. 

Okay, well, there are several ways to tackle this kind of situation. **The most obvious route is to simply restate the previous element that you're talking about.** 

**teachers of the white crane style of kung fu encourage students to cultivate a tenderness of mind and lightness of body. A lightness of body gives fighters the ability to kick more forcefully.** 

Or you could be more subtle than that because that's kind of a bit too crass. 

**Teachers of the white crane style of kung fu encourage students to cultivate a tenderness of mind and lightness of body. A supple physical stance gives fighters the ability to kick more forcefully.** 

**Now, we've done away with the vague this and replaced it with something specific that refers back precisely to the element previously that we're talking about.** 

**The word that, on its own, would've given us exactly the same imposition and imprecision, and thus, lack of clarity.** Note the abstract this as well in this sentence I'm gonna show you now. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Now, you have to ask, although what continues? Australia faring better than other developed nations since the crisis? Are you talking about the global financial crisis, are you talking about Australia's strong trade links with China continuing? Even if you can figure it out, it still requires a little more of effort to still get perfectly clear and be completely unambiguous. 

Now, I'm gonna tell you that the this in this case refers to Australia's strong trade links with China. Knowing that, how would you write that more clearly? 

**Australia fared better than many other developed nations during the global financial crisis thanks to its strong trade links with China. Although robust two-way trade between the countries continues, financial ties remain weak.** 

**Now, you need to be careful similarly with the words these and those, which also refer back to something said previously, like this and that. It's best to be specific unless there's absolutely no chance of ambiguity there.** 

And here's something else to consider. **The word that is sometimes better for referring back to something than the word this because this can have a subtle connotation of something yet to come, as in, this is the way that I make fried eggs**. 

First I heat up the pan and so on, right? That has more of a connotation of something already mentioned. It's not wrong to say this in reference to something said previously and sometimes it can work better than that, even. I've found that at times I prefer this, but it's something to weight up in your writing, this subtly different connotation. The same goes for the word these, which can have a more forward-looking connotation, while those can have more of a backward tilt. You don't have to agree with this idea, but I encourage you to play around and experiment with it. 

**An alternative word to this and that that often works quite well, which I wanna tell you about, is the**. For me, the word sometimes can sound just a little bit nicer in some instances, like this. 

**Australia maintains strong trade links with China. The enduring relationship has enabled Australia to withstand recent shocks to the international economy.** 

For me, that sounds quite elegant, perhaps because it's slightly less schoolteachery than this and that. So, I'm just giving you, all the time, options that you have. 

**One final thing I wanna say about the word that, it's an overused word and one that can often be omitted completely in many cases.** And journalists do this a lot, take this sentence. 

**The specter of a landslide opposition victory has intensified speculation that the prime minister will call an early election.** 

Now, some purists may be against doing this, but in journalism, it's quite common for us to drop the word that in such sentences to create a slightly more punchy sentence. 

**The specter of a landslide opposition victory has intensified speculation the prime minister will call an early election.** 

Still, you should also be careful with omitting that. It's not something you just need to be careless about. If you feel a sentence losing either some of its clarity or its elegance, then it's probably worth keeping that in. 

**Retailers are discovering that redesigning their own-label brands to look more like those of upmarket products is helping sales.** 

If you take out that in that sentence, it would read, I think, a bit more messily or like a car crash. 

**Retailers are discovering redesigning their own-label brands to look more like those of upmarket products is helping sales.** 

Now, you feel that the potential for a trip-up there at some times when you read it. Because hearing the words retailers are discovering redesigning, well, it can lead the mind in a certain different direction than the one that it eventually, the sentence, goes in. **By contrast, the that gives you a certain stability and more of a perfect clarity.** Now, on the elegant side, the that can also create a certain rhythm to the sentence. Removing it from that sentence makes the sentence, for some people's taste, maybe too clipped and the rhythm of it changes. So, look for opportunities to scrap the that, but if in doubt, either for reasons of clarity or for elegance, keep it in.

### Remove clutter

What do I mean by clutter in a sentence? 

Basically, by clutter, I mean **unnecessary information that can take away from the sharpness of a sentence. Information that, while perhaps necessary or important to what you're writing, could be moved further down into the writing.**Simply, to clear up space for the main idea. See, amateurish writers often pack sentences with as much information as they can without thinking about the extent to which it might obscure their main point. **They'll include days of the week, percentages, and other such distractions that, while possible important** and worth keeping in the text, **don't necessarily need to be said all at once**. 

I want you to acquire the power to decide exactly how you want a sentence to look. And how to shift information in and out of sentences without feeling scared or daunted by the process.

**The simplest way to deal with what I'm calling clutter is basically to move less important, or less vital, ideas out of the sentence.** You can simply get to those points later. 

And I'll give you an example from journalism, to begin with. 

**The Reserve Bank of New Zealand kept interest rates steady near a record-low 2.5% on Wednesday, signaling satisfaction with the speed at which the economy is recovering.**

Now study that sentence closely. There's nothing really wrong with it. It could easily fly as the start of an economic story in a newspaper. But notice that it has details in it that aren't necessary to express the main idea. Let's call it secondary detail, if you like. Or incidental, and not essential. 

There, they aren't unnecessary words that we want to get rid of completely. But they are non-urgent. You can afford to push them down. And try to identify what might be secondary detail in that sentence. 

You know, it's kind of arbitrary, we're sort of guessing. But move those details out of the sentence as you feel is right and save them for later, or further down. Okay? Have a go. 

Now here are the details that I think could wait till later. 

Near a record-low 2.5%. And Wednesday, okay? I would regard those as secondary details. So, removing that secondary information, assuming it wasn't urgent to note, would leave the sentence clearer, like this. 

**The Reserve Bank of New Zealand kept interest rates steady, signaling satisfaction with the speed at which the economy is recovering.**

Now, without those little two details, you're getting the primary idea coming out very sharply, aren't you? Remember, you don't have to say everything at once. And if you have too many details blurring the signal of your sentence, then this is a good way to deal with that problem swiftly.

**Chapter 5 - ELEGANCE**

### The beauty of elegance

The beauty of simplicity we saw is that it makes your writing light and fast, not slow and heavy. Then we experienced the beauty of clarity, and saw how it makes your writing crisp, sharp and focused. 

**The beauty of elegance, the subject of this section is that it makes your writing orderly and graceful. It's the quality that makes writing flow.** 

Now **imagine what can happen when simplicity and clarity meet elegance.** They're enough to create enormous power in your writing because when they come together, the results can really be magical, because elegance the quality that can turn something clear but clunky like this: 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Into writing that's musical and flowing like this: 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

- The following lectures will help you to understand what gracefulness and orderliness mean in the realm of writing. 
- You'll see how to make your writing less chaotic or random. 
- You'll learn how to avoid monotony, and how instead, to give your writing rhythm and musicality. 
- You'll discover how to infuse elegance into the very structure of your prose. 

Elegance comes in many forms. We'll look here, at among other things, elegant words and sentences, elegant narratives and elegant presentation. All these lessons will contribute to giving a certain look and feel to your writing that most people who write have no idea about. 

At times, elegance is a difficult concept to get one's head around. It takes time to fully understand, for instance, stuff like rhythm and musicality in the context of writing, but I'm gonna do my best here to transfer my own understanding of this wonderful concept of elegance to you, so that you can begin experimenting with it in your own writing. It's a concept I love because though it's so hard to define, it's a quality that gives writing a very tangible feeling of beauty. 

Simplicity and clarity can help to make your ideas succinct and bright, but elegance is that almost mystical aspect of writing that can make someone read something you've written and go, "Wow, that was beautiful." 

**That beauty comes from two things: the way the writing is arranged and the way it moves.** 

With this ingredient, we're getting deeper into the artistic realm of writing, and that's exactly why the concept of elegance is so hard sometimes to explain. 

**It's about viewing your writing not just as a piece of text anymore, but as a composition where every note, every pause and every accent is a line to create something that resembles, really, a beautiful piece of music, if anything.** So, as you can see, I'm really excited about being able to teach this concept elegance to you, as it's not one that's taught, really, much anywhere else. But believe me, learning how to use this ingredient will put you miles ahead of most writers.

### Presentational elegance or "house style"

Presentational elegance is something that many writers overlook, but it can make a big difference to how others perceive your work. 

**By presentational elegance, I mean much more than just clearing up the typos and the spelling mistakes in your copy, which is, of course, very important.** 

The concept I'm going to teach you here is something called **house style.** 

**These are style rules that create a distinctiveness through consistency in the way that things are written.** 

You'd be surprised how far major newspapers go to ensure consistency on so many different levels. In fact, many publications have their own official style guides that go into minute detail about these issues. 

**Everything from how you should refer to a particular country, to whether or not a particular word should be capitalized or hyphenated or not. Style rules are brought in to give a flavour of unity to the writing.** 

If you have a blog, you too can give it a look of consistency or orderliness using this idea of house style. 

- It's basically about having principles about how you refer to things like, say, book titles, are they always written in italics, or do you put them inside quotation marks when you mention a book. 
- Decide whether you're writing U.S. or UK English, where some words are spelled differently, like color. 

It will give all of us more confidence in you as a writer when your writing appears to have some kind of discipline to it. When you bring a sense of order to your writing, it makes you seem more authoritative as well. 

So, in what areas of your writing could style rules come in handy? Let's think about that. 

1. **Headings**. Should all your headings start every word with a capital letter in them? Or should only the first letter of the first word in the headline have a capital letter. 

For example, The Economist writes headlines like this. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The Wall Street Journal may write the same thing like this. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

In a book, how do you want your chapter headings to look? Here are some variations. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Should your subheadings match the main headings' style, exactly or be different in some way. **The thing is to try to avoid inconsistency, unless there's an artistic reason for having inconsistency.** You can go really deep with this, way beyond the question of headlines. 

2. **Punctuation**. Do you want your dashes looking which of the following ways?

![](data:image/png;base64,)

 Decide which one of those that you prefer, and stick to it throughout at least that piece of writing. And don't mistake it for a hyphen, for instance. 

Whether it's a cover letter for a job, a report for work, or a blog article, you'll do well by bringing this degree of order into your writing at the presentational level. 

**Orderliness has a resonance all by itself, so take the trouble to create a beautiful house style for yourself**. People may eventually recognize your style after a while, your writing may be recognizable just by its look, the way the headlines are, the way that the paragraphs are arranged, and everything will point towards a certain identity. 

There's a controversial matter, say, of the Oxford comma. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The Oxford comma in that example is the comma that comes after the word pen, which isn't strictly necessary because it's the second last item in the list, and many people wouldn't use a comma there, the Oxford comma is where you do, even in the second last item, include a comma. 

Some people don't like it, others do. And you might decide to have a more intricate rule around Oxford commas, like, as some people do, you use the comma only when you have more complex items within a list, where it helps to distinguish those items better, like this. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

And if you have that kind of rule, your focus is mainly on clarity. 

But fundamentally, it's about bringing order to your writing through these style rules. 

3. How you'll **highlight words that you want to emphasize**, do you wanna underline these words, italicize them, or bold them. 
4. How you'll **refer to movies**, there's many different ways that you could do it, have a look. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Don't write movie titles one way on one page of your book and then in a different way on the next page. You're looking to create consistency, presentational elegance. It gives the impression of being very, very professional. 

5. If you **introduce a person** within a piece of writing, whom you refer to as John Davis, and if you mention him later on, do you say John Davis again, or Mr. Davis, with a full stop after the word mister, or just Davis. 
6. In newspapers, you sometimes get style rules around **numbers**. Some say to write out numbers one to nine as words, but use figures from the number 10 upwards. But what about when you have a mathematical formula, or have to start a sentence with the number 11, for example, and will you say 

**There's a 20% chance it will rain today."** 

Or

**There's a 20 per cent chance it will rain today.** 

Where percent is written out as two different words. Or, where percent is written out as one word. Keep it consistent. 

7. How will you **introduce organizations and later refer back** to them? 

For instance, will you say The United Nations on first mention, and later The UN, once you've spelled it out the first time. Or will you write on first mention, this. Where the UN is in brackets. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

8. **Quote marks**, will you put the commas and full stops inside the quote marks, like the Wall Street Journal does, like this? Or outside, like the Guardian does? 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

It can depend on the country you're in, as well. But whichever you use, keep that consistent throughout, or it's gonna look inelegant. 

Will you use rounded quote marks, the diagonal ones, or the vertical straight-lined ones. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Will you say UK and U.S. like this, with dots in between each letter, or without? 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Keep that consistent. So I hope you see that having a house style can bring a critical layer of elegance to your writing. Sure, you can write great things without this sort of care towards your presentation, but great writing, like great food, becomes even more lovely when it's presented beautifully.

### Narrative elegance

**Writing flows well when it's arranged in an orderly way, when it's well defined, and well structured, when it's not random and disorganized. It's what I call narrative elegance**, is probably one of the most important qualities that goes into creating exceptional writing. 

So, listen to this lecture very carefully, because it's also one of the least understood concepts, which explains why so much writing that we see all around us is so bad. 

Let's start by considering what a narrative actually is. **Anything you write, a blog post, a book, a report, or an essay has a narrative. It just means the material is organized in a certain kind of way**. 

There are so many ways you can organize a narrative, but most decent writing will have some kind of order to it, some kind of structure. 

Imagine doing a college essay. You're asked to discuss whether Shakespeare's play, King Lear, gives useful insight into mental illness, for example. 

Such an essay could take a narrative form like this. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Now there's a certain elegance to that narrative, isn't there**?** 

**A clear progression of ideas**. 

You start with why the question is important. 

You follow with an illustration of where mental illness is depicted in that play. 

You follow that with an explanation of what kind of insights those scenes bring to the subject. 

And finally, you show how that understanding might be used for treatment of mental illness in modern times, for example. 

I'm not saying that this would make a particularly great essay, but just look at it in terms of structure. I just want you to notice how the narrative flows quite well here. 

There is an elegant progression in it. Let's call that **narrative flow**. 

Narratives can be made to flow in so many different ways. There are some very complex narratives around, which is something you can experiment with later as you, you know, get the hang of this. 

I want to introduce you to just the basic idea of narrative elegance here. I can't actually prescribe to you how you should make your own narratives flow, and that's something you'll want to work with, and experiment with. 

**There are so many different types of writing that have their own conventions as well, in terms of structure.** Certain research papers or news article, or investment reports, or business plans, some of these have a fixed, pre-established structure, a template that you might have to follow.

So you know, there's not always flexibility. All I'm saying, is it's important to make your narratives flow in some kind of way, and that simply means **making sure that the main blocks of ideas, the sections, in your narrative, are connected in a way that links up nicely.** 

**To do that, you need to have an overview of your writing, a bird's-eye view, where you're able to see the main blocks, the sections, in the first place.** 

You're writing a report to your boss, say about, the progress of the companies reorganization. Now, you want to organize your writing in a way that presents that information best, in terms of its arrangement. 

You analyze the situation first, to get a handle on the different aspects of the reorganization, and then you divide your narrative into sections relating to each of those aspects, right? These might be, for example, the categories that you come up with. 

**One, staff integration.** 

**Two, desk reordering.** 

**Three, communication systems.** 

**Four, Salary rebalancing.** 

Now, those are the four blocks of ideas that you'll be working with, that will give your narrative a particular type of definition. This is all that really, structure means, organizing your analysis that you've done into meaningful sections on the page. 

**To give that structure elegance, you have to go one step further. You have to think about how to order those categories.** See you could just do it randomly, but that's the amateurs way of doing it. 

The exceptional writer takes the extra step of looking at which ideas flow absolutely best into each other. Is it better to begin with staff integration or with communication systems? Would it be more elegant to start with the more technical aspects of the restructuring, and then get to the more people-focused stuff after that? Would you organize it that way? Or would the reverse order be more elegant, and flow better for you? 

I'm not telling you which order you should prefer, just that you should consciously choose one. You will usually find your own reasons. Some of them will be rational, and some of them will just be a gut feeling. 

Look at the key sections of this course, writing with flare. What are the sections? 

**Simplicity, clarity, elegance, and evocativeness.** 

I like the way they flow into each other right now. I think it's more beautiful than saying 

**evocativeness, simplicity, then elegance, and then clarity.** 

These categories didn't just fall into my lap in the way that I ordered them. I created that order. So, know the blocks of ideas that define your narrative, and arrange them then in the most beautiful way possible. And that's all narrative elegance is.

### Narrative elegance: Exercises

When you read good writing that flows well, elegance is present. You can see it in a simple cover letter with only four paragraphs. Will this be an elegant ordering of the information? 

**My background/experience.** 

**What I'm seeking.** 

**An interview.** 

**The job I'm applying for.** 

**Why I'm interested in that job.** 

Try to make the structure of that more elegant. Well, how about this? 

**The job I'm applying for.** 

**Why I'm interested in that role.** 

**My background and experience.** 

**And what I'm seeking. An interview.** 

Now in making writing a sales proposal about a new solution that your company has just launched. Let's call it Jinx 2, the solution. It's designed to replace an earlier product called Jinx 1. These are the broad areas that you wanna talk about in your proposal. The main blocks of ideas that are gonna come up in your sales email. 

1. how to order Jinx in order to buy it. 
2. how Jinx 2 differs from Jinx 1. 
3. the key benefits of Jinx 2. 
4. why we created Jinx 2. 
5. what Jinx 2 actually is. 

Now, is that order elegant? It's a structure, but is it an elegant structure. Not really. For one thing it's not very logical. So use your logic and your instinct to make the structure more elegant. 

I wanna do it for two purposes. 

First for sales prospects that already have Jinx 1. 

And then for sales prospects that don't know about Jinx 1 at all. 

Think about how each group will respond to each bit of information. I'm just asking you to order the categories here. And how you want the information to unfold in their minds given that you want to sell them this new product. And don't worry if the assignment is a bit abstract because we don't have details of the product. But I just want to get your mind working in this direction so that you know what it feels like to try to order something elegantly. You're just bringing that quality into your judgment. There's no magic answer written in the stars about how this has to be done. There's only what you think and feel works best for your purpose. And that's all.

First for sales prospects that already have Jinx 1. 

1. how Jinx 2 differs from Jinx 1. 
2. what Jinx 2 actually is. 
3. why we created Jinx 2. 
4. the key benefits of Jinx 2. 
5. how to order Jinx in order to buy it. 

And then for sales prospects that don't know about Jinx 1 at all. 

1. what Jinx 2 actually is. 
2. how Jinx 2 differs from Jinx 1. 
3. the key benefits of Jinx 2. 
4. why we created Jinx 2. 
5. how to order Jinx in order to buy it. 

### Stray thoughts

Now, sometimes you find points sitting around in your writing that just don't seem to belong where they're sitting. We could call these stray thoughts. Ideas without, really, a proper home. 

**Stray thoughts are inelegant.** **They happen usually because we haven't tied a point well enough to its narrative section and as a result readers may be baffled as to why you're telling them that thing that you're saying.** 

I'll give you a quick made-up example to help increase your awareness of this problem. Imagine you're writing something about a British airline called Frills Airways. Now, you're writing about a host of setbacks that Frills has been having recently. 

You've already divided the narrative up into various types of problems that they're having. 

1. Striking workers,
2. a flood of customer-service complaints, 
3. An advertising campaign that flopped, 
4. technical problems with its planes. 
5. profit decline at their long-haul business. 

Those are the five narrative blocks, right there. Now you get to the part, let's say, in your analysis of their setbacks about profit declines at the long-haul business and you write this, 

**Frills has seen profit dive in recent months due to slumping ticket sales at its long-haul business. The airline recently signed a code-sharing deal with Dubai-based Kingdom Airways. Despite problems on the international side of its business, Frills has seen an uptick in sales at its short haul division.** 

Now, where is the disconnect that you see there? That's right, it's here.

**The airline recently signed a code-sharing deal with Dubai-based Kingdom Airways.** 

And what's the problem with that? The reader can't immediately see its relevance to the rest of the points in that section. They might be able to infer the connection if they know a lot about the topic but even then, they're left to supply the information themselves. The lack of elegance here stems from the fact that this piece of information isn't actually tied very well into the narrative. Do you see what I mean? It just sits there as a loose end. 

Think about how we could tie this stray thought into the narrative. First, we have to know, of course, why you mentioned this point. The section is all about Frills’ profit problems, right? So, what does the alliance with Kingdom have to do with Frills’ profit problems? 

Let's say that the vital information that helps make sense of that loose strand of information is this. 

A month ago, Frills decided to form this code-sharing partnership with another international airline called Kingdom hoping that this would give a boost to its flagging long-haul operations, but the partnership hasn't had too much of an impact yet on Frills’ long-haul business. 

And that's why you mentioned it. Now try to weave that new information into the existing narrative in order to give that stray point an elegant tie-in to the information that's already there. 

Here's one way you can do it after you've had a go. 

**Frills has seen profit dive in recent months due to slumping ticket sales at its long-haul business. Things haven't improved even after the airline began a code-sharing partnership with Dubai-based Kingdom Airways, aimed at bolstering the struggling division. Despite problems on the international side of its business, though, Frills has seen an uptick in ticket sales at its short haul division.**

Notice how much more elegant that section is now. Everything is tied together. No stray thoughts anywhere. In other words, it's elegant.

### Elegant paragraphs

**People sometimes wonder “how do you decide how many sentences to have in a paragraph?”, and when to change from one paragraph to another.** 

There isn't actually a fixed answer, it's really about what you prefer. But **I want you to consider elegance as a key principle in your decisions about paragraphs. It's not the only consideration. You should think also about clarity,** about how clear the information is as you're switching from paragraph to paragraph. You'll often change to a new one when it helps readers to follow your argument or your instructions or whatever it is that you're writing. 

Say you're writing a blog post explaining how to cook a lasagna. And there are various steps to cooking a lasagna, right? Those steps might form the blocks of ideas that you create for your narrative, right? You might decide to change paragraphs at each new step of the cooking process, which you would make the recipe easy to follow if you switch paragraph at each new step. 

Or if each step is really long, you might switch paragraphs at other useful points within the description of each step. 

Or if you're writing an opinion piece, say where you're not dealing with steps, but with a progression of ideas in an argument, then **the paragraph changes might be used to highlight key turns in your ideas, or in the progression of your ideas.** 

If you're writing an analysis of a piece of literature, **your paragraphs could meaningfully shift as you move on to different sections in your analysis**, except some sections are too long to just have as one long paragraph. 

Alternatively, **you could change paragraphs to begin each new point,** right? So whenever you move to illustrate or elaborate or explain, contextualize or support a point that you have already made, and that's one way of doing it. 

It's really up to you how you want to shift paragraphs, but it's good to maintain some logical consistency around why you're making changes, if you can. 

But sometimes it'll just feel right. 

You could do it for clarity, so, we saw that, but where does elegance come into it? 

Let's say you're explaining that lasagna recipe and suddenly, you break a sentence out into its own paragraph for no obviously logical reason. It's not connected to any kind of progression in your recipe. Let's say you're describing how to cook the meat sauce in one section, and in the middle of that paragraph on the meat sauce, you break out a sentence as a standalone paragraph or one word paragraph, like this. After doing that, add pepper to the boiling sauce. 

And then you continue elaborating on how to cook the meat sauce in a new paragraph with six more sentences in that new paragraph. 

So then you have after that add peppers to the boiling sauce sitting out on its own in a standalone paragraph, and it's kind of inelegant to do that, right? 

Because it's messy, there's just no justification for it. It might harm clarity a little too as the material isn't organized in a way that's easiest to follow, but you may not necessary get confused by it, because instruction is clear. You can still follow the recipe. 

But it would make the whole piece look messy and ugly. In other words, inelegant. 

And another thing I wanna say about paragraphs and elegance is **don't let your paragraphs get too long and unwieldy.** 

**Depending on what you're writing, there's no telling how long each step or each section might be**. Now, that opens up the possibility of mega thick paragraphs if you're doing paragraphs with sections, and that's something you want to avoid in most types of writing. 

**It can be pretty unsightly to have really long paragraphs in a piece of text. It's also unpleasant to read.** 

In nonfiction books for some reason, you often tend to find longer paragraphs than say in newspaper articles or in marketing copy. 

Nothing wrong with that per se if you feel that it's more elegant for some reason to have your books written in that way. I'm still a fan of shorter paragraphs because, thick, clunky paragraphs just feel forbidding and they're a bit of a turnoff for me. They're unpleasant to read. 

I don't want to get lost in a jungle of points and words. I want to sort of, you can imagine you've been to a website I'm sure wanting to learn about some topic or other and you see this huge block of text that goes, right the way across the screen and also it's all the way to the bottom of the page. 

Do you feel like reading it? Even if you really want the information, do you feel like reading it? **I'm just asking you to look at paragraphs through the lens of elegance** in the hope that you'll just begin applying that principle, as well as the principle of clarity to how you switch and how you move through paragraphs in your writing.

### Elegant paragraphs: Exercises

What would you do with this passage if you wanted to create elegant paragraphs out of it. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Here's one way to do it. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

We've divided it there into points related to Luke's studying habits and points related to his partying habits. You could also do it elegantly like this. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Switching paragraphs every time there's a meaningful shift in emphasis. 

We have there, the point about studying at home with illustrations, followed by the point about studying at school with illustrations, followed by the point about partying with an illustration. 

And here's another elegant way of doing it. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

There we separated the supporting points, or the illustrations from the main point each time. But we might also re-write the thing completely differently in the way that it isn't even easily definable in words but might still be elegant in some way to you. 

For example, this. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

So there, we've highlighted a piece of the, of the text that for whatever reason, we wanted to emphasize. And that may read elegantly too. If it gives you a flow and a kind of feeling that you like. And I often do that. By the way, make sure you're not plunking paragraphs down on your page without any kind of, a, a regard for elegance. Design them so they're as pretty as they can be, giving your audience the most pleasant reading experience possible.

### Timing, rhythm, and musicality

**A key part of elegance is musicality. You could also call it rhythm or timing. Basically it's the quality that turns an ordinary or clunky sounding piece of writing into something that moves along with gracefulness.** 

Study the timing in this blog post that I showed you earlier. 

**As you battle to turn your company into a raging success, remember that a lot can be learned from people whose start-ups have crashed. They might, in fact, be the best people to consult with if your business is stuck in a hole - as their tragic tales may help you avert a foreseeable catastrophe. I understand that for budding entrepreneurs, like yourself, it's natural to yearn for the countless success stories in the world of business. A  new study, however, suggests it may be more instructive to go and grab a beer with some entrepreneurial losers.** 

Now, it has a fairly nice rhythm, to it, doesn't it? Perhaps it's not impeccable timing, but it does have a certain musical quality running through the sentences. 

Musicality or rhythm is really quite hard to define, but still most of us know it when we read it and that's what it usually makes a feel that it's well-written. 

This excerpt from Martin Luther King's famous I have a dream speech contains an element of musicality that most of us can easily recognize. 

**I have a dream that one day on the red hills of Georgia, the sons of former slaves, and the sons of former slave owners, will be able to sit down together at a table of brotherhood.** 

**I have a dream that one day, even the state of Mississippi, a desert state, sweltering with the heat of injustice and oppression, will be transformed into an oasis of freedom and justice.** 

**I have a dream that my four children will one day live in a nation where they will not be judged by the colour of their skin, but by the content of their character.** 

Explaining rhythm in a logical way is exceedingly difficult. You can't really do it by pointing to a set of principles very easily. Sometimes it's best to just show people and hope that they will feel what rhythm is. 

It's sometimes possible to break down a piece of writing in a way that can help people to understand the musicality of it by showing perhaps the beats. Look at this where I've tried to put some beats into it that show you the way that you should emphasize and give you a kind of rhythmic quality to it. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Notice how the writing has a certain poetry to it, right? 

It's possible to break that down into smaller elements of rhythm, too. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Have a look at this. Having those lines there, I hope helps you to feel the music in it. 

Try to read this sentence in a way that sounds poetic and you'll see what the opposite of music is. 

**Not only do you lock in your rate but you bring forward the tax deductibility to the current year so all the deductible interest that you would have paid over the year you bring into the current financial year as a deduction.** 

Now, apart from anything else, it's not musical and it's difficult to see any music in it, right? But would you agree therefore that it lacks elegance? 

Well, how about this one? 

**We were not prepared to stand by while a fire was burning in the house, Mr. Hockey told reporters. Unless we fix the budget together, we will leave the next generation a legacy of debt, not opportunity.**

And that's a quote from a newspaper by a finance minister and, you know, he's talking about his budget proposals at the time. 

It might not be Shakespeare, but it's better than the first example in terms of timing, I hope you'll agree. It has some kind of a musically to it. 

And if you can bring even a tiny dose of this musical sensitivity into your writing, you'll be able to add a dimension to it that most people are not even aware of.

### Timing, rhythm, and musicality: Exercises

And to help you to explore this idea of rhythm and timing, I'll have a go at trying to make a clunky sentence into something more musical. 

This one ... 

**The reason that I maintain that Mr. Davis is not exactly suitable for the job that he recently applied for is that his temperament is lacking in some important respects, and in this regard Mr. Davis would need to implement some major changes to this character before we would reconsider an application from him.** 

That's an ugly sentence in terms of rhythm, so try to make it musical. You're going to add words, change words, take words away, you can add or change the punctuation, you can create more than one sentence, but keep its basic meaning while trying to get some rhythm out of it. 

Here's one way to do it. 

**Mr. Davis isn't suited to the job he applied for; his temperament has some major flaws. Unless his character changes, we won't reconsider his application.** 

Now it's more musical than the original. Right and that's really all I'm talking about. 

We could change the music a bit if we wanted to as well, even with that new version. 

**Mr. Davis, who has some major flaws in his temperament, isn't suited to the job he applied for. We won't - unless he makes changes to his character - reconsider his application.** 

Now look at this sentence ... 

**Qantas reported record profit. The airline said it was pleased with the result. Qantas is planning to increase the number of routes it flies. The carrier hopes that doing so will boost future earnings.** 

Can you feel that it's a bit monotonous, the sentence structure there? 

Try and make that more musical. Experiment with breaking sentences up, extending them and changing their structure to get a better rhythm. 

Here's a very simple solution ... 

**Qantas reported record profit and said it was pleased with the result. To help boost future earnings, the airline is planning to increase the number of routes it flies.** 

Now it's not tremendously beautiful, but you can just see that when you experiment with rhythm, you can break monotony. It's certainly less monotonous than the original version. 

Look for it in the writing that you read. In time you'll just be able to feel immediately when a sentence is clunky and that's what we really want to avoid. Awareness string of sentences is too monotonous. Now you have all the tools you need to fix both of those problems.

### Elegant transitions

**Another way to bring more elegance to your writing is to make sure sentences and paragraphs transition really well. Sentences can occasionally get a bit divorced from each other.** 

You know, like this one:

**They say that exercise is essential for your well-being. It's a waste of time. I have friends who spend every day in the gym, and they seem just as miserable as people who don't work out.** 

Strictly speaking, there's nothing wrong with those three sentences sitting side by side. There really isn't. Each one independently makes sense. But you might detect a slight dissidence between the first two sentences. What I might call a lack of stability. 

It's very, very subtle. 

**As if something is missing that ought to come between them. Something that would connect them more fluently, that would lock them in a bit more firmly. Some kind of a transition** really, so what might you add there to create a smoother transition between those sentences? Can you feel for the kind of thing it might be? 

You could add a completely new sentence to create more stability like this: 

**They say that exercise is essential for your well-being. I don't agree. It's a waste of time. I have friends who spend every day in the gym, and they seem just as miserable as people who don't work out.** 

The extra thought there creates more stability in the transition. "They say", that part of it, is more balanced by the addition of "I don't." There's a certain musicality to it as a result and a follow through that is more elegant. 

You could weave in that extra thought in another way to balance things: 

**They say exercise is essential for your well-being, but I don't agree. It's a waste of time. I have friends who spend every day in the gym, and they seem just as miserable as people who don't work out.** 

Or you could do this: 

**They say exercise is essential for your well-being. But I think it's a waste of time. I have friends who spend every day in the gym, and they seem just as miserable as people who don't work out.** 

That fleshes out a bit more the reason for the disagreement that's being expressed in this sentence.

Or you could say this: 

**They say exercise is essential for your well-being, but I think it's a waste of time. And that's part of the same sentence. I have friends who spend every day in the gym, and they seem just as miserable as people who don't work out.** 

There we've combined the original first and second sentences again to make the connection more stable. 

Whichever way we chose, the difference is that we've created some kind of a transition that just gives the whole thing more balance, and therefore, more elegance. 

Now this: Can you think of a phrase or something that would help to bring more balance to those sentences and create a better transition? How about this? 

**Jason Fernandez spends the entire working day running office paper through a shredder. At night, he dreams of opening his own restaurant.** 

Or:

**Jason Fernandez spends the entire working day running office paper through a shredder. All the while, his mind is elsewhere, dash, on dreams of opening his own restaurant.** 

Begin noticing where you get any slippage in your prose, where the transition between the ideas isn't that well balance, where it isn't elegant. And you can fine-tune that as you see fit. 

Align them somehow, usually by creating this balance so that they sit next to each other in a more stable way.

### Parallel ideas

A closely related concept to elegant transitions is what some people call parallel ideas. 

**Keeping ideas parallel is another thing that contributes to the overall elegance of your writing.** 

Have a look at this sentence and tell me if you can see what's inelegant about it. 

**The new head of the department, Mr. Emerson, is quiet, friendly, fond of drinking tea, witty and keen on jogging.** 

Why does that sentence sound inelegant or clumsy? Well, it's because the ideas aren't strictly parallel, are they? It mixes together attributes that have a fundamentally different quality about them. Quiet, friendly, and witty are all personality traits, right? 

But fond of drinking and keen on jogging relate to specific activities that the new head of department likes to do. 

**So be careful where you list things that are qualitatively different, like this, you don't wanna just mindlessly drop things into a sentence,** because that's inelegant. 

You wanna distinguish different types of features, as necessary, to make your writing more neat and more orderly. 

There are various ways of doing this. 

**You could break the thing up into two individual sentences in order to highlight the differences more neatly.** But first, try making the sentence itself more elegant without breaking it up into two parts. Okay, here's one way to do it. 

**The new head of the department, Mr. Emerson, is a quiet, friendly and witty man who is fond of jogging and drinking tea.**

Notice how just adding man who is creates a distinction that adds neatness or elegance to the sentence. You're showing a bit of sophistication in the way that you're presenting the information, by distinguishing the attributes of Mr. Emerson's character with the activities that he enjoys doing. 

You can achieve the same elegance by breaking the items down into two separate sentences, as well. And I want you to have a go at doing that yourself, as well. 

So, how about doing it like this?

**The new head of the department, Mr. Emerson, is a quiet, friendly and witty man. His pleasures include jogging and drinking tea.**

Okay, now have a look at this one. 

**People with depression often report the following symptoms: anxiety, low self-esteem, and they frequently feel antisocial.** 

Do you notice the lack of elegance and balance in that sentence? The sentence isn't in a parallel form, which thwarts readers' expectations and causes a bit of a trip-up. 

Try to fix it so that the three ideas there sit together in a neatly parallel way. Here's one way of doing it. 

**People with depression often report the following symptoms: anxiety, low self-esteem, and antisocial tendencies.**

### Parallel ideas: Exercises

Here's another example of a sentence where ideas aren't perfectly parallel. 

**Josie is in charge of packing crates, keeping track of outgoing stock and she maintains records of customer complaints.** 

How could you make the ideas more parallel there? How about this? 

**Josie is in charge of packing crates, keeping track of stock and recording customer complaints.** 

The sentence now lines up the items in a parallel way, doesn't it? 

**You have the parallel verbs, packing, keeping track and recording, which are of a similar nature, lining up and giving the sentence more elegance and grace.** 

Another thing I wanted to say. Do you ever use the sentences that begin with **not only**? 

**Not only does smoking harm your health, it makes your breath smell.** 

Some people suggest for style purposes **always to balance not only with but also**, like this: 

**Not only does smoking harm your health, but it also makes your breath smell.** 

Does that sound more elegant to you? 

I hope that this series of lectures has given you a better idea of how to balance your sentences for more elegance and better rhythm. You've seen how doing this can bring a level of gracefulness and flow to your writing that can make a substantial improvement to the reading experience.

### Word echoes

Here's another popular journalism concept, it's called word echoes. 

**A word echo is basically a repetition of the same substantive word in proximity.** It could be that the same word occurs in two sentences that follow each other or even worse, within the same sentence. We say it creates an echo when this happens. 

It's something considered ugly, or at least inelegant in journalistic writing, right. The good news is that getting rid of word echoes is almost always possible to do with a combination of sense and creativity. 

To show you how ugly word echoes are first, have a look at this totally made-up example of one. 

**China last week reported a $10 billion trade deficit, the first trade deficit China has reported in five years.** 

Now that's pretty horrible, isn't it? First, where are the word echoes? Well, we've got China twice, we've got reported twice, and we've got trade deficit twice. All in the same sentence. 

Recognize first, that this is inelegant. You have to first get some kind of feeling about that. So, after that, how do we tidy up something like that? You've basically got two options. 

**You could delete the repeated words in the sentence, if you can do so without distorting the meaning of it.** Remember, clarity is important too. 

Or **if you can't do without distorting the meaning, then you change those words into different ones that maintain the meaning of the sentence**. So let's try the deletion method first, and let's start with the word China. 

**China last week reported a $10 billion trade deficit, the first trade deficit China has reported in five years.** 

We can't easily remove the first mention, as China is the subject of the sentence, right? But what would happen if we just deleted the second mention? 

**China last week reported a $10 billion trade deficit, the first trade deficit reported in five years.** 

Well, that makes the sentence a bit too ambiguous, doesn't it? Because technically speaking, it could mean that this is the first trade deficit reported by any country in the last five years. We need to keep it clear that we are talking, you know, only about China here. 

So if deleting the second mention of China doesn't work to get rid of the word echo, we need to change the word to something else. What are our options? Well, you know, how about the country? Because referring back to the country that we've just mentioned. 

**China last week reported a $10 billion trade deficit, the first trade deficit the country has reported in five years.** 

Okay, and that works. How about the word reported? We can't really just delete it, can we? Because unless perhaps we change the entire structure of the sentence, but I don't wanna go there just now. We could replace the word reported, perhaps, with something, another similar word which gets across the same idea, like, for example, announced, or delivered, or revealed. We could say China delivered or revealed or announced. Now let's see, 

**China last week reported a $10 billion trade deficit, the first trade deficit the country has announced in five years.** 

Now already without those echoes, the sentence is starting to look a bit more elegant and a bit more stylish. 

But how about the trade deficit part? That's a lot tougher. There aren't many words that we can replace trade deficit with as it's a particularly precise term for a particular economic concept. But have a go yourself, just to see how tough it is. 

Now you may have tried simply deleting the words trade deficit in the second mention. 

**China last week reported a $10 billion trade deficit, the first the country has announced in five years.** 

But the problem with that is that it wouldn't be entirely clear whether it's the first trade deficit that China has reported in five years, or maybe the first trade deficit of $10 billion that it's reported in five years. It's clear from the original sentence that it's the first trade deficit that China had reported in five years, not the first $10 billion trade deficit. So how about this as a solution? 

**China last week reported a $10 billion deficit, the first time in five years the country has revealed a shortfall in its trading accounts.** 

I moved the word trade out of the first mention and spoke only of a deficit and then clarified the nature of the deficit in the second part of the sentence. Now there are other ways of tackling a situation like that, perhaps even better ways, and maybe you have found a better solution, I hope so. 

But I hope at least that you've enjoyed watching this process of getting rid of those clumsy word echoes. 

**Many journalists even try to keep echoes not just out of the same sentence, but out of adjoining sentences.** For us, the same word repeated anywhere in proximity just sounds a bit too samey. Take these made up sentences. 

**Apple said profit jumped in the latest financial year, as iPhone sales picked up momentum in several countries. The profit boost comes as Apple prepares to launch a new phone shrouded in the company's characteristic secrecy.** 

Now how might you get rid of the echoes there? I might do something as simple as this. 

**Apple said profit jumped in its latest financial year, as iPhones sales picked up momentum in several countries. The earnings boost comes as the device maker prepares to launch a new phone shrouded in the company's characteristic secrecy.** 

Now that's slightly more elegant, at least I think so.

**Chapter 6 - EVOCATIVENESS**

### The beauty of evocativeness

We've now seen how simplicity, clarity, and elegance each bring its own aspect of beauty to writing. 

Simplicity, we saw turns your writing when it's slow and heavy into prose that is lively and punchy.

Clarity, we saw turns hazy and fuzzy writing into prose that's bright and sharp. 

Elegance, we saw turns writing that is messy and clunky into writing that's orderly and graceful. 

So you might ask what does **evocativeness** bring to the table? Well, **it's really the missing piece of the writing puzzle, the one that completes the picture**. 

See, you can have writing that is punchy, sharp and elegant, all of those things, but it still might be missing that crackle, that flair, unless it's also evocative. 

**Evocative writing is basically writing that has the power to fire up your imagination or to move you emotionally in some way**. In other words, evocativeness is all about writing **in a way that is stimulating, in a way that adds flavour or spice to your writing**. 

It could be that it has colourful language, vibrant imagery or passionate expressiveness. Like this passage from a Nabokov novel. 

**The males of the firefly, a small luminous beetle, more like a wandering star than a winged insect, appeared on the first warm black nights of Ardis, one by one, here and there, then in a ghostly multitude, dwindling again to a few individuals as their quest came to its natural end.** 

Now, as you can probably tell, you don't always necessarily have to have this ingredient of evocativeness for ordinary business writing, for instance. 

You can come off as professional enough with just simplicity, clarity and elegance. 

Yet, **evocativeness can add something even to ordinary business writing, if you know how to use it properly and in the correct dosage.** 

See, when I presented the three ingredients of simplicity, clarity and elegance in the first edition of this course, people would ask me but Shani, doesn't writing very simply just make your writing boring? Or what if I avoid fancy words, won't that make my writing dry and lifeless? Or do you mean that I shouldn't bother about improving my vocabulary, if plain words are always better? 

I felt that to answer those very reasonably sorts of questions adequately, I needed to bring the concept of evocativeness front and centre to any understanding of high quality writing and that's the main reason why I recreated this course, to add this section in because I felt it would complete the picture and answer all those extra questions that students were having. 

So let me begin to explain to you the concept of evocativeness. 

A quick story. I remember once as a university student picking up a copy of Milan Kundera's novel The Unbearable Lightness of Being. I recall seeing a quote about the book on its front cover and it was from another well-known fiction writer called Ian McEwan's. 

And the quote read simply "A dark and brilliant achievement," which was a quote about the book. I remember thinking that the sentence was itself on the face of it a bit off because I can understand what a brilliant achievement is, but I don't know what a dark achievement is. 

He said a dark and brilliant achievement. Did McEwan's, an accomplished writer, mean to say it in that way, a dark and brilliant achievement? What is a dark achievement anyway? 

And yet, I couldn't deny that there was something wonderfully evocative about the expression, so much so that it has stuck with me ever since those days. It somehow created a feeling of awe and wonder. It made me wanna read the novel perhaps more than if the sentence had been perfectly grammatical. 

It showed me that in very rare cases, at least, there might be exceptions even to clarity. And one of those cases, for me at least, is when it's justified by certain evocativeness that's brought into the writing. 

I'm not saying go around looking to break principles of clarity for the sake of evocativeness. Usually that won't be necessary and it's better to make your writing both clear and evocative in most of the context that you'll probably be writing in. 

I'm just showing you how powerful a principle evocativeness can be. 

Now remember I showed you earlier a sentence that George Orwell invented to show how horrible abstract language can be. 

**Objective consideration of contemporary phenomena compels the conclusion that success or failure in competitive activities exhibits no tendency to be commensurate with innate capacity, but that a considerable element of the unpredictable must invariably be taken into account.** 

Well, this was the original source that he invented it from. 

**I returned and saw under the sun, that the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, neither yet bread to the wise, nor yet riches to men of understanding, nor yet favour to men of skill; but time and chance happeneth to them all.** 

That may not be a perfectly clear or simple sentence in the way that we normally understand, yet it does trigger imagery and emotion in a way that the first version, obviously, doesn't. 

Have you ever looked at lyrics by the rock band R.E.M.? Often they appear to have no precise meaning but they do stoke your imagination and emotions a lot. Like this

**Sometimes I feel like I can't even sing** 

**I'm very scared for this world** 

**I'm very scared for me** 

**Eviscerate your memory** 

**Here's a scene** 

**You're in the back seat laying down** 

**The windows wrap around** 

**To the sound of the travel and the engine** 

**All you hear is time stand still in travel** 

**And feel such peace and absolute** 

**The stillness still that doesn't end** 

**But slowly drifts into sleep** 

**The stars are the greatest thing you've ever seen** 

**And they're there for you** 

**For you alone you are the everything.** 

Do you see what I mean about evocative? 

Now, when I encourage you to write in an evocative way, I'm not suggesting you go to the extent seen in this example where being clear about your meaning is almost irrelevant. 

For you, meaning will usually matter greatly, especially if you're writing a report to your boss. But I use these extreme examples just to give you an idea of the direction we're heading in in this section. 

**We're going to look at ways that we can bring in elements of evocativeness to enhance your writing in various ways.** 

Things that will take writing beyond just being punchy, sharp and graceful, things that make it captivating. And that right there is the beauty of evocativeness.

### Create variety

Sameness is boring and adding variety to your writing simply means avoiding sameness as much as possible. And **evocativeness, as we said, is all about making your writing more stimulating.** 

So, can you figure out why this is boring? 

**In this course, you will learn how writing in a simple way can make your writing punchier. In this course, you will learn how writing in a clear way can make your writing more comprehensible. In this course, you will learn how writing in an elegant way can make your writing more ﬂowing. In this course, you will learn how writing in an evocative way can make your writing more stimulating.** 

Now, that's boring for two main reasons. 

1. **Each sentence repeats exactly the same structure**. 

They have almost exactly the same rhythm, don't they? We learned a bit about this when we looked at musicality, but this time we're looking at it from the point of view of evocativeness and how monotony creates dullness. 

2. **Each sentence repeats the same types of words.** 

As far as sentence structure goes, notice how each sentence begins with the predictable in this course, followed by you will learn how writing in a blah, blah, blah way, and so on. The cadence of each sentence is almost identical. 

There are times when you may want to do that consciously. In fact, you see it a lot in marketing copy, where repeating the same rhythm is deliberate, like this. 

**Can you imagine being your own boss?** 

**Can you imagine having tons of money?** 

**Can you imagine having total freedom?** 

But usually, when you just repeat structures, in other context, it's just boring. 

So what could we change to give more variety to the sentence structure in the example? 

Have a go first, but do so without changing too many of the fundamental words in the sentence at this stage because I want to get to the word level later. Focus on the sentence structure only for now. Though, if you do need to chop and change a couple of words in order to do that, that's okay. 

Here's one way to get some variety into the sentence structure.

**In this course, you will learn how writing in a simple way can make your writing more punchy. You will learn how writing in a clear way can make your writing more comprehensible. The course will also teach you how writing in an elegant way can make your writing more ﬂowing, as well as how writing in an evocative way can make your writing more stimulating.** 

Now, I haven't got rid of the writing, writing, writing there, but the sentence structure I've played around with. 

The second sentence, for instance, drops the in this course structure and just says you will learn. And the third and fourth sentences combine to create a different rhythm altogether from the first two sentences. 

That's one level of variety you can add within the sentence structure. But the whole thing is still a bit boring, isn't it? Because the words are also too similar, right? Each sentence repeats the same types of words. 

Have a go at bringing more variety into the words of your new structure, of your new sentence structure. Change it up a bit. 

Here's my version of changing the words for more variety within my new structure. 

**In this course, you will learn how writing in a simple way can make your prose more punchy. You'll discover how maximizing clarity can make it more comprehensible. The course will also teach you how adding elegance can help your writing to flow better, and how, by focusing on evocativeness, you can make it more stimulating.** 

That reads better, doesn't it? The variety has given it a new liveliness. 

- Here's what I've done. I swapped writing for prose in the first sentence. 
- I've contracted you will to say you'll in the second sentence to differentiate it from the first sentence a bit more. 
- I've said discover instead of learn in the second sentence, too, for variety. 
- And I've replaced writing in a clear way with the words maximizing clarity. 
- I've changed writing in an elegant way to adding elegance. 
- I've also varied the repetitive can make your writing expression in the third sentence to say can help your writing. 
- And I've changed writing in an evocative way to by focusing on evocativeness. 
- I've also switched the word writing for it in the last sentence. 
- And there, I've also added, I've also altered the structure a bit more with the use of commas. 

Basically, I've tried to pack in as much variety as possible.

### Create variety: Exercises

Change the sentence structure and words in this passage to create more variety. 

**Standard & Poor's said Australia's top credit rating was largely safe from any future disruption to the world economy.** 

**Standard & Poor's said that, with its extremely low debt levels, the country would have time on its side to deal with any such disruption to the world economy. In the latest potential disruption to global financial markets, China said its economic growth had come in weaker than expected.** 

It could become

**Standard & Poor's said Australia's top credit rating was largely safe from any future disruption to the world economy. With its extremely low debt levels, the country would have time on its side to deal with any new jolts to the system, the rating agency said.** 

**In the latest potential blow to global financial markets, China said its economic growth had come in weaker than expected.** 

In terms of sentence structure:

- I've moved the attributions, Standard & Poor's to the end of the second sentence instead of at the start of it, like in the first sentence, 
- I've also referred to it as the rating agency so as not to repeat Standard & Poor's. 

As far as the words go:

- I omitted the second mention of world economy, which was too samey, 
- I used alternative words for disruption throughout, specifically blow and jolt, 
- I could also have used shocks or bumps or plenty of other words as alternatives too. 

Speaking of which, when it comes to words, it can be handy to keep an inventory of alternative words for the most common scenarios that you can use for variety in whatever you write, often you can consult a thesaurus, but you can also as I say, keep an inventory, especially if you find that in the type of writing that you mostly do, you're constantly repeating the same sorts of words. 

For instance, instead of repeating delta, delta, delta, all the time, you could switch it up with descriptions like the **company**, the **firm**, the **carrier**, the **airline**, so that's if you were writing a lot about the company delta.

With Australia's Qantas airline, you could refer to it at different times to Australia's biggest **airline**, Australia's flag **carrier**, the Sydney-based **company** and various other descriptions or even just as it, because, it, can often be used for a company. 

Let's say you write about financial markets or business performance a lot, at work, and you find that you keep having to repeat the words, rose, or fell, in your writing, you know, the stock rose, and that stock fell, or this company's profit rose and that one's fell or whatever.

If you're doing writing where you must use those words a lot, for rose, you've got various alternatives, of varying strength, which you have to use in a measured way:

**Increased**

**Climbed**

**Gained**

**Added**

**Advanced**

**Surged**

**Jumped**

**Soared**

They can be alternatives for rose, at different levels.

Instead of **fell**, you have these options of varying intensity:

**Decreased**

**Declined**

**Dropped**

**Dipped**

**Slipped**

**Sank**

**Plunged**

**Tumbled** 

If you're talking about the earnings of a company and you find that you're saying, the earnings were boosted by various things, a bit too often you could vary it to say instead of that earnings were boosted by something, you could that they were:

**Lifted**

**Helped**

**Bolstered**

**Driven**

**Fuelled**

**Pushed higher**

Don't feel that you must use an alternative word every time though, just consider whether it might be better to have a bit of distance between the various times that you use the word rose or fell, or whatever. 

Having variety makes your writing less dry and dull, I hope you've seen that, so I encourage you to experiment with the concept and witness how much flare it can add to your writing.

**Add freshness**

Freshness is another quality that brings evocativeness to your writing. 

Avoid using tired, worn out phrases that lack vitality because they're so over-used. There are some such phrases that we're just so used to seeing that it can be tempting to throw them into our writing without even thinking. They're like ready-made stock phrases that come easily to your mind like this:

|   |   |   |
|---|---|---|
|**At the end of the day…**|**it has been repeatedly shown that…**|**needless to say…**|
|**in all likelihood…**|**it is invariably the case that…**|**when all is said and done…**|

Or cliches like these, that have become common in business writing:

|   |   |   |
|---|---|---|
|**going forward…**|**blue-sky thinking…**|**leverage…**|
|**wow factor…**|**market-leading product…**|**synergy…**|
|**touch base…**|**interface with…**|**incentivise…**|
|**innovative service…**|**operational…**|**Low-hanging fruit…**|

Or cliches like these:

|   |   |   |
|---|---|---|
|**found elsewhere…**|**sent shockwaves…**|**thrown into chaos…**|
|**an axe to grind…**|**as quick as a flash…**||

Such phrases, because they aren't fresh, can make your writing a lot duller than if you just avoided them all together. 

**Start to develop a sensitivity to hackneyed phrases while being mindful that this can be a bit of a moving target as cultural taste and, you know, word fashions change.** 

Here are some more unoriginal stock phrases that George Orwell highlights in a powerful essay on the subject. 

**A consideration which we should all do well to bear in mind is that…**

**A conclusion to which all of us would readily assent is…** 

**in my opinion, it is not an unjustifiable assumption that.** 

Now, I'm not saying never ever use such cliches, sometimes they might fit perfectly what you're trying to convey, I'm not sure about those ones, but some might. But do set a very high bar for them. Here are some straightforward replacements for the following hackneyed phrases. 

|   |   |   |
|---|---|---|
|**Finalize (usually just means finish)**|**Output (results)**|**In my opinion, it is not an unjustifiable assumption that (I think that)**|
|**Strategize (plan)**|**Conceptualize (imagine)**|**Interface with, you could say talk to. Operational, working.**|
|**Utilize (use)**|**Parameters (maybe limits)**||

Of course, sometimes these words can have specific meanings that might help you to get your ideas across, as I say, all I'm saying is to always weigh your use of them very carefully.

### Dramatic and forceful writing
