Now, there are words that we sometimes use that can weaken writing or take away it's force. 

As we saw with cliches, these are words we often use out of habit, they don't really mean that much but we just default to them. When we litter our copy with such words, it can take away from the evocativeness of the writing in the sense that it can lessen its force. I call this weak words. But they're also sometimes referred to as **filler words or hedging words**. 

I'm talking about words like:

|   |   |   |
|---|---|---|
|**somehow**|**a little**|**arguably**|
|**somewhat**|**rather**|**quite**|

Now, often, these types of words are leaned on for security when we feel maybe unsure of ourselves and though there are times when it's okay to hedge, maybe, to convey humility or genuine doubt about what you're saying, it can still be overdone. 

**As always, we shouldn't default to such expressions simply because we've seen them a lot or because it's a habit**, especially if it weakens the power of our writing so we should try to remove them and see what happens. Take this sentence. 

**The government's decision to hold the election in September is somewhat puzzling.** 

Can you spot the weak word there? Yes, it's **somewhat**. As an elite writer, you have to ask yourself is this word there due to habit or do I really want or need it there? 

After all, what does somewhat puzzling actually mean? What's the difference between somewhat puzzling and just puzzling? Is there any difference? Isn't something that's somewhat puzzling also just puzzling? Therefore, you have to ask: does the word somewhat add any value to my sentence? 

Well, let's see what happens when we remove it and that's often the best way to test these words. 

**The government's decision to hold the election in September is puzzling.** 

And it seems to me the same thing. No meaning is lost. And it sounds a little bit more forceful, too, right? So, is the word somehow actually needed at all? 

**Notice how the new sentence sounds more robust and tight** than the original and the same goes for, you know, the government's decision to hold the election in September is somehow puzzling. 

**Ask yourself why you're saying it like that**. Again, **what value does the word somehow bring to the sentence**. 

Or worse still, you might see something like:

**The government's decision to hold elections in September is arguably a little puzzling.** 

I say that's worse because you've added in another word there, arguably, that is likely not necessary either. And now again, there are exceptions. 

One of them is where **you actually want to make a point of your own uncertainty**, about your belief in your own puzzlement, for example, in that case the words somehow will certainly help to do that. 

Maybe it would give more clarity to your readers about your own state of mind about the whole thing. Maybe you really are that uncertain about your own puzzlement. 

You might also have **elegance reasons** for keeping the somehow in there, right? You might **prefer the rhythm or the stability that this additional word gives** you in your sentence. 

And finally, you may have even an **evocativeness reason** for keeping it. For instance, you may want throughout your writing to employ a tone of extreme humility or perhaps if you thought that it made your voice sound a bit friendlier. 

I won't tell you not to do that, but I do want to say that **you can often get more force from your sentences by steering clear of these types of weak words.**

### Dramatic and forceful writing: Exercises

Here are some more hedging or weak words. 

**It seems that Apple's earnings this time around are less than impressive.** 

Can you spot them? First off, we've got the words, it seems. Now, just think about that weak phrase, it seems. If that's how it seems to you, then do you really need to say that that's how it seems? Won't the reader already assume that this is how it seems to you, since you're the one who is saying it? We can make this sentence more forceful by simply saying, 

**Apple's earnings this time around are less than impressive.** 

You still got your view across, that Apple's earnings are less than impressive. And you got it across more directly and forcefully. So please look out for little phrases like that, it seems, it appears, or I think, or I believe, which can often slip through the net and weaken your writing, people will usually already assume that this is your thought, your belief, your view, your opinion, so do you really need to take the bite out of your sentences by spelling that out? 

I'd advise only using such weak or hedging phrases where they truly do matter. 

What about the phrase, less than impressive, in that sentence? 

Well, I would have to ask, do you believe Apple's earnings were less than impressive or actually that there were unimpressive? If unimpressive, then you'd want to consider saying that, so that the sentence is more forceful. 

**Apple's earnings this time around are unimpressive.** 

Doesn't that sound stronger than, 

**It seems that Apple's earnings this time around are less than impressive?** 

Now, of course, similar exceptions apply as before. You may have reasons for preferring an extremely humble tone in your writing. For example, **you may want to take the sting out of a sentence for reasons of tact or diplomacy**, but except for a good reason, these hedging and weak words can really suck the life out of your writing. 

Look at this passage. 

**I fell down at work yesterday and my colleagues sort of chuckled. I have to admit, it was kind of funny. I laughed somewhat along with them. As they say, laughter is arguably the best medicine.** 

There are several weak words there that you could remove to create more impact, definitely. And you probably should remove them. But there are also some filler type words there that may help to create a certain tone that you may quite like in the writing. 

So you want to be able to distinguish between those things and be making a conscious choice about it, because we are all about writing consciously. Now, have a go at rewriting that passage to make it more forceful while keeping some of its warmth as an exercise. Okay, I'd probably do something like this. 

**I fell down at work yesterday and my colleagues chuckled. I have to admit, it was kind of funny. I laughed along with them. As they say, laughter is the best medicine.** 

You may have done something different, and that's fine as long as you considered the matter carefully. 

Here's some examples of more weak words. 

**Notably, this year will be a leap year.** 

Now, do you really need to say that it's notable? In what sense do you mean that it's notable? Doesn't the fact that you're noting it already suggests that the point is notable? 

It's another one of those words that we might use to impress readers, but **it often adds nothing to a sentence and to some extent takes away power and impact** from it. 

Or this. 

**Interestingly, the fruit was speckled with red marks.** 

If that's truly interesting, do you need to say that it's interesting? And might the sentence be even more interesting written like this? 

**The fruit was speckled with red marks.**

### Arouse the senses

One of the best ways to make your writing more stimulating is to create vivid pictures for your readers. The simplest way to do this is to write sentences that focus on people and action because both of those things are things that you can see. That way you stimulate readers' senses. Look at this sentence:

**Where applications for citizenship are concerned, the available forms on the desk are required to be attended to.** 

Do you know one reason why that's not very stimulating to read? It's because it's hard to visualize anything, right? Try to watch your mind as you read those words. Watch it and try to find something, some image to grab onto. It's a bit of struggle, right? You can see forms and a desk perhaps, but the rest is kind of a haze. You can sorta get the idea behind it, but no image comes through very powerfully, does it? How can we change that and make it more visually powerful? Well, we can try and turn it into a picture. So, have a go at doing that yourself first. Basically, you wanna bring some kind of a picture into sharp focus. 

Okay, done? Well, here's the simplest way of doing it. 

**People applying for citizenship should fill out one of the forms on the desk.** 

Now, what have we done there? 

First, we've got people into the picture, so now **we can visualize the scene to some extent**, can't we? Before we had merely applications for citizenship, an abstraction that's hard to visualize. 

Second, we've now got the words fill out in there, which means **we've also got some well-defined actio**n there too. Before we had should be attended to, which didn't quite create much of an image, did it? People filling out forms is, at least, a striking visual image, so we've taken this sentence from being an abstraction and turning it into a picture. So, do think of, where possible, you can add players and action to the scene. I say players because the thing might not be people, but at times, it might be a company or an organization or a football team or whatever else. 

How about this one:

**Ensure that, prior to submission for the examination, assignments have been assessed by course tutors.** 

Again, that is quite horrible, right, for the same reason. Unfortunately, a lot of educational material is worded in this abstract kind of way for some reason. How would you turn that into a vivid picture? 

First off, **who is the player** that's missing from the scene? That's right, it's you, the reader, which means that simply getting you into the picture can sometimes make all of the difference. 

You try that. Okay, this version at least gives us some kind of a picture. 

**Give your assignments to course tutors for assessment before submitting them to the examiner.** 

This gives you something to really actually see happening. 

Okay, so how about this one:

**It has been claimed, in the past, that epistemology is a difficult subject.** 

You get this type a lot in academic writing, don't you? I hope you notice first off that in the past is an unnecessary phrase here because if I was saying people have claimed this, then this would necessarily have been in the past that the claims were made, but the main problem is, once again, that **the sentence doesn't indicate who or what has claimed this**. 

So, it's difficult for any picture to form in our heads. It would be better if it was even slightly less abstract than this because then, as a reader, we would have some kind of picture to focus on at least. 

Try and fix it. I hope you notice that the trick is, again, to introduce some way, it can be as simple as saying:

**Some people have claimed epistemology is a difficult subject.** 

Or even:

**Some people claim epistemology is a difficult subject.** 

The main thing is notice just how much more vivid just **introducing a subject** into it can make the sentence. 

It's even better if you can refer to the particular type of people that you mean because the more specific you get, the more of a picture that forms. For example:

**Some philosophers have claimed epistemology is a difficult subject.** 

Or:

**Some philosophers claim epistemology is a difficult subject.** 

See how you can get a better handle on that? I hope you're getting the picture that **creating pictures like this out of abstract sentences just makes your writing that much more stimulating and lively**. 

How about this one:

**The official national threat level relating to the chances of a volcanic eruption has been changed to orange from red.** 

Can you try to create a better visual imagery out of that and by focusing on players and action? 

Well, how about this:

**The government lowered the volcano threat level to orange from red.** 

Or:

**Authorities lowered the volcano threat level to orange from red.** 

The difference is that now you can visualize some kind of entity actually doing something. Not just changing the threat level, which is also a bit abstract but actually lowering the threat level, so we've got a stronger picture out of the whole thing, do you agree? 

And why, because we saw through to the picture; cause most of these sentences have pictures that you just have to see through to and then describe it. As a result, the writing is that bit more stimulating. 

**You can't always do this with every sentence because some ideas are abstract by their very nature**. You just have to have the general intention to make your writing more stimulating, and then these opportunities will show themselves. 

Simply having that mindset will help you to notice opportunities for creating pictures where things just sound a little bit too abstract. 

You can go further actually with this technique than you might think. Even after you've got a picture formed, **you can play around with the language to make that picture even more vivid**. 

You do this basically by what I call **amplifying the picture, making it more colourful by tweaking certain words** within the sentence. 

So, for instance, instead of the rather dull word lowered, we could say something like:

**The government wrenched down volcano threat level to orange from red.** 

Now, it depends on the tone you prefer to strike. If you're going for sober and measured then lowered would do the job, but if you wanted to inject a bit of drama into the preceding, say, in a blog or a newspaper article, then wrenched down or ratcheted down or yanked down might be the way to go. They're just little tweaks that you can do to make it more vivid. 

You can play around to excite other senses too other than just the visual sense, for instance, instead of saying: 

**The financial system has come under growing pressure from currency speculators.** 

Where come under growing pressure can sound a bit dry and stale, you could say:

**The financial system is under mounting strain from currency speculators.** 

That's a kind of tactile sensation, and so it's an often used term, but you see it has a kind of a vivid element to it. 

What could you say to get, say, an auditory sensation around that same idea? Well, how about this:

**The international financial system is creaking from the accumulating weight of currency speculators.** 

So just notice how you can sprinkle these little gems onto your sentences to make them sparkle that little bit more, and you can do without them, sure, but they can definitely add a certain vibrancy to your prose if done well.

### Reversing into sentences: Part 1

When we're trying to get stronger imagery into our writing, we want to **carefully consider the structure of our sentence**s, and there's a particular habit in writing that isn't in itself bad or wrong, but something that you should be aware of. **It's the habit that I call reversing into sentences**, and here is an example of that. 

**As part of its campaign to improve the nation's health, the charity has committed thousands of dollars to fund several new projects.** 

Notice how the reversing into the active part of the sentence is happening there. 

The active part is the charity committing thousands of dollars. Now, I want you to understand that there is nothing at all wrong, per se, with that type of sentence. You don't need to avoid this way of reversing in altogether. In fact, if done consciously, reversing in can be quite a cool stylistic device, giving a variety to straight sentences that might otherwise sound too samey. 

But backwards writing, as I also call this tendency, needs to be handled with care. You should at least **be aware of the impact that it has on comprehension, and in particular, the way that pictures, again, form in a reader's mind as a result**. Done too often, **reversing into sentences can seriously slow down a reader's comprehension of your idea**, and that's why you should use it sparingly. It increases the time it takes for a picture to actually form in a reader's mind. 

To help you get a better idea of reversing into sentences, I want you to consider how you'd write that same sentence in a forwards way. Basically, just means putting the active part of the idea first so that a picture forms more quickly. Have a go. 

Okay, did you get something like this? 

**The charity has committed thousands of dollars to fund several new projects as part of its campaign to improve the nation's health.** 

Now I want you to just notice how the idea is quicker to grasp when put in a forwards way, for reasons that I'll explain. For now, just try to do the same thing with this sentence. 

**To help boost weak parts of the economy, the central bank has cut interest rates six times since November.** 

Write that in a forwards way. Okay, that one's easy, right? 

**The central bank has cut interest rates six times since November to help boost weak parts of the economy.** 

**The reason why that is quicker and easier to grasp than the original is that the cause comes first, and the effect second**. When reversing into sentence, we're usually putting the effect first and the cause afterwards, and that's just not how our brains work in terms of comprehending information. We think cause, then effect. It's easier for us to see a picture that way. Let's have another look. 

**As part of its campaign to improve the nation's health, the charity has committed thousands of dollars to fund several new projects.** 

Now look closely at the structure of that sentence. Notice the way your mind comprehends that sentence, in particular, how the first part of it leaves you hanging until the second part comes along to give you the full picture. 

Versus the other version,

**The charity has committed thousands of dollars to fund several new projects as part of its campaign to improve the nation's health.** 

**The picture there is created as you go along**. And the same thing happens here with the economy example, which is,

**To help boost weak parts of the economy, the central bank has cut interest rates six times since November,**

which is the reversing in way, versus,

**The central bank has cut interest rates six times since November to help boost weak parts of the economy.** 

Notice the different way in which your mind wraps around each idea. That's why it's often better to start with the active part of the sentence, rather than reversing in, in terms of the picture forming. As I said, there is sometimes a very good case for **reversing** in. It **can be quite elegant**, for one thing, giving a string of sentences a fresh change in rhythm. **If you've got a lot of forward sentences in a row, the occasional backwards one can add some gracefulness** to the writing, but some people do it all the time. 

When you've got a lot of very straight statements in the active voice in that forwards mode, it can begin to sound repetitive in terms of a rhythm. Reversing in occasionally can change that rhythm up, so it can be used to great effect as a device contributing to the beauty of your writing immensely when done properly. I just want to make you're not randomly defaulting to the reversing in structure or doing it all the time, because of this impact you just saw on comprehension.

### Reversing into sentences: Part 2

Now I'm going to give you an example of a three-paragraph piece of writing also about Australia, and the third paragraph is going to reverse in. But you'll see that doing this might actually help in terms of the rhythm and flow of the writing. 

**The United Nations has delayed a decision to place one of Australia's top tourist attractions, the Great Barrier Reef, on its list of endangered world heritage sites.** 

**The UN has given Australia a year to act and says the reef may lose its world heritage status if the country doesn't comply with the recommendations by then.** 

**Since a commodities boom began a decade ago, Australia has struggled to balance the interests of its mining companies and those of tourism operators and others whose livelihoods are tied to the fragile reef.** 

Notice that the last paragraph begins by backing into the sentence, whereas the previous two sentences proceed in a forwards way. Now, doing that doesn't disturb comprehension terribly. In fact, I think it nicely varies the rhythm of the writing. So I hope you're getting that reversing in can be both a blessing and a curse, a positive and a negative thing. It's all about how you use it, the measured and considered way in which you use it. On the negative side it can make pictures harder to form, but if you use the device with awareness and skill, it can be a wonderful tool for adding elegance to your writing.

### Passive sentences

Some people place passive sentences and reversing inter-sentences into the same category but I like to handle them differently because I think the dynamics are a bit different in each case. 

Now, have you ever been advised to avoid passive sentences in your writing but you've had the trouble understanding exactly what that means? 

I want to introduce you to the idea in this course, in terms of evocativeness. 

A passive sentence is one where, as with reversing inter-sentences, the picture you're trying to create forms, as I said, more slowly, than if it's phrased actively. This is a passive sentence. 

**The company was told by the regulator to hand over the documents.** 

It's passive because of the way the information is ordered. Actually, in terms of the players and the action. Who are the players? The company and the regulator, right? What's the action? It's one of those players telling another to hand over some documents. The trouble is that in this passive sentence the focus is on the thing that is being acted upon, the company. Instead of the thing that's doing the acting, which is the regulator. Can you see the difference? 

The active version of that sentence would turn it the other way around. So that the focus is on the thing that is doing the acting. Can you try and change it into an active sentence? 

Okay, here is the active version of that sentence that I hope you also got. 

**The regulator told the company to hand over the documents.** 

I hope you notice that by saying it this way around, creating this new perspective, creates a sharper picture for the reader. Read them both and see which version gives you faster comprehension of the idea, and the scene, and what's going on. It's the active one, right? 

That's because it's easier to see a regulator telling a company to hand over documents than it is to see a company being told by a regulator to hand over some documents. **The visual image forms more quickly with the active sentence.** 

Here is an even simpler example to make that point really, really obvious. 

**John hoisted the flag.** 

Now that's active because a subject simply performs an action. 

**John hoisted the flag.** Notice that there are three elements to that sentence. John, the subject, hoisted, the action, and finally, the flag, the object. 

In a passive sentence of this kind, the object, the flag, instead would have the action, hoisting, done to it by the subject, John. So what would be the passive version of that sentence? Have a go. 

That's right, **the flag was hoisted by John**. 

Again, when a subject is seen doing an action, it generally makes for faster comprehension of the sentence because the picture, as I keep saying, is formed more quickly. 

Similarly, **the cat chased the mouse** is quicker to comprehend and more evocative than **the mouse was chased by the cat**. 

I hope you're beginning to notice a **subtle difference in the power and strength of a sentence when it's active versus when it's passive**. An active sentence also usually has fewer words in it which makes it more punchy, too. 

When a sentence is passive, you've got to do more of that spinning of the idea in your mind to turn it the right way up before you fully get it. And as elite writers, we're gonna try and avoid that as much as possible.

### Passive sentences: Exercises

Here are some more examples of some passive sentences and this one is slightly trickier than the earlier ones that we saw. 

**Mr. Watkins' advice should be heeded if you want to enhance your career.** 

Have a go at turning that into an active sentence. That might have been trickier because the subject is kind of hidden a bit in the weeds. Nevertheless, it is there. 

If we spelled the subject out more clearly in this passive sentence, we would write

**Mr. Watkins' advice should be heeded if you want to enhance your career.** 

The subject, in other words, is you, the reader. So another way of writing that passive sentence would be to say

**Mr. Watkins' advice should be heeded by you if you want to enhance your career.** 

The order of the elements there is this, Mr. Watkins' advice, the object, should be headed, action, by you, subject. To turn that into an active sentence, you would basically reverse the order of elements, to say you, the subject, should heed, the action, Mr. Watkins' advice, the object. So you could say, 

**You should heed Mr. Watkins' advice if you want to enhance your career.** 

But since the you is already there in the sentence, you could also more simply say, 

**Heed Mr. Watkins' advice if you want to enhance your career.** 

Another one, **healthcare stocks are viewed by many investors as defensive investments.** 

What's the active version of that? 

**Many investors view healthcare stocks as defensive.** 

Again, notice the difference in the speed at which comprehension takes places in case, the speed of which a picture forms. And notice that little extra tug that you get, that little extra tightness in the sentence when you express it in this active form. 

A few more examples and let me just say that the easiest way to do this is to just look at it, in terms of who or what is doing the acting and make that the focus of the sentence. 

**A picture was painted by Carly.** 

Yes, **Carly painted a picture.** 

And this: **The key should be used to open the gate.** 

Yes, **you should use the key to open the gate.** 

Or **one should use the key to open the gate****.** 

And this one: **My car was stolen by someone.** 

**Yeah, someone stole my car.** 

And this one: **The porridge had been left uneaten on the table by Richie****.** 

Yes, **Richie left the porridge uneaten on the table.** 

And just to be sure you've got it, now change this active sentence into the passive voice. 

**The cover band performed U2 songs all night.** 

Yes, **U2 songs were performed all night by the cover band.** 

Now I want you to imagine you had passive sentences sprinkled throughout a piece of writing. It's going to sound slow and pretty, it's gonna be pretty cumbersome to read. As a reader, you'll be forced to turn these ideas the right way throughout in your head, wouldn't you, in order to see these pictures. 

Conversely, imagine active sentences sprinkled throughout a piece of writing. You're gonna take away all of that slowness and that dullness aren't you? It's just gonna feel more interesting to read, because you're having to do less of that mental juggling and pictures will just form instantly in your mind. 

There could be rare exceptions when a passive sentence could help with either the rhythm or the clarity, perhaps, of a sentence, in certain rare instances. 

For instance, **the subject of a sentence might not actually be known, in which case you might wanna keep the subject vague** for precision and use a passive sentence for that reason. 

Or you may find on really rare occasions that starting a sentence with the thing acted upon rather than the subject, somehow flows better from the preceding sentence. 

Or you might feel that a passive sentence helps to break some monotony in the preceding sentence structure. 

It might, very **occasionally,** even **make your writing more evocative**. Now take this passionate sentence once again, from Nabokov. 

**The glow of the afternoon had entered its most oppressive phase, and one of the first bad mosquitos of the season was resonantly slain on Ada's shin by alert Lucette.** 

Now I wouldn't dare tell Nabakov to switch that to the active voice. 

**The glow of the afternoon had entered its most oppressive phase, and alert Lucette had resonantly slain one of the first bad mosquitos of the season on Ada's shin.** 

Just to get rid of that passive sentence. **In most business or blog writing, however, the need for passive sentences, you should remember, will be extremely rare.**

### Voice, tone, and character

You can say the same thing in so many different ways, can't you? Take a bland sentence like this one. 

**I went yesterday to the Ideal Home Exhibition and saw a range of modern furniture that made me feel instantly like redoing my apartment.** 

You'll hopefully agree that there is very little character or tone in that sentence, right? The narration isn't particularly colorful. It's very, shall we say, vanilla. That's because there isn't any sort of recognizable voice that's coming through, is there? As a piece of writing, it's therefore kinda flat. And so I want you to try and rewrite it to give that same thing some character. 

Now compare the original and your version with this one. 

**Sickened by the sorry state of my apartment, I dropped into the Ideal Home Exhibition thirsting for inspiration. Inside a set of dimly lit cubicles, I encountered a jaw-dropping array of stylish, new-age furniture that spun my head and had me rushing home to reinvent my living space.** 

Now what's the basic difference? Well, it's got some character to it, hasn't it? Can you feel that **there's a character in there, a voice that is present in the writing**, even if you don't like the type of writing it is? It may not be a voice that you like, but at least you can feel that there's someone there, okay? In the first version, no such character was present and as a result, the writing is bland. 

Whenever you write something, a book, a blog post, an opinion piece, or whatever, try to avoid this vanilla approach. **Try to give your writing some life** by giving it some kind of a voice. In other words, write in character. 

Now wait, don't think I'm asking you to write business reports in this sort of frantic, breathless, and emotional way. I'm not saying turn yourself into a character that you're not and, or to write inauthentically. 

I'm saying **write in a voice suitable for your purpose that allows you to say whatever you want to say but with as much vibrancy and resonance as possible**. 

Think about it. We're often drawn to particular blogs, aren't we? Not only because the writing contains some interesting ideas that we want to read. That's part of it, sure. But we're also drawn to them because the writing has a certain character that appeals to us. 

Now, a voice doesn't have to be overly obvious. The Financial Times has a sober, measured, conservative and objective sounding voice which appeals to a certain kind of readership. It's not at all over the top. But the tone is still distinctive. 

The Wall Street Journal, another business newspaper, has some elements of that tone but it also has more of a fun character in some of its writing. Amusing and witty, these distinctions between the voice of newspapers can be subtle but some kind of voice is usually there. If you have a blog or a book to write, think about the kind of voice that it carries. Is it vanilla or does it have a recognizable flavour in terms of the tone and the attitude and the perspective? Just know that if you are used to writing in a very vanilla sort of way, your writing is likely to have a limited appeal, even if your ideas are actually quite ground-breaking. 

**Spend some time exploring your voice. What type of tone are you writing with**? Is it the tone of a cynical person mostly? Or a grave and authoritative person? Or an objective, dispassionate person? Or a passionate and excitable person? Or a sober and rational person? Or an upbeat and fun person? A witty, ironic person? Or an angry, bitter person? Or a hugely compassionate person? Bordering-on-callous person? Maybe your tone is some kind of combination of these things. I don't know what your voice is but find it. 

Or maybe your tone is more subtle than any of those things and that's all fine. Or **maybe you prefer changing your tone all the time for different things** and that's okay too, if that's what you want to do. Either way, whenever you write something in character, something just happens that brings your writing to life. 

Of course, if you're writing in a business context, you can only go so far with this. You've got to find the balance that's appropriate to your own kind of situation. But even there, you'll often be seeking to convey a voice that is at least serious, committed, efficient and thorough. And in that case, that particular voice should shine through in your writing. When you do that, **you're not just asserting a series of facts or dry statistics, you're infusing those facts and statistics with a certain type of energy**. An energy that you can carry from the beginning to the end of a piece of writing, giving it a distinctive tone and a distinctive flavour.

### Voice, tone, and character: Exercises

Okay, to understand the concepts of tone and character a bit better, I want to show you an angry letter written by George Bernard Shaw to the editor of a London newspaper a long time ago in 1892. It shows how something that could have been written in a much blander way takes on huge energy and life because of the character and the voice with which it's written. 

Just look at how evocative this is, also in terms of the colourful detail with which the ideas are stated. 

**Sir, if you do not immediately suppress the person who takes it upon himself to lay down the law almost every day in your columns, on the subject of literary composition, I will give up taking The Chronicle. The man is a pedant, an ignoramus, an idiot, a self-advertising duffer.** 

**A little while ago, when somebody pointed out to him a case of the misuse of and which, the creature, utterly missing the point, rushed about denouncing every sentence containing and which until some public-spirited subscriber of yours stopped him by a curt exposure which would have shamed any corrigible human being into humble silence for a least a month.** 

**Yet he has already broken out in a fresh place. Mr. Andrew Lang, moved by a personal antipathy to split infinitives and to sentences ending with the word such, for example, Shakespeare's line, "No glory lives behind the back of such," once made a jocular attempt to bounce the public out of using them by declaring that they were bad English.** 

**Of course, all competent literary workmen laughed at Mr. Lang's little trick; but your fatuous specialist, driven out of his "and which" stronghold, is now beginning to rebuke second-rate newspapers for using such phrases as "to suddenly go" and "to boldly say."** 

**I ask you, Sir, to put this man out. Give the porter orders to use such violence as may be necessary if he attempts to return, without, however, interfering with his perfect freedom of choice between to suddenly go, to go suddenly, and suddenly to go.** 

**See that he does not come back; that is the main thing. And allow me, as one who has some little right to speak on the subject, to assure your readers that they may, without the slightest misgiving, use adverbed infinitives in any of the three ways given above.** 

**All they need consider is which of the three best conveys by its rhythm the feeling they wish to express.** 

Now, it's impossible to write something as evocative as that, without being thoroughly steeped inside character. For sure, it no doubt expressed exactly how he felt, but he's infused his sentiment with such charisma that the writing just cannot fail to move a reader in some way. 

I don't suggest you infuse your everyday business writing with this kind of tone, unless it's called for. I'm just showing you extremes like this so that you can find a balance, and so that you gain the confidence to express yourself in the way that you feel is most appropriate to the kind of writing that you do. 

Now, I want to look at the tone of this Wall Street Journal article, in a column that's called The A-hed. Notice how the choice of words, the length of sentences, the details depicted are all faithful expressions of the character, the voice that the writer has taken up. 

**In the world of hip-hop, Violet Hills is strictly Old Skool and unabashedly so. Twice a week, the New Zealander joins her friends practicing dance moves like voguing and popping to beats from Missy Elliot and Nicki Minaj.** 

**Her clothes, hot pink and fluorescent green leg warmers, a studded glove and a black hoodie, are modern. But Ms. Hollis has a piece of bling that sets her apart from other hip-hop dancers: a wheelchair. Also, she's 96 years old.** 

Oh, and what about the Communist Manifesto where the purpose was to inspire revolution? The tonal use of questions there, the use of particular words like haunting and spectre, the whole mood about and the pace of the writing, the detail like the German police spies, all flow from the character with which this is written. It's all written in the spirit of a revolutionary who is out to change the world and inspire a whole generation of at the time, other revolutionaries. 

I hope you see **how being in character when you write can really give so much flavour and spice to your writing**. So have fun with this as you just experiment with it. And as an exercise, you might try to write all of those samples with a different tone and a different character and see what you can do with them. 

But for now, here's another stark example of how differently the same bit of information could be written, depending on the kind of voice that you bring to it. Both are articles that I saw covering information that was revealed in the same survey. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

It's okay in terms of the information but the way it's written is a bit bland isn't it, a bit vanilla? It's a faithful delivery of the facts, sure, but it's not very lively or evocative, is it? And okay, it has character, if the character you want to write in is a sober, unexcitable sort of person, which is fine if you do. 

But is this version any better in terms of the character of the writing? 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Now, you could try it in the voice of someone who is cynical about surveys, or somebody who is especially sarcastic, or someone who is hugely compassionate. 

Play around with these as well, you know, to flex your writing muscles. And if you do, notice each time how it leads to different ways of expressing the ideas, the content, the details, the rhythm. 

Finally, I'll leave you with an excerpt from an article by Charlie Brooker, a pretty funny bloke who sometimes writes for the Guardian newspaper. In here, he discusses the traits of a couple of UK party leaders shortly before an election. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Just think, how would you describe the character and the tone in which Brooker writes? It has traces of cynicism, right? I reckon that's one of the biggest things coming through, together with that type of dry English wit. 

So, I hope you're beginning to appreciate what writing in character means and having a distinct voice and what that can do for your writing. As I said, play with it, experiment with it, until you feel comfortable that you can write with charisma whenever you need to.

### Summary

So, Congratulations, guys. I hope you found this journey as exhilarating as I've tried to make it and as exhilarating as I found it. If you've made it this far, you can definitely pat yourself on the back. You've come a huge way towards perfecting the skill of writing and making yourself standout as an exceptional writer. 

I just wanna take a moment to review what you've learned to help you realize just how far you've come. Let me remind you of the journey so that you can see it in perspective a bit better. 

You learned crucially about the mindset of an elite writer. 

First off, that you need approach your writing not as a hat, but as an artist, a painter of words, a sculptor of sentences, a composer of narratives. 

Then I told you about the four vital ingredients that you need in order to write well, and that was the secret sauce, **simplicity, clarity, elegance, and evocativeness**. 

And I explained that you need to apply those ingredients as a master chef would, balancing them artistically for the best results each time. 

Then you learned the importance of being very fun about your intention before even beginning to write so that you can get your writing highly focused from the start, and so it contains everything you need and that your narrative hangs together very firmly. 

Then you learn why you must care deeply for your readers, because doing so establishes an emotional bond with them that captures and holds their interest throughout. 

And you saw why it was important to write all the time with sincerity and integrity, so that your prose never becomes pompous or pretentious, but that you’re laser focused on the goal of communicating well. 

After we learn about mindset, we went on to explore each ingredient of the secret sauce one by one. 

In the **simplicity** section, you saw all the spectacular things you can achieve by bringing conciseness and removing unnecessary complications from your prose. 

You saw how in so many different ways, you can **make your writing punchy and light**, instead of slow and heavy by stripping out fancy language by cutting the fact from your writing, and things like redundant words and implied words, and by avoiding careless repetition. 

You saw how to **manage the amount of information you share** with reader, so that you don't end up rambling and this boring them. 

You saw how to **uncomplicate sentences by making your ideas short enough to comprehend properly, and by removing obstacles, such as double negatives** and multiplying entities beyond necessity. 

And you saw how to **erase unnecessary ceremony from your writing**, like **pointlessly capitalizing** certain words and putting **quote marks** around others for no good reason, that would just distract readers. 

Then we looked at the second crucial ingredient, **clarity**, which brings sharpness and brightness to your ideas. 

You learned how to **approach your sentences with clear thinking** **about what you wanna say** so that you avoid putting down fuzzy or out of focus ideas. 

You saw **how ambiguity can create headache to your readers** and how to spot so many different ways in which ambiguity can arise in your writing. 

Then you learned how **making crisp distinctions and sharp contrast** **between ideas** could make your writing shine even more brightly. 

You saw **how writing in a curly way can destroy the clarity of your writing** and leave readers scratching their heads unnecessarily. 

You learned **how jargon and corporate buzzwords can interfere** with your meaning and create a horrible experience for your readers and how mixing up your tenses can hurt comprehension of your ideas. 

And you learned how to **use important words like it, and they, and this, and that** with much more skill and care, to deliver your meaning more strongly. 

Then you learned about the third crucial ingredient, **elegance**. And you saw that the concepts of order and flow and what they can do for your writing. 

You saw how everything from the presentation of your prose to its structure, to its music and rhythm can create a totally different kind of reading experience when applied with skill. 

You learned about **narrative elegance**, how **ordering the sections in your writing** in an elegant flowing way can bring another gorgeous dimension, a gorgeous layer of beauty to your writing. 

You saw how to **make your paragraph sit elegantly**, how to **make sure ideas are beautifully parallel**, and how to **pluck out ugly word echoes from your writing**. 

And you discovered how to apply the concepts of **timing and rhythm** to give your writing a feeling of musicality and add yet another layer of gracefulness to your narratives. 

And finally, I gave you a tour of **evocativeness**, showing you how **varying your sentence structures and words** can bring a completely different flavour and bite to your writing. 

You saw that how to make your writing more forceful by turning your sentences into pictures to fire up a reader's imagination and stir their emotions. 

You discovered how **reversing into sentences and using passive sentences** can sometimes harm the way a picture forms in a reader's mind, but you also saw how evocativeness principles can be balanced against the beauty of the other ingredients to achieve a perfect synthesis. 

And finally, you saw how **adding voice and character** to your writing can lift it out of the ordinary and give it a power that few writers now how to wield. 

My friend, that's the journey so far, and I congratulate you for persevering with such commitment and with such dedication. Make no mistake, you have an enormous power in your hands now. You're gonna be able to hone and perfect these techniques to start bringing a new mastery to your writing. Keep practicing, adding these four powerful ingredients and watch your writing transform each time better and better into something more magical.
